Based on the optical and NIR data, we revised the distance to the host galaxy, NGC 6951, through the MLCS method, decline-rate estimated intrinsic brightness, and absolute NIR magnitudes 10 and 13.75 days past maximum B light.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 6951 as a Seyfert 2 with nuclear spiral arms, a large molecular reservoir feeding star formation, limited inflow to the AGN, and possible stochastic fueling.

quotes.quote: NGC 6951 is a prototypical Seyfert 2 galaxy for which subarcsec resolution CO maps have been recently completed within the NUGA project (figure fig:feeding ,d adapted from Schinnerer \ 2004, in prep). Molecular gas distribution in the inner 700 pc shows two highly contrasted nuclear spiral arms containing a significant gas reservoir of 4 10 ^ 8 M _ which is presently feeding an ongoing star forming episode, also identified by its prominent radio-continuum emission ( ). The m=2 gas instability is likely reflecting gas crowding along the x _2 orbits of the stellar bar. As can be guessed from figure fig:feeding , only a small amount of molecular gas seems to have succeeded in making its way down to the AGN. A compact molecular complex of a few 10 ^ 6 M _ appears to be linked to the central engine.

sources.summary: The paper studies NGC 6951's circumnuclear starburst ring, deriving cluster ages, ring age, and evidence for preferred recent star-forming sites near gas contact points.

quotes.quote: Here we combine new optical IFU data covering the full stellar bar with existing multi-wavelength data to study in detail the 580 pc radius circumnuclear starburst ring in the nearby spiral galaxy NGC 6951. Using HST archival data together with and IFU data, we derive the ages and stellar masses of star clusters as well as the total stellar content of the central region. Adding information on the molecular gas distribution, stellar and gaseous dynamics and extinction, we find that the circumnuclear ring in NGC 6951 is 1-1.5 Gyr old and has been forming stars for most of that time. We see evidence for preferred sites of star formation within the ring, consistent with the `pearls-on-a-string' scenario, when focusing on the youngest stellar populations.

sources.summary: NGC 6951 is described as an active barred galaxy with custom GALEX processing, a high central molecular peak, and FUV evidence for bar star-formation gaps.

quotes.quote: NGC 6951 has been reported to host an active galactic nucleus (AGN) 1997ApJS..112..391H,1999ApJ...511..157K,2000A&A...353..893P .
For NGC 6951 (not in S ^4 G) we obtained and processed the GALEX NUV GR6/7 image ourselves; to our knowledge no usable FUV image exists for this galaxy.
The visual inspection of the FUV images of NGC 4123, NGC 6951, and NGC 6217 (only in the north-east) also reveal SF gaps along the bar see e.g.

sources.summary: The paper compares CO measurements for NGC 6951, finds its bar CO flux is much higher in another study than in the NRO atlas, and excludes it from further analysis.

quotes.quote: We compare the CO fluxes in NGC 4535 and NGC 6951 with those reported by diaz-garcia_molecular_2021 . The differences in the CO fluxes in NGC 4535 is within a factor of 1.5. However, the CO fluxes in the bar of NGC 6951 reported by diaz-garcia_molecular_2021 are a factor of 6 higher than those in the NRO atlas. Owing to the lack of consensus on the CO(1--0) flux, NGC 6951 is not included in the remaining study.

sources.summary: The paper studies H II-region emission-line profiles in the barred spiral NGC 6951, finding low-intensity high-velocity wing features in more than a third of the regions and interpreting them as shell signatures.

quotes.quote: We study the presence of low intensity high velocity components, which we have termed wing features in the integrated \ emission line profiles of the \ region populations of the spiral barred galaxies NGC 1530, NGC 3359 and NGC 6951. We find that more than a third of the \ region line profiles in each galaxy show these components. The highest fraction is obtained in the galaxy whose line profiles show the best S:N, which suggests that wing features of this type may well exist in most, if not all, \ region line profiles.

gmw_00001026.name: IRAS05083
gmw_00001026.overview: IRAS05083, also known as VII Zw 31, is a gas-rich ultraluminous infrared galaxy located at a distance of 240 Mpc [arXiv:1811.00838]. It is a merger-induced starburst that is identified as a candidate molecular outflow source [arXiv:1811.00838, arXiv:1508.03223].

sources.summary: The paper presents VII Zw 31 as a gas-rich ULIRG and merger-induced starburst, observed across the whole galaxy with new HNCO and C2H detections and enhanced C2H abundance.

quotes.quote: VII Zw 31 is a gas-rich ULIRG 1986MNRAS.219P...1F at the distance of 240 Mpc 2011ApJ...739L..25L . It is a possible protogalactic disk at a low redshift and a merger-induced starburst 1990AJ.....99.1414D . This source had one of the highest known CO luminosities and its molecule gas may be a large part of the dynamical mass 1987ApJ...321L.103S,1991ApJ...368L..15R . Detections of dense gas tracers of HCN 1-0 2004ApJS..152...63G and CS 5-4 Wang11 were also reported in literature.
For VII Zw 31, HNCO 12 _ 1,11 11 _ 0,10 (see Figure fig:VIIZW31 and Table Table:VII Zw 31 ) and C _2 H 3-2 (see Figure fig:NGC6240_C2H,VIIZW31_C2H ) were detected as new results.

sources.summary: The paper identifies IRAS05083 as a candidate molecular outflow, detects faint CO line wings at marginal significance, and notes that no previous faint line-wing measurement was known.

quotes.quote: We identify one of our targets, IRAS05083 (VII Zw 31), as a candidate molecular outflow.

Faint Broad Wings Though we do not find a high-flux extended component, in both I05083 and I17208 we do detect line wings containing at most a few percent of the flux of the main line at marginal significance. These are visible in Figure fig:spectra and in the integrated line wing fluxes presented in Table tab:obs .
To our knowledge, I05083 (VII Zw 31), does not have a previous measurement of faint line wings.

gmw_00001027.name: KUV 0031-1938
gmw_00001027.overview: KUV 0031-1938 is a BL Lacertae blazar [arXiv:1909.03960] that has been detected in radio, X-ray, and high-energy gamma-ray bands [arXiv:1909.03960, arXiv:2004.03306]. On parsec scales, the active nucleus exhibits a core and an extended jet with a measured proper motion [arXiv:2310.10206].

sources.summary: The paper describes KUV 0031-1938 as a BL Lac blazar with uncertain redshift, derives its Fermi-LAT GeV spectrum, and compares absorbed TeV predictions with HESS data.

quotes.quote: KUV 0031-1938 is a BL-Lac blazar with unknown redshift, detected by Fermi-LAT at HE and by H.E.S.S. at VHE in 2012 . It was also detected previously in the X-ray band by ROSAT . Several attempts to determine its redshift were made with no success, or contradictions among them.

All these results place KUV 0031-1938 in the very interesting condition of potentially being one of the most distant blazars detected at VHE, with redshift in a range poorly populated by other VHE blazars, i.e. 0.506 < z < 1.54. In this analysis we use Fermi-LAT observations of the blazar KUV 00311-1938 to derive an intrinsic TeV spectrum. We then simulate the absorption process for different models and redshifts to compare the results with the available HESS data to provide a redshift estimation for the source. The GeV spectrum of KUV 00311-1938 (4FGL J0033.5-1921 in the 4FGL catalog) was obtained using 2 years of Fermi-LAT PASS8 data from December 2009 to December 2011, corresponding with the same period of time in which HESS observed the same source.

sources.summary: The paper reports RBS 76 as a z=0.61 high-energy gamma-ray source detected above 100 GeV and interprets its broadband spectrum with inverse-Compton models.

quotes.quote: RBS 76 at z=0.61 has 2 photons within the distance 0.175^ from the catalog source position. It is detected with significance more than 4 in the 100-300 GeV band. The source is also detected with significance more than 7 in the 30-100 GeV energy band.

Broad band spectrum of the source is shown in Fig. fig:RBS76 . The historical multiwavelength data are taken from the NASA Extragalactic database (NED) http://nedwww.ipac.caltech.edu/ . Comparing the radio, X-ray and \ data one could conclude that the broad-band spectrum of the source fits into the general scheme of synchrotron-self-Compton or synchrotron-external Compton models with radio-to-X-ray emission being produced via synchrotron mechanism and \ flux being generated via inverse Compton scattering.

sources.summary: The paper discusses KUV 00311-1938 as a BL Lac/HSP blazar with ROSAT, radio, VLBI, gamma-ray detections, and redshift constraints.

quotes.quote: KUV 00311-1938 \ has been classified as a BL Lac firstly by a spectroscopic identification in the sample of bright, soft, high-Galactic-latitude X-ray sources from the ROSAT All-Sky Survey 1999yCat.9010....0V, 1998A&A...335..467T . Later, associated this bright ( S_ X = 1.32^ 11 ergs s^ 1 cm^ 2 ) ROSAT source with a strong radio emitter S_ 1.4 GHz = 18.8 mJy 1998AJ....115.1693C . Its extreme value of the X-ray to radio flux ratio and its high X-ray flux, led to its inclusion in the Sedentary Multi-Frequency Survey catalogue 2007A&A...470..787P , which primarily selected HSPs.

A first evaluation of the redshift of \ was performed by , where a value of 0.61 was quoted although flagged as ``tentative''. Later, detected the MgII doubled with the X-Shooter spectrograph operating on the VLT to estimate a secure lower limit on the redshift of the source to 0.506, and used non-detection of the host galaxy to place an upper redshift limit of 1.54. Recently, attempted to constrain the redshift of , by estimating the range of EBL absorption allowed by the preliminary \ observations presented in 2012AIPC.1505..490B and matching this to existing EBL models. They conclude that a redshift around 0.5-0.6 is the most plausible.

sources.summary: The paper treats KUV 00311 1938 as a hard-spectrum BL Lac whose gamma-ray spectrum can be modeled with source or UHECR-induced cascades; its uncertain redshift affects the interpretation.

quotes.quote: Most of these high-energy gamma-ray spectra, like those of other extreme high-frequency peaked BL Lac objects, can be well explained either by gamma rays emitted at the source or by cascades induced by ultra-high-energy cosmic rays, as we show specifically for KUV 00311 1938.

Figure fig:kuv00311sed shows the SED of the BL Lac object KUV 00311 1938, the nearest object in the source list. This is the only source detected by IACTs in the Neronov2012arXiv1207.1962 sample at present.

For instance, a recent detailed spectroscopic study of KUV 00311 1938 identifies no line related to the host galaxy, and provides a conservative lower limit of z 0.506 from a Mg II doublet of narrow lines in intergalactic medium Pita2012arXiv1208.1785 , contrary to the often quoted value of z = 0.61 but large uncertainty is mentioned in Piranomonte2007A&A470p787 .

sources.summary: The paper identifies 0031 196 as an HBL-sample source with parsec-scale extended jet motion, VHE detection by H.E.S.S., and only a 2.3 GHz core in these images.

quotes.quote: 0031 196: 0031 196 is part of the HBL sample presented in piner14 . It shows an extended jet on parsec scales that has a proper motion of 0.204 0.071 mas yr ^ 1 and an opening angle of 18.2 . The source was detected at VHE by H.E.S.S. becherini12 . Our images show only a core with the flux density of 200 mJy, because we lack the sensitivity to detect the extended jet emission.

sources.summary: The paper reports a fast apparent jet component in 0031 196, observed at four epochs, seen as a shoulder off the core, and assigned a Good motion-quality code.

quotes.quote: 0031 196 Component 1 is observed at four epochs, with a proper motion of 0.204 0.071 mas yr ^ 1 . It is seen as a `shoulder' of emission off of the core in some images, so is not always a well-defined feature. It receives a quality code of `Good' using the system above.

gmw_00001028.name: 3C 66b
gmw_00001028.overview: The Fanaroff-Riley-I radio galaxy 3C 66B is an elliptical galaxy [arXiv:1109.6451, arXiv:1806.02346] that forms a dumbbell pair with a nearby companion [arXiv:astro-ph/9909256, arXiv:0901.0913]. It features a central dust disk [arXiv:astro-ph/9909256], a faint carbon monoxide detection [arXiv:1001.5009], and a jet observed across radio, optical, and X-ray wavelengths [arXiv:0809.2829, arXiv:astro-ph/0106029].

sources.summary: The paper describes UGC 1841 as a dumbbell-pair radio galaxy with a central dust disk, nuclear emission, optical jet features, and distorted double-sided radio jet.

quotes.quote: UGC 1841 (3C 66B) UGC 1841 forms a dumbbell pair with a companion southeast at a distance of 24'' (10 kpc). The pair belongs to a small group of galaxies close to the Abell cluster 347. UGC 1841 has a central dust disk (diameter 330 pc/ 0.8'' ) which appears to be neither a perfect ellipse nor a circle. There is a protuberance 0.25'' ( 100 ) in length with PA 230 . The emission image shows bright nuclear \ emission slightly extended towards PA 228 .

Intriguingly, both the slight extension of the \ emission and dust protuberance of the disk are in the direction of the counter jet (Fraix-Burnet 1997). The optical jet was detected by Butcher (1980) and studied by Keel (1988) and Fraix-Burnet (1989, 1991). The surface brightness of the optical jet is quite low and therefore hardly detectable on our images but clearly shows up once a model galaxy is subtracted.

sources.summary: The paper finds that 3C 66B's nuclear radio through X-ray spectrum is well explained by a pure jet model, with ADAF emission too hard for the observations.

quotes.quote: Jet dominated the nuclear X-ray emission for lower Eddington ratio sources The point-like nucleus is detected in 3C 66B by Chandra yielding L_ X (2-10 \ keV)=1.1 10^ 41 ergs^ 1 ( 1 10^ 6 L_ Edd ) and a photon index of =2.17^ +0.14 _ 0.15 (e.g., Donato et al 2004). Figure 3 shows the fitting result of nucleus of 3C 66B.

We find that all the radio, sub-millimetre, optical and X-ray can be fitted by a pure jet model very well (dashed line). The parameters of the jet are m _ jet =1 10^ 5 , _ e =0.18 , _ B =0.02 , and p=2.35 . For illustration purposes, we also show the X-ray emission using an ADAF model with m _ out =2.6 10^ 3 (dot-dashed line).

The predicted spectrum by an ADAF is too hard to be consistent with the observations (so the accretion rate in the ADAF should be smaller than 2.6 10^ 3 ). The X-ray emission in this source should be produced by the jet.

sources.summary: The paper treats 3C 66B as a nearby FR-I radio galaxy coincident with the 2007 MAGIC excess, but rejects it as the 2009-2010 source.

quotes.quote: This excess (named MAGIC J0223+430) coincides within uncertainties with the position of a nearby, Fanaroff-Riley-I (FRI) type galaxy 3C 66B . Still, judging from the skyplot alone, and taking into account statistical and systematic errors, the probability of the emission to originate from 3C 66A was 14.6 .

According to , the radio galaxy is also a plausible source of VHE gamma-ray radiation. Also, the recent MAGIC detection of IC 310 , a radio galaxy at a very similar redshift ( z=0.0189 ) indicates that 3C 66B is in principle a feasible object to explain all or part of the MAGIC detection from 2007.

The fitted center of gravity of the excess (small black square in Figure fig1 ) is at a distance of 0.010 0.023 stat 0.025 sys from 3C 66A, and 0.108 0.023 stat 0.025 sys from 3C 66B. While being compatible with the blazar 3C 66A, the statistical rejection power for the emission to emerge from the radio galaxy 3C 66B corresponds to 4.6 standard deviations. Even considering the unlikely case of a systematic offset exactly towards the blazar, the rejection significance of 3C 66B is 3.6 .

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 66B as an elliptical SMBBH candidate whose radio-core orbit interpretation implies a very high merger rate and gravitational-wave background tension with PTA limits.

quotes.quote: First, it is apparent that the derived merger rate for 3C 66B is four orders of magnitude above that of OJ 287 or NGC 5548, with both having similar chirp masses. The reason that 3C 66B produces such a high merger rate is due to its very short merger time. If it is a true binary, it is expected to merge in only 500 years, whereas others will typically merge in 1 Myr.

3C 66B The elliptical galaxy 3C 66B is located at a redshift of 0.0213. 3C66B03 observed variations in the radio core position with a period of 1.05 years and interpreted this as due to the orbital motion of an SMBBH. The proposed binary system, with inferred total mass of 5.4 10^ 10 M_ and mass ratio of 0.1, was subsequently ruled out with 95 confidence by Jenet04 using timing observations of PSR B1855+09 presented in Kaspi94 .

One can see that the median masses can already be ruled out by current PTA upper limits with more than 99 confidence, whereas the entire error box is in tension with PTA observations with 95 probability.

sources.summary: The paper evaluates 3C66B as a binary SMBH candidate whose orbital period should have evolved measurably, with greatly improved IPTA2025 detection prospects and useful nearby pulsars.

quotes.quote: However, we find that for 3C66B f _ gw = 0.14 nHz/yr. Since Sudou2003 's claim that 3C66B is a binary with an orbital period of 1.05 0.03 years, the source should have evolved from 60.4 nHz to 62.8 nHz over the last 17 years if it were indeed a SMBHB system. Today, it would have a period of P=2/62.8 nHz = 1.01 yrs -- an evolution of 15 days since the initial measurement. The error on the original period is about 11 days, so new EM measurements may prove to be illuminating.

Importantly galaxy 3C66B's detection prospects leap by almost an order of magnitude from the 11-year data constraints Arzoumanian2020 to IPTA2025, Table:table_h0 . Much of this increase in detectability is due to the IPTA's enhanced sky coverage, even over IPTA DR2. Similar to the estimates for PSR J1640+2224, PSR J0218+4232's proximity to 3C66B makes it an ideal target for longer observing periods, and a high-cadence timing campaign.

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C66B as an FRI radio galaxy in an elliptical host with multiwavelength jet counterparts and tentative parsec-scale polarization detected only at 8 GHz.

quotes.quote: 3C66B resides in an elliptical galaxy at a distance of 91 Mpc that forms a part of a dumbbell pair close to the Abell cluster 347. It is one of the few FRI radio galaxies in which the dominant radio jet has an infrared Tansley00 , optical Fraix-Burnet97 , and an X-ray counterpart Hardcastle01 . Kiloparsec-scale observations of 3C66B suggest the jet inclination to be less than 53 Hardcastle96 , while an inclination for the VLBI jet of 45 has been inferred by Giovannini01 . X-ray observations have revealed the presence of copious amounts of hot gas, with which the kiloparsec radio jet seems to be interacting Croston03 .

3C66B: Our observations reveal tentative polarization close to the radio core only at 8 GHz, consistent with the epoch-2002 observations of Kharb05 . We failed to detect any polarization at 5 and 15 GHz.

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 66B as a bright radio galaxy with a claimed supermassive black-hole binary inferred from VLBI radio-core motion, later ruled out by pulsar-timing data.

quotes.quote: In 2003 a team of radio astronomers reported evidence for the discovery of a supermassive black hole binary in the bright radio galaxy 3C 66B , which is located about 90 Mpc from Earth. Their claim was based on very long baseline interferometry (VLBI) observations of the galaxy, in which the radio core of the galaxy was seen to move slightly over the course of 15 months in a manner consistent with an elliptical orbit with a period of 1.05 year. This suggested the presence of a binary system with a total mass near 5 10^ 10 M_ . Remarkably, such a system would also be expected to merge in 5 years due to energy loss by GW emission.

Jenet, Lommen, Larson Wen determined that pulsar timing could be used to check this claim, since the gravitational waves from the binary would cause pulse time-of-arrival variations of up to several microseconds . They analyzed seven years of archival Arecibo timing data for PSR 1855+09 and found no such variation, definitively ruling out the proposed binary system in 3C 66B.

sources.summary: The paper presents ISO infrared detections of the 3C 66B jet, describes its optical and radio jet structure, and models the jet and counter-jet as synchrotron-emitting components.

quotes.quote: The radio galaxy 3C 66B has one of the best-studied optical jets. The source has a redshift of 0.0212 (Stull et al. 1975) and is associated with a 13th magnitude elliptical galaxy in a small group in the vicinity of the cluster Abell 347. Its radio structure (Leahy, J agers and Pooley 1986; Hardcastle et al. 1996) has been studied in detail, and reveals a morphology which is intermediate between an edge-darkened double and a head-tail structure, possibly indicating the effect of the parent galaxy's motion through the ambient medium. The optical jet in 3C 66B was first detected by Butcher, van Breugel and Miley (1980).

Fits to synchrotron models using radio and optical data points have shown that the overall spectral-energy distribution of 3C 66B resembles a simple synchrotron spectrum from the radio to the optical, though the optical slope is unexpectedly flat, perhaps because of inhomogeneities in the jet. The counter-jet in 3C 66B is not detected in our data.

sources.summary: The paper presents 3C 66B as an FR-I radio galaxy whose lobe morphology traces X-ray gas distribution and whose eastern jet heats dense gas and its group environment.

quotes.quote: For both sources, the round, sharply bounded lobe (3C 66B east and 3C 449 south) is embedded in bright X-ray emitting material, whereas the narrower plumes (3C 66B west and 3C 449 north) are relatively free of surrounding material. This suggests that the rounded lobes have formed where the jets flowed into dense material, and expansion along the jet axis was slowed; conversely, such lobe structures failed to form on the opposite side of the sources, because the jets encountered less dense material and continued to flow more freely. More evidence that the environment has played an important role in determining the source structure comes from the bright blob of emission at the end of the eastern jet of 3C 66B.

The bright blob of gas that is interacting with 3C 66B's eastern jet is hotter than the surrounding material ( kT_ blob = 2.4 0.4 keV; kT_ env = 1.73 0.03 keV). We interpret this temperature difference as evidence for local heating due to the work being done by the jet on the gas. In addition to this local heating, there is also evidence for more general heating by 3C 66B.

sources.summary: The paper reports VLBI polarimetry of 3C 66B, finding pc-scale core polarization, transverse inner-jet magnetic fields typical of BL Lac jets, and an ordered field component about 0.3 pc from the core.

quotes.quote: We observed the four FRI radio galaxies viz., 3C 66B, 3C 78, 3C 264 and 3C 270, at 8.4 GHz ( ) with the Very Large Baseline Array (VLBA) 4 The NRAO is operated for the National Science Foundation (NSF) by Associated Universities, Inc. (AUI), under a cooperative agreement. The European VLBI Network (EVN) is a joint facility of European, Chinese, South African and other radio astronomy institutes funded by their national research councils. and five stations of the European VLBI Network ^4 viz., Effelsberg, Onsala, Yebes, Medicina, and the phased WSRT, on 2002 March 1. The observations were made in a ``snap-shot'' mode with the observing time per target totalling five to eight hours.

In this case, the inferred jet B fields within one parsec of the cores of 3C 66B and 3C 78 are roughly transverse to the local jet direction, while the relative B field orientation in the inner jet of 3C 264 is unclear; it may be oblique to the flow direction, but no definite conclusion can be drawn because we cannot account for possible bends in the jet on scales smaller than our beam or for local variations in Faraday rotation.

The jet B field geometries in 3C 66B and 3C 78 are both typical of those that are observed in BL Lac objects, providing support for the standard unification picture. If the transverse field in 3C 66B is associated with an ordered toroidal B field component and not shock compression, this component begins to be the dominant ordered component on scales as small as 0.3 pc from the core, assuming a black-hole mass of 1.9 10^9 M _ (Noel-Storr et al. 2005, preprint) and an inclination 40 Giovannini01 . This corresponds to only 1650 Schwarzchild radii from the putative central supermassive black hole.

sources.summary: The paper presents multi-frequency VLBA observations of 3C 66A, identifying its compact core, fitting it with synchrotron self-absorption, and finding a bent jet with component motions.

quotes.quote: We present the results of six-epoch Very Long Baseline Array (VLBA) observations of 3C 66A. The high-resolution Very Long Baseline Interferometer (VLBI) maps obtained at multi-frequency (2.3, 8.4, and 22.2 GHz) simultaneously enabled us to identify the brightest compact component with the core. We find that the spectrum of the core can be reasonably fitted by the synchrotron self-absorption model. Our VLBA maps show that the jet of 3C 66A has two bendings at about 1.2 and 4 mas from the core. We also give possible identifications of our jet components with the components in previous VLBA observations by analysing their proper motions.

sources.summary: The paper identifies 3C66B as a nearby radio galaxy close to the 3C66A sightline, possibly focusing cosmic rays so secondary photons appear to originate from 3C66B.

quotes.quote: The pair 3C66A and 3C66B provides an interesting case. 3C66A is believed to be a blazar at distance 1.7 Gpc. The radio galaxy 3C66B appears to be only 6^ away from the line of sight, at distance of 88 Mpc. VERITAS  2009ApJ...693L.104A has reported observations of VHE photons from 3C66A but not 3C66B, while MAGIC has apparently observed photons from 3C66B  2009ApJ...692L..29A .

It is possible that the magnetic fields of the radio galaxy close to the line of sight could bend (and focus) the trajectories of the high-energy protons produced by blazar 3C66A. Therefore, it is possible that MAGIC has observed a flare that originated in 3C66A as a burst of cosmic rays, which were focused by the radio galaxy, and which produced secondary photons pointing back to 3C66B, rather than 3C66A.

sources.summary: 3C 66B shows strong ISM emission and an X-ray jet; its weak IGM and asymmetric radio lobes are attributed to interaction with hot gas.

quotes.quote: 3C 66B ( z=0.022 ) : We detect strong ISM emission and the X-ray jet in this Chandra exposure. The IGM is detected and studied (with regards to its interaction with the radio lobes) using XMM-Newton data by croston03 . In the Chandra exposure, however, the IGM is very weak and we cannot measure the morphology. The different character of the two lobes is attributed to interaction with the hot gas by croston03 , with the western lobe being more confined. There is a small unresolved source to the southeast in the X-ray image which has an optical counterpart, but it is far enough from the ISM emission that our ellipse fitting is unaffected.

sources.summary: The appendix describes 3C 66b as a jet-hosting radio galaxy with an optical disk matching the CO spectra, a faint CO detection, and a roughly 10 arcsec central disk.

quotes.quote: The well known bright radio jet of this galaxy was first discovered by and the optical jet was first detected by . were able to perform a detailed study of the jet using the HST/WFPC2 images comparing them with the high resolution radio images and finding excellent correspondence. The optical image reveals the presence of a disk, which is in agreement with the CO spectra of the galaxy. The detection is a very faint one. The central disk diameter is about 10'' in the optical image, and the CO velocity width is 250 km/s.

sources.summary: The paper treats 3C66B as a targeted candidate supermassive black-hole binary system, using prior electromagnetic constraints to search for gravitational waves and set a stringent chirp-mass limit.

quotes.quote: Using long-baseline interferometry, the authors found apparent elliptical motions in 3C66B's radio core, modeling this motion as the gyration of the jet nozzle due to an orbit-induced precession of the smaller black hole's jet. Sudou2003 proposed a period and chirp mass for the binary of 1.05 0.03 years and 1.3 10^ 10 M_ , respectively. Given the relatively small redshift of the galaxy ( z=0.02126 ), a binary with those properties would be emitting gravitational radiation well within the sensitivity of pulsar timing arrays (PTAs).

The work reported here presents a Bayesian cross-validation framework in which we use 3C66B's binary parameter measurements as priors for our continuous wave search. Our search has resulted in the most stringent direct GW derived limit to date on the chirp mass of 3C66B's candidate SMBHB.

sources.summary: The paper finds 3C 66B requires hot dust and NLR components, leaving the torus interpretation inconclusive but strongly indicating some MIR thermal component.

quotes.quote: 3C 66B The best fit for this source requires a prominent hot dust component and a significant NLR model component. The best fit to the diffuse ISM component is unusually dim but the uncertainties are wide. The upper limit provided by the fit with a forced torus component is a significant fraction of the total flux but the forced torus is unusually cold, suppresses the diffuse ISM component, and those two fits have a large BIC difference (best: 472.3, forced torus: 506.8) of 34.5. We note that the BIC difference may be primarily due to the removal of the hot dust component since we expect the most degeneracy between the NLR and torus components as they are both MIR thermal components. Overall, we cannot rule out the possibility of a torus, especially given the presence of another MIR thermal component, but we also do not favor the presence of a torus so we consider our results inconclusive with regard to the presence of a clumpy torus but our results quite strongly indicate the presence of some MIR thermal component.

sources.summary: The paper reports Chandra detection of 3C 66B's X-ray jet, finding a steep synchrotron-like spectrum and an unusually X-ray-bright inner knot that may require distinct acceleration or inverse-Compton emission.

quotes.quote: Our Chandra observation of the FRI radio galaxy 3C 66B has resulted in the first detection of an X-ray counterpart to the previously known radio, infra-red and optical jet. The X-ray jet is detected up to 7 arcsec from the core and has a steep X-ray spectrum, 1.3 0.1 . The overall X-ray flux density and spectrum of the jet are consistent with a synchrotron origin for the X-ray emission. However, the inner knot in the jet has a higher ratio of X-ray to radio emission than the others. This suggests that either two distinct emission processes are present or that differences in the acceleration mechanism are required; there may be a contribution to the inner knot's emission from an inverse-Compton process or it may be the site of an early strong shock in the jet.

sources.summary: The paper uses 3C66B as a radio-galaxy binary-black-hole test case, simulating pulsar-timing residuals and noting that the predicted signal was not observed.

quotes.quote: In Figure fg:3c66b we use the GWevolve plug-in to reproduce the expected PSR B1855 + 09 timing residuals for the postulated binary supermassive black-hole system in the radio galaxy 3C66B (Sudou et al. 2003) simt03 . As described in Jenet et al. (2004), the induced signal has a low-frequency component due to the GW signal at the pulsar and a higher frequency component due to the GW signal at Earth. Figure fg:3c66b a shows the pre-fit timing residuals induced by the simulated GWs. Figure fg:3c66b b gives a realistic representation of observed post-fit timing residuals if 3C66B did contain a binary black-hole system with the chirp mass and period given in Sudou et al. (2003). As concluded by Jenet et al. (2004), such a signal would be easily detectable, but has not been observed in actual pulsar data-sets (Figure fg:3c66b ).

gmw_00001029.name: 3C 442
gmw_00001029.overview: The radio galaxy 3C 442 is a smooth elliptical galaxy [arXiv:1001.5009, arXiv:0712.0595] that is undergoing an extreme merger [arXiv:0812.3401]. This interaction has smothered its old jet activity while restarting nuclear activity [arXiv:0812.3401], leaving diffuse ionized gas that extends out to 14 kpc [arXiv:1903.10768].

sources.summary: 3C 442A is presented as an extreme merger case where gas may have smothered old jet activity, restarted nuclear activity, compressed radio plasma, and helped date radio outbursts.

quotes.quote: In the more extreme case of 3C 442A (Figure fig:3c442a ) there is evidence that a merger may have smothered a previously active jet, leaving a large volume of decaying radio plasma, while at the same time re-starting jet activity in the nucleus of one of the galaxies . Here the merger gas has sufficiently high pressure for the radio lobes to be riding on the pressure front of the merger gas that is sweeping them apart. The energy in the merger gas will eventually be dissipated in the outer regions of the group atmosphere --- an additional source of heating to that arising from both the old and new merger-induced radio activity. The radio spectrum from the old decaying radio lobes is flatter where they are being compressed by the expanding merger gas, suggesting that energy from the gas has a second effect, in re-exciting relativistic electrons through compression and adiabatic heating .

While it is undoubtedly true that mergers produce messy substructures, the example of 3C 442A suggests that there is some prospect that the switching on and off of radio activity by mergers can be timed (albeit roughly) using the morphology of the stellar component of the galaxies and spectral changes in the radio plasma, and that this can be combined with the measured energy content of the gas and radio plasma to trace the history of radio outbursts and their effectiveness in heating gas.

sources.summary: MUSE shows 3C 442 has two compact knots with similar spectra, southwest ionized-gas tongues, and diffuse gas extending to 14 kpc.

quotes.quote: 3C 442: FR II/LEG, 1 = 0.53 kpc. Two compact knots, separated by 0.7 kpc dominate the emission line structure. They show a velocity offset of 130 \ but their spectra are very similar from the point of view of the line ratios and this does not suggest, unlike the case of 3C 459 discussed below, the presence of a dual AGN. At large radii two tongues of ionized gas extends in the SW direction for 4 kpc, a feature reminiscent of the edges of the cavity seen in the 3C 317 balmaverde18 , but the resolution of the available radio images does not allow us to perform a detail comparison between these structures. Along the same SW direction diffuse gas is detected out to a radius of 14 kpc, showing an almost constant velocity of 150 .

sources.summary: The appendix reports 3C 442 as a CO(2-1)-only detection with smooth outer elliptical isophotes but irregular nonuniform light in its central 520 pc.

quotes.quote: 3C 442: UGC 11958, Arp 169. Detected in the CO(2-1) emission line, but not in the CO(1-0). The HST optical image shows the elliptical shape of the galaxy but no evidence of dust obscuration. The isophotes of the outer halo of this elliptical galaxy are relatively smooth but within the central 520 pc, they are irregular and the light distribution becomes non uniform.

sources.summary: The paper says 3C 442 has limited usable exposures, a smooth elliptical host with two WFPC2 companions, and a WSW point source aligned with the radio axis as a hotspot candidate.

quotes.quote: 3C 442; z=0.026 Only two exposures can be used of the four, due to telescope pointing errors. The host galaxy is a smooth elliptical, with 2 companions visible on the WFPC2 image martel+99 . The point source on the edge of the NICMOS chip to the WSW is in line with the radio axis, and is a candidate hotspot - also clearly visible on the WFPC2 images.

gmw_00001030.name: NGC 541
gmw_00001030.overview: NGC 541 is a cD/S0 radio galaxy located in the Abell 194 cluster [arXiv:1001.5009, arXiv:2004.13025]. The galaxy features a central dust disk with a nearly face-on orientation [arXiv:astro-ph/9909256], and its radio jet is believed to have triggered star formation in the nearby Minkowski's Object [arXiv:0906.0287, arXiv:astro-ph/0604557].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 541 as an Abell 194 galaxy with a nearly face-on central disk, darker rings, a central emission peak, and an emission ring.

quotes.quote: NGC 541 NGC 541 is located in Abell cluster 194 (Abell 1989). It is located at 4.5' (96 kpc) from the dumbbell pair NGC 545/NGC 547 identified with the radio source 3C 40 . NGC 541 has a central disk (diameter 640 pc/ 1.8'' ) which appears to be nearly face-on. The disk has an outer rim which is darker, especially on the north side. There is also an inner ring with radius 0.22'' (78 pc) which shows enhanced obscuration.

At the center of the dust disk there is a central \ emission peak. A ring of emission surrounds the peak just inside the darker rim of the disk. There is a slightly brighter emission spot at PA 51 and on the opposite side in the ring.

sources.summary: NGC 541 is treated as a radio galaxy observed in CO, with molecular gas modeled as a compact rotating ring and linked to jet-induced star formation in MO.

quotes.quote: NGC 541 was detected in CO(2-1) and partially detected in CO(1-0). The CO(2-1) luminosity of NGC 541 is: L'_ CO(2-1) =(3.7 0.4) 10^7 K.km.s^ 1 .pc^2 . For the CO(1-0) emission, only the blueshifted part of the spectrum is used to get a lower limit of the luminosity: L'_ CO(1-0) (1.4 0.3) 10^7 K.km.s^ 1 .pc^2 .

For NGC 541, the half-light radius is 5-8 kpc Loubser_2012 , with a stellar mass of 4.7 10^ 11 M_ Bell_2001 . The observational spectrum presents a peak velocity of 100 km.s^ 1 and a relative well depth of 1/4-1/3 . The range of parameters that fit the observations are d_1=0.4-1.1 kpc and d_2=0.2-0.4 kpc for an inclination angle between 30 and 40 (see right panel of figure model_results ), which is consistent with the optical image. The gas is distributed in a narrow ring that extends at distances up to 2 kpc with an average radius 0.5 kpc , but this also needs to be confirmed by interferometric data.

sources.summary: The appendix describes NGC 541 as a CO(2-1)-detected cD/S0 galaxy affected by possible infall or tidal influence, with radio core structure and irregular central isophotes.

quotes.quote: NGC 541: Arp 133 (in Abell 194). This galaxy has been detected in CO(2-1) but not in CO(1-0). From the HST image there is another galaxy that might be falling into NGC541 and there is another larger galaxy probably having some tidal effects on NGC 541. According to , this cD S0 galaxy has a radio core on VLBA scales and a core-jet morphology on VLA scales. The central isophotes, measured from the WFPC/2 images, vary considerably.

sources.summary: The paper links NGC 541 to G4Jy 150 and Minkowski's object, noting jet-triggered star formation, Abell 194 membership, and possible extra lobe emission.

quotes.quote: `Minkowski's object', originally identified by Minkowski1958 , is a well-known starburst whose star formation appears to have been triggered by a radio jet emanating from NGC 541 vanBreugel1985,Croft2006 . NGC 541 is listed in the G4Jy Sample as G4Jy 150 (GLEAM J012548 012157), and along with the dumb-bell galaxy NGC 545/NGC 547, resides in cluster Abell 194. NGC 547 is the host of G4Jy 151 (GLEAM J012603 012356 and GLEAM J012604 011802), as confirmed by archival VLA data at 1.5 GHz Jetha2006,Sakelliou2008 . Together, G4Jy 150 and G4Jy 151 make up the (confused) radio source known as 3C 40 (Figure varietyoverlays ). Being at z=0.018 , the 26 ' angular extent of G4Jy 151 corresponds to a projected, linear size of 540 kpc. Interestingly, the GLEAM contours (for GLEAM J012627 012017) suggest the presence of an `extra' lobe in the system, towards the east and possibly associated with NGC 541 or NGC 545. We note that this feature has been observed previously by Sakelliou2008 and Govoni2017 , but no conclusive interpretation has been made.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC0541 as a young BCG without a cooling-flow host, suggesting recent star formation from merger gas or a radio jet linked to Minkowski's Object.

quotes.quote: For the six young galaxies (younger than 6 Gyr) for which cooling flow information is available, only one is hosted by a cluster without a cooling flow. This suggest that the recent star formation in this galaxy (NGC0541) might have been fuelled by gas deposited in a merger event, or perhaps triggered by the radio jet originating from the galaxy's supermassive black hole. NGC0541 is known to be very peculiar and is associated with Minkowski's Object, an irregular dwarf located 16 kpc from NGC0541 and in the path of the galaxy's radio jet. The radio jet is thought to have triggered the starburst in Minkowski's Object (Schaerer, Contini Pindao 1999; Verdoes Kleijn et al. \ 1999; Croft et al. \ 2006).

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 541 as the nearby active radio galaxy whose jet triggered star formation in Minkowski's Object and likely interacted with a multiphase surrounding medium.

quotes.quote: We present neutral hydrogen, ultraviolet, optical and near-infrared imaging, and optical spectroscopy, of Minkowski's Object (MO), a star forming peculiar galaxy near NGC 541. The observations strengthen evidence that star formation in MO was triggered by the radio jet from NGC 541.

In a previous paper, twenty years ago, wvb:85 hypothesised that the starburst seen in the peculiar ``Minkowski's Object'' MO; mink in the cluster Abell 194 was triggered by the radio jet emerging from the nucleus of the nearby active galaxy NGC 541.

They assumed that NGC 541 is surrounded by a multi-phase medium resembling galaxy cluster atmospheres , ferland:02 ; the simulations began with a hot ``mother'' cloud with a semimajor axis of 10 kpc, a semiminor axis of 5 kpc, density 0.1 cm ^ 3 , and temperature 10^6 K.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC541/3C 40A as a radio galaxy in Abell 194 with Chandra surface-brightness edges and an extended tail interpreted as merger cold-front structure.

quotes.quote: Two galaxies in Abell 194, namely NGC545 and NGC541, exhibit sharp surface brightness edges and extended tails in the soft band Chandra images (Fig. fig:edge upper panels). The edges are located on the north-eastern and western side of NGC545 and NGC541, respectively. To demonstrate the existence of the edges and tails, we extracted surface brightness profiles of the galaxies using the regions displayed in the upper panels of Fig. fig:edge .

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 541 as the nearby elliptical/radio galaxy whose FR I jet impacts Minkowski's Object and whose radio luminosity constrains the jet power.

quotes.quote: In this paper, we focus on the case of M.O., a peculiar star forming object located at a redshift of z = 0.0189 (C06) that is currently being bombarded by a FR I radio jet from the nearby galaxy NGC 541.
In the case of M.O., it is thought that the collision was between a jet and a stellar bridge connecting the elliptical galaxy, NGC 541, with the interacting galaxies, NGC 545/547 (C06).

from the claimed correlation with radio luminosity, P_ jet 7.2 10^ 36 (L_ rad /10^ 30 erg s ^ 1 )^ 12/17 erg s ^ 1 Kording08 , using the measured radio luminosity of NGC 541, L_ rad 10^ 41 erg s ^ 1 vanBreugel85 .

sources.summary: The paper finds NGC 541 does not require a torus component and can be explained by host-galaxy emission, ruling out significant MIR-bright obscuration.

quotes.quote: NGC 541 Not only does the best fit to the continuum emission in this source not require a torus component but the best fit contribution from a forced torus component is not significant, contributing only 20 at its brightest. The largest difference between the best fit model and the data is the 70 m but that point is an upper limit and the best fit model is within the uncertainty of the data point. We therefore conclude that we can explain all the emission with only contributions from the host galaxy and none from the nucleus and so rule out any significant MIR-bright obscuration.

gmw_00001031.name: B2 0116+31
gmw_00001031.overview: The active galaxy B2 0116+31 is associated with a galaxy pair [arXiv:astro-ph/0309620] and hosts a young, variable, double-lobed compact symmetric radio source [arXiv:astro-ph/0212232, arXiv:astro-ph/0309620]. Its expanding radio lobes interact with a circumnuclear molecular disk [arXiv:1807.01475, arXiv:2405.17389], driving a cold-gas outflow [arXiv:2405.17389] and exhibiting strong CO absorption [arXiv:1001.5009].

sources.summary: The paper measures expansion in the compact symmetric radio galaxy 4C 31.04, identifies its core, lobes, circumnuclear disk, and optical-axis features, and estimates a very young kinematic age.

quotes.quote: We report on the results of a two epoch study of the low power Compact Symmetric Object 4C 31.04. Observations performed with the VLBA at 5 GHz in 1995 and 2000 have yielded images of this source at milliarcsecond angular resolution. A central core is detected, with bright compact hot spots and extended lobes on both sides.

Model-fitting and other analysis of the data (brightness profile, difference map) clearly indicate that the source is expanding. We estimate the velocity of this expansion to be (0.085 0.016) mas/yr, i.e. (0.33 0.06) h_ 65 ^ 1 \ in both hot spots. Assuming a constant expansion velocity, we estimate the kinematic age of the source at 550 yrs.
The radio galaxy 4C 31.04 (B2 0116+31) was observed with VLBI by Wrobel Simon ( ) at 92 cm using 7 antennas. Their image showed two components separated by about 70 mas.

sources.summary: The paper applies its jet-ISM simulations to 4C31.04, interpreting its lobe motions, source age discrepancy, and bubble energetics as a frustrated-jet phase.

quotes.quote: Mass transport in the simulations is investigated, and we propose a model for the morphology and component proper motions in the well-studied Compact Symmetric Object 4C31.04.

giroletti03a highlighted a paradox with the observations of proper motions in 4C31.04. Four of the brighter components (including the Western hotspot) are observed to have mildly relativistic velocities indicating a kinematic age for the source 550 yr . However the spectral age of the source (assuming equipartition conditions) 3-5,000 yrs .

However, we suggest on the basis of model A that the source has been in the spherical bubble phase for about 3-5,000 yr, that persistent collimated jets are a relatively recent phenomenon ( < 1 kyr )and that the high hot spot velocities represent the phase where jet breakout is about to occur. This differs from the ``wandering jet'' model proposed by giroletti03a . Their model implies that recognisable jets have been active in the source for some time; our model implies that the jets have been frustrated for a large proportion of the source lifetime.

sources.summary: The paper describes MCG 5-4-18 as the host of a young radio source whose lobes disturb a circumnuclear disc, create turbulent gas, and drive an eastern-lobe cold-gas outflow.

quotes.quote: \ is a young radio AGN hosted by the gas-rich galaxy MCG 5-4-18, making it an interesting source for studying the possible interaction between the radio jets and the ISM. It is one of the few sources for which the age has been estimated both from the expansion of the hot spots and a spectral index analysis Giroletti03 . This age estimation is in the range of 500 -- 5000 years, making it a genuinely young radio source.

Combining the above findings, we arrive at the conclusion that the lobes of \ are expanding into a circumnuclear disc, partially disrupting it along the eastern lobe, and overall, making the gas highly turbulent. While the gas is predominantly in a smooth distribution at the resolution of our data ( 4 pc), being detected all across the radio source, we also find the presence of clumps at least along the eastern side. Here, the eastern lobe is strongly interacting with gas clouds and also driving an outflow of an \ cloud. We estimate a mass-outflow rate of 0.3    M    1.4    year 1 .

sources.summary: 4C 31.04 is presented as a variable double-lobed compact symmetric radio source, associated with a galaxy pair, whose nuclear emission resembles a Seyfert 2 galaxy.

quotes.quote: 0116 + 319 or 4C 31.04 is a variable radio-source with a double-lobed radio structure; it shows the presence of a complex \ absorption (Conway 1996), which hides one of the mini-lobes and partially covers the other. The core source was identified with a faint component between the two extended lobes; no jet-like structures were observed either side of the core (Giovannini et al. 2001). From the steep 843 value, Dennett-Thorpe March a (2000) suggest that 4C 31.04 is not highly boosted; also its symmetric structure and core-to-total radio power ratio suggest that this source is near the plane of the sky (Giovannini et al. 2001). 4C 31.04 is classified as a low-redshift CSO (Giovannini et al. 2001; Cotton et al. 1995).

This object is associated with a galaxy pair (MCG 5 4 17 and 5 4 18) at z = 0.060 . Spectroscopic observations by Gelderman Whittle (1994) reveal the presence of , 6548, 6584 and 6300 in emission, although the quality of their spectra does not allow to classify the object easily. Our data seem to indicate the presence of a nuclear emission region with properties similar to those of Seyfert 2 galaxies.

sources.summary: The appendix calls B2 0116+31 peculiar because CO(1-0) shows strong absorption with blue and red emission wings, probably tracing rare line-of-sight molecular gas toward the AGN.

quotes.quote: B2 0116+31: This is a peculiar galaxy in this sample, showing a strong absorption line in CO(1-0). The molecular/dusty disk has been studied in details by using the IRAM PdBI. The absorption line is surrounded by emission at both blue and red-shifted wings. Because of the absorption, we cannot discard a possible double horn profile. As mentioned in lineratios , this absorption is the signature of molecular gas mass on the line of sight towards the AGN covering a very small area and because of its small filling factor, this absorption is quite rare, contrary to what could be expected for a radio-selected sample.

sources.summary: 4C 31.04 is a compact double-lobed radio galaxy where H I absorption indicates an almost edge-on torus or disk, matching an HST dust disk and possible infalling-cloud properties.

quotes.quote: One of the best examples is 4C 31.04. In this compact, double-lobed radio galaxy, a sharp edge in optical depth has been observed against the western lobe, while for the eastern lobe the opacity is quite uniform Conway96,Struve12 . This has been taken as a signature of an almost edge-on torus/disk which extends to radii < 200 pc. HST observations of 4C 31.04 Perlman01 have confirmed the presence of a dust disk of the dimensions as derived from the \ absorption.

An example is observed in 4C 31.04 Conway96,Struve12 for which results from VLBA observations ( Conway99 , see sec:vlbi ) confirm that its size (5-10 pc) and opacities are similar to those of Galactic High Velocity Clouds which are located in the halo of the Milky Way.

sources.summary: J0119+3210 is treated as a CSO 2.2 whose component motions may trace jets rather than true outer-lobe separation.

quotes.quote: 2003AandA...399..889G have carried out a detailed study of the motion of components in J0119+3210. The speeds they derive for various components are shown in plt:sixmaps (d), where we highlight components E1, E2 and W1 with red arrows. 2003AandA...399..889G derived a significant separation speed between components E2 and W1. However, we see that component E1 is closer to the leading edge of the eastern lobe, and is moving almost due south. It is by no means clear to us, therefore, that the outer edges of J0119+3210 are actually separating, and that the measured relative motions do not apply to components moving along the jets.

sources.summary: The paper presents 4C 31.04 as a nearby compact symmetric object in a giant elliptical, with dusty optical features and a massive rotating molecular gas reservoir.

quotes.quote: 4C 31.04 is a nearby CSO (z 0.06) lying at the nucleus of the giant elliptical galaxy MCG 5-8-18.
( ) reveal that the optical image of 4C 31.04 is permeated with dusty features; these consist of a disk and a spiral structure.
The existence of a massive gas reservoir in 4C 31.04 is confirmed by the detection of significantly strong HCO ^ + (1--0) emission coming from a rotating molecular disk (Fig.

sources.summary: The paper finds moderate PAH emission and water ice in 4C +31.04, placing its PAH equivalent width between starburst and AGN branches.

quotes.quote: 1146+59, 4C +31.04, 1946+70, PKS 1718 649, and 4C +37.11 all show moderate PAH emission strongest at 11.3 and 12.7 .
We detect water ice in 4C +31.04 at an optical depth of =0.57 .
4C +31.04 lies between the two branches, with a PAH EW that does not clearly classify it as either a starburst galaxy or an AGN.

gmw_00001032.name: B2 0924+30
gmw_00001032.overview: B2 0924+30 is a remnant radio galaxy [arXiv:1701.06903, arXiv:2111.04776] that resides as the brightest member of a Zwicky cluster [arXiv:astro-ph/9907009]. The galaxy features a perturbed dust lane [arXiv:1001.5009], a young stellar component [arXiv:1508.03287], and possible interactions with a smaller companion [arXiv:1001.5009].

sources.summary: The paper maps B2 0924+30 as a remnant FRII radio galaxy, finding older central plasma, younger lobe edges, an active phase near 100 Myr, and shutdown about 50 Myr ago.

quotes.quote: In the case of B2 0924+30, for the youngest regions at the edges of the lobes we determined an age of around 50 Myr; the source age increases as we look toward the host galaxy; the lobe inner edges show ages of up to 120 Myr, and the center regions around 150 Myr. These findings are in agreement with the ageing profile reported by . Since we fitted an ageing-only model (JP), we have an estimate for the time elapsed since the plasma was last energized across the source. Consequently, for resolved sources, we can estimate the duration of their active phase as the difference between the oldest and youngest age read-off from the map: t_ on = t_ max t_ min . For B2 0924+30, we find an active phase duration of around 100 Myr. Furthermore, the elapsed time since the shut-down is given by the youngest age found ( t_ off = t_ min ) and, in the case of B2 0924+30, this is found to be around 50 Myr.

B2 0924+30 is an AGN radio remnant, a leftover from the time when it was an active radio galaxy. The remnant radio lobes are very well outlined, which may indicate confinement by the IGM. Our analysis shows that the youngest plasma is located at the outer lobe edges.

sources.summary: The paper presents B2 0924+30 as a well-known non-cluster remnant radio galaxy whose low-frequency spectral gradients suggest recent switch-off and a fading FR-II progenitor.

quotes.quote: The importance of adding the resolved spectral information down to low frequencies has also been demonstrated for the case of B2 0924+30, until recently and before the discovery of blob 1, the most well known remnant radio galaxy not residing in a cluster environment . The distribution of the total intensity and of the low-frequency spectral index from our LOFAR study are shown in Figure fig:0924 . They illustrate how the low-frequency spectral index can reveal more details on the nature and evolution of the source.

The addition of the low frequency information to that of the high frequencies studies Jamrozy04 has allowed us to study the spectral index properties over a large band (up to 5000 MHz). Using this combination, it was found that the lobe spectral index is somewhat flatter at low frequencies and, interestingly, the flatness is more pronounced at the outer edges of the lobes (see Figure fig:0924 ). The flatter spectral index at low frequency and the spectral index gradient indicates a recent switch-off of the source. Furthermore, the flatter regions at the edges, reminiscent of old hot spots, suggests that B2 0924+30 could be a fading Fanaroff--Riley type II (FR-II) source, i.e., the youngest regions are found towards the outer edges of the lobes, and the oldest are the regions towards the host galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper describes B2 0924+30 as a remnant radio galaxy in a Zwicky cluster, with marginal extended X-ray emission and no detectable core radio emission.

quotes.quote: B2 0924+30 appears to be a remnant radio galaxy whose nuclear engine is inactive (Fanti et al., 1987; Cordey 1987; Giovannini et al. 1988). It is the brightest member in a Zwicky cluster (Ekers et al. 1981), and the X-ray data suggest extended X-ray emission, although the detection is of marginal significance. The relatively high X-ray emission for a source with no detectable core radio emission may therefore be due to the extended gas in the cluster.

sources.summary: The appendix describes B2 0924+30 as a CO(1-0)-detected galaxy with a perturbed dust lane and possible interaction, including a smaller galaxy visible in HST imaging.

quotes.quote: B2 0924+30: It shows a peculiar perturbed dust lane in the optical image which could possibly be due to interactions. It has not been detected in CO(2-1), although there is a clear detection of the CO(1-0) line. The HST image shows another smaller galaxy that might have fallen into B2 0924+30.

sources.summary: For B2 0924+30, a young stellar component fits the UV spectrum while WISE luminosities are offset because of point-source modeling.

quotes.quote: B2 0924+30. A young stellar component gives a good fit to the spectrum, including NUV and FUV. The WISE data are different by a large factor, but again, as in B2 0908+37, the slope appears to be consistent. We note that the WISE luminosities were derived using a point-source model.

gmw_00001033.name: HATLAS J114637.9-001132
gmw_00001033.overview: HATLAS J114637.9-001132 is a lensed, high-redshift starburst galaxy [arXiv:1204.3917, arXiv:1802.07430] situated within a surrounding overdensity of galaxies [arXiv:1204.3917]. The system is gravitationally lensed by a complex foreground structure [arXiv:1204.3917, arXiv:1504.05254], which produces multiple background arcs and compact images [arXiv:2311.01158].

sources.summary: The paper describes HATLAS J114637.9-001132 as a lensed high-redshift submillimeter galaxy with filamentary Keck structure, two SMA components, complex lensing, and a significant surrounding overdensity.

quotes.quote: The K_ s band image shows complex filamentary structures that do not appear at J band. Observations with the SubMillimeter Array (SMA) reveal two 880 m sources with flux densities of 31 mJy and 27 mJy, separated by 3^ . In their work, show that the observations can be nicely explained by a lens model in which the lens is a rather complex system located at z 1 .

In order to test if the over-density we observe around HATLAS J114637.9-001132 is statistically significant, we have chosen randomly 1000 Herschel sources and counted the number of galaxies in circles with the same radius _ 857 . The median number of neighbours is 1, with a standard deviation of 1.2. None of the sources in the control sample has a number of neighbours equal to or larger than HATLAS J114637.9-001132 .

sources.summary: The paper detects CO(7--6) and CI(2--1) in the lensed z~3 starburst G12v2.30, finding extreme line ratios consistent with shock-driven molecular gas excitation.

quotes.quote: The spectra extracted from the SEPIA5 receiver are shown in Figure Spectra . The CO(7--6) line is clearly detected in all three sources and the corresponding fluxes and line widths resulting from the Gaussian best fit are listed in Table table:data . The (2--1) line is detected at a 4 level in G12v2.30, marginally ( 2 ) in NBv1.78 and not detected in G12v2.43. The corresponding fluxes or upper limits and line widths are listed in Table table:data , together with the CO(1--0) fluxes taken from har+12 , CO(3--2) in NBv1.78 flux from omo+13 , the IR luminosities and magnification values from zha+18 .

Two of the three targets also show very large linewidths, so at least for G12v2.30 and NBv1.78 it can be interpreted as a possible sign of merger-driven velocity fields with expected large mechanical energy available as a source of energy for their molecular gas.
The high values of CO(7-6)/FIR (and also of CI(2-1)/FIR ) in NBv1.78 and G12v2.30 are consistent with a chemistry driven by shocks per+10 .

sources.summary: The paper describes G12v2.30 as a complex lensed DSFG with four foreground near-infrared lenses and crowded neighboring sources that may skew background photometry.

quotes.quote: HATLASJ114638.0-001132 (G12v2.30): High-resolution data has shown the lensing morphology to be highly complex and lensed by four foreground sources in the near-IR---for a detailed analysis, see Fu2012 . Similar to HFLS08, this field is fairly crowded, with multiple sources within 4 '' to the southeast and southwest. Attempts to fit for the nearby sources created very degenerate results, so only the foreground and background components were used in the de-blending process. It should be noted that light from neighboring sources may skew photometry of the background DSFG.

sources.summary: The paper models this strong-lensing system, describing the foreground lens components and the background source morphology, such as arcs, rings, knots, multiple images, or shear-driven structure.

quotes.quote: HATLASJ114638 001132 _ 1,2 (HerBS2; S 15) : This type 2 foreground lens is formed by two galaxies at z_ s ^ spec =1.22 Bussmann2013 , which we deblend by simultaneously modelling them with GALFIT using two Sérsic components each. The background lensed source at z_ s ^ spec =3.26 Yang2017 shows a very complex structure with multiple arcs and compact images. This system was studied in detail by Fu2012 . It was also observed with ALMA Band 6, Amvrosiadis2018 , SMA 340 GHz, Fu2012 , PdBI 232 GHz, Omont2013 , and the JVLA Ka band Fu2012 in the sub-mm/mm wavelength range and Keck/NIRC2 AO J and K _ s band, Fu2012 in the near-IR.

gmw_00001034.name: NGC5746
gmw_00001034.overview: NGC 5746 is a massive, quiescent edge-on spiral galaxy [arXiv:0801.1069, arXiv:astro-ph/0512485] featuring a boxy or peanut-shaped bulge [arXiv:1505.00590, arXiv:1907.10706] and an unusually large 60.3 kpc envelope [arXiv:1907.10706]. It hosts a vertically extended dust component [arXiv:2207.11589] and extraplanar neutral gas [arXiv:0902.4525], but lacks a massive, lagging neutral halo [arXiv:0801.1069].

sources.summary: VLA 21-cm observations of NGC 5746 find high-latitude neutral gas mostly in a warp, a few distinct cloud features, and no massive lagging neutral halo.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: a kinematic center about 6" or 850 pc west of the pointing center, at R.A. 14 ^ h 44 ^ m 56.0 ^ s , Decl. 1 ^ 57' 16" (J2000)

quotes.quote: We present Very Large Array 21-cm observations of the massive edge-on spiral galaxy NGC 5746. This galaxy has recently been reported to have a luminous X-ray halo, which has been taken as evidence of residual hot gas as predicted in galaxy formation scenarios.

We detect a substantial high-latitude component, but conclude that almost all of its mass of 1.2-1.6 10^9 \ most likely resides in a warp. Four features far from the plane containing about 10^8 \ are found at velocities distinct from this warp. These clouds may be associated with the expected infall, although an origin in a disk-halo flow cannot be ruled out, except for one feature which is counter-rotating. The warp itself may be a result of infall according to recent models. But clearly this galaxy lacks a massive, lagging neutral halo as found in NGC 891.

sources.summary: The paper reconstructs NGC 5746 as a massive edge-on disc galaxy without a classical bulge, whose early disc/bar formation survived a later 1:10 merger.

quotes.quote: We test these ideas with deep MUSE observations of NGC 5746, a massive ( 10^ 11 ) edge-on disc galaxy with no classical bulge. We analyse its stellar kinematics and stellar populations, and infer that a massive and extended disc formed very early: 80 of the galaxy's stellar mass formed more than 10 Gyr ago. Most of the thick disc and the bar formed during that early phase.

Around 8 Gyr ago, a 1:10 merger happened, possibly on a low-inclination orbit. The satellite did not cause significant vertical heating, did not contribute to the growth of a classical bulge, and did not destroy the bar and the nuclear disc. It was however an important event for the galaxy: by depositing its stars throughout the whole galaxy it contributed 30 of accreted stars to the thick disc. NGC 5746 thus did not completely escape mergers, but the only relatively recent significant merger did not damage the galaxy and did not create a classical bulge.

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 5746 as a massive spiral model for testing coronal thermal instability, constrained by rotation speed, virial temperature, X-ray halo limits, and extraplanar neutral gas.

quotes.quote: The first is NGC 5746, a very massive spiral with flat rotational speed 310 corresponding to a virial temperature 3.4 10^6 K; Rand08 , for which we take from Rasmussen09 the upper limit on the X-ray luminosity of the corona < 4 10^ 39 . The second is the Milky Way, whose corona is constrained observationally as in FukugitaP . The third galaxy is NGC 6503, a low-mass spiral seen at an inclination of about 75 degrees, for which Chandra observations yield an upper limit < 1.6 10^ 38 to the X-ray halo luminosity Strickland . For this galaxy we use v_ = 120 Begeman87 , which by equation ( eqTvir ) leads to a virial temperature = 5.1 10^5 . In both NGC 5746 and in NGC 6503 a component of extraplanar neutral gas has been detected Rand08, Greisen09 .

The isothermal model of NGC 5746 has total halo luminosity =3.99 10^ 39 in the range 5 < < 40 , consistent with the upper limit on the X-ray luminosity Rasmussen09 . Our isothermal model of the Milky Way is very similar to ``model 2'' of FukugitaP . We checked that the obtained luminosity is in agreement with observational constraints on galaxies similar to the Milky Way, for instance NGC 891 Strickland , which happens to be the case. For the isothermal model of NGC 6503, we assume =1 10^ 3 , which gives a luminosity that matches the upper limit by Strickland . Similarly, the adiabatic models of the three galaxies are constructed so that their X-ray luminosities are consistent with observations. In contrast to the isothermal case, an adiabatic atmosphere has a sharp edge and a well defined mass. The location of the corona's edge and thus the total gas mass depend strongly on the reference gas temperature . We find that using the same (temperature at =10 ) as the isothermal models leads to gas masses which are one or two orders of magnitude lower than what is expected for a \ corona. Lacking constraints on the temperature profiles, for the Milky Way corona we choose and as the largest values (in order to increase the mass of the corona) that do not conflict with the luminosity constraints: these values, shown in Table GalTab , give a total \ mass of 1.3 10^ 11 , in line with the cosmological predictions. For NGC 5746 and NGC 6503 we keep the same ratio between and as for the adiabatic Milky Way model (i.e. \ same cut-off radius 350 ), maximise according to the X-ray luminosity constraints and find total masses of the same order (see rightmost column of Table GalTab ). The density and temperature profiles of our models are shown, respectively, in the first and second rows of panels of Fig. figisot (isothermal models) and Fig. figadiab (adiabatic models). The values of the parameters for all models are summarised in Table GalTab . figure=mw_adiab.eps, ,angle=0,bbllx=18bp,bblly=260bp,bburx=577bp,bbury=725bp,clip= Isothermal coronae Consider first coronae with isothermal gas distributions ( =1 ), which are known to provide reasonable approximations to the hot atmospheres of elliptical galaxies and galaxy clusters. In Fig. figisot we plot the results of applying the instability analysis of Section sec:per to isothermal models of NGC 5746 (model N5746i; left-hand column), the Milky Way (model MWi; central column) and NGC 6503 (model N6503i; right-hand column).

sources.summary: The review describes NGC 5746 as an edge-on B/P-bulge galaxy with boxy or X-shaped infrared morphology and kinematic evidence for a nearly end-on bar.

quotes.quote: Structural decompositions Two edge-on galaxies with B/P bulges, NGC 4565 and NGC 5746, have been decomposed into multiple structure components by kormendy2010 and barentine2012 . In the classification by buta2015 the Hubble types of these galaxies are SB _x ()ab sp, and (R')SB _x (,nd)0/a sp. In direct infrared images the bulges in both galaxies clearly have boxy or even X-shape morphology. The surface brightness profiles were decomposed into an exponential disk, and two bulges (a 'boxy bulge' and a 'disky pseudobulge') fitted with separate S ersic functions. In both galaxies the boxy bulges were assumed to be bars seen in nearly end-on view.

For NGC 5746 there is also kinematic evidence for this interpretation, manifested as a 'figure-of-eight' line-of-sight velocity distribution, typical for boxy bars, which characteristic will be discussed in more detail in the next section.

sources.summary: The paper presents NGC 5746 as a massive, nearby, quiescent edge-on SBb spiral whose halo shows diffuse soft X-ray emission detected with Chandra out to more than 20 kpc.

quotes.quote: As a test of current disk galaxy formation models we used the Chandra X-ray telescope to study the most promising candidate spiral galaxy for detecting halo X-ray emission, NGC 5746. We also studied a similar, but less massive galaxy, NGC 5170, as a test of our procedure. The galaxies are massive and nearby (NGC 5746 is an SBb galaxy at d =29.4 Mpc and has V_c =307 5 km/s; NGC 5170 is an Sc galaxy at d =24.0 Mpc and has V_c =250 5 km/s).

Both galaxies are quiescent, showing no signs of either starburst activity, interaction with other galaxies, or an active galactic nucleus. The disks of the galaxies are viewed almost perfectly edge-on. Diffuse, soft X-ray emission extending more than 20 kpc from the stellar disc was detected around NGC 5746. A total of about 200 net counts in the 0.3-2 keV band were detected from the halo of NGC 5746, corresponding to a 4.0- detection.

sources.summary: NGC 5746 has a comparable fit quality to NGC 5529 but a poorer V-band extinction-lane fit, inconsistent disk parameters, and a clumpy K-band ring.

quotes.quote: NGC 5746: the fit quality is the same as for NGC 5529, in both bands. The fit to the extinction lane in the V-band, though, is poorer. The parameters for the stellar and dust disks are not consistent with those reported for the B-band by AltonA&AS2000 , with h_ s larger by 40 in the present case. Extinction is located in a clumpy ring structure of radius 1 3, which is evident in the K' image. From the ring in K', an inclination =86^ 1^ can be estimated, which is consistent with the fitted value.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 5746 as a blue-cloud edge-on galaxy with a boxy/peanut bulge, an unusually large 60.3 kpc envelope, and high peak rotation velocity.

quotes.quote: NGC 5746 shows one of the largest envelopes found for a galaxy in the blue cloud. This edge-on, boxy/peanut shaped bulge galaxy has an extraordinary 60.3 kpc diameter envelope, and its rotation curve has the highest peak velocity in the Bureau99 sample; 500  km/sec. NGC 772 is also identified as being in the blue cloud, but it is a face-on spiral with one spiral arm and 3 galaxies entrained in a stream; they all lie projected on a field of complex infrared cirrus. It is in the blue cloud by virtue of its disk, and hosts an extremely large envelope due to the ongoing interaction. NGC 474, NGC 772, and NGC 5746 are anomalous in their hosting of large envelopes yet not residing on the red sequence.

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 5746 as the MUSE-observed edge-on analog setting its mock Milky Way projection, observing strategy, noise model, and disk-scale comparisons.

quotes.quote: The mock MW catalog is assumed to have a distance of 26.5 Mpc and inclination of 86^ to the observer. The selected distance and inclination are the same as the projection of NGC 5746, which was observed by MUSE and has comprehensive analysis in 2021MNRAS.508.2458M (hereafter M21). We use MILES variable SSP templates 2015MNRAS.449.1177V with the BaSTI stellar isochrones 2004ApJ...612..168P and Kroupa Universal IMF 2001MNRAS.322..231K .

In reality, radial scale lengths of the thin disk (R^t) and thick disk (R^T) in NGC 5746 and the MW are very different ( R^t_ MW =2.6 0.5 kpc and R^T_ MW =2.0 0.2 kpc from 2016ARA&A..54..529B ; R^t_ NGC 5746 =6.1 kpc and R^T_ NGC 5746 =8.2 kpc from M21).
Given that NGC 5746 from M21 is four times more massive than the MW, it has a larger scale length and the definition of its thick disk might not apply to the MW.

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 5746 as an observed edge-on Sb spiral comparison, noting its distinct thin and thick disks, 12 Gyr thick disk, metallicity within the simulated range, and relatively high [Mg/Fe].

quotes.quote: In particular, we compare qualitatively our sample to the only two edge-on spiral galaxies with clearly distinct thick and thin disks, with published two-dimensional stellar-population analyses from IFS data. One is NGC 5746, an Sb galaxy of similar mass to the most massive AURIGA galaxies, with a 12 Gyr-old thick disk Martig2021 . Another is the Sc galaxy ESO 533-4, of similar mass to the least massive AURIGA galaxies, and with a 10 Gyr-old thick disk Comeron2015 . Both thick disks are older than all AURIGA thick disks, and the difference is larger than the estimated uncertainties in observations ( 1 Gyr in Martig2021 ).

However, the two thick disks observed in NGC 5746 and ESO 533-4 show [ M/H ] values in the range covered by our simulation sample. While Comeron2015 did not provide [ Mg/Fe ] values for ESO 533-4, NGC 5746 shows higher values than our thick-disk sample.

sources.summary: The paper flags NGC 5746 as a Main-sample galaxy with vertical-profile bumps suggesting an additional extended dust component, arguing the extraplanar FIR excess is real rather than PSF or inclination artefact.

quotes.quote: Four galaxies from the Main sample (NGC 4244, ESO 373-008, NGC 5529, and NGC 5746) show bumps in their cumulative vertical profiles above the model profile which can point to the existence of another vertically extended component.

We note that this extraplanar FIR emission, which typically starts to dominate at heights 1--2 kpc above the mid-plane, cannot be explained by the scattered light of the PSF since we accounted for this effect while modelling. Also, variations in the galaxy inclination angle as described in Sect. sec:method cannot explain the observed excess of dust emission (the red spread along the model profile in Fig. fig:profiles shows a one- spread of the model profiles for varied inclination angles, as described in Sect. sec:3Ddisc ). This indicates that the observed extraplanar emission is real and produced by the dust at high distances above the mid-plane.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC5746 as a large edge-on spiral resolved by SPIRE whose Planck/Herschel flux discrepancy arises from resolution and background-subtraction differences.

quotes.quote: The source 28 (object (h) in Figure fig:poststamps_GAMA-15 ) is identified as NGC5746, a large edge-on spiral that is clearly resolved as a very extended source by SPIRE but is not flagged as extended by the ERCSC. We believe that the discrepancy between the flux densities for this object reported by H-ATLAS and the ERCSC is due to the very different angular resolution of Herschel and Planck and by the different way in which background subtraction has been performed by the catalog making pipelines of the two experiments. In particular, if aperture photometry is applied to the raw (non background subtracted) SPIRE 350 micron map, a flux density of 10 Jy is obtained for this object, which is more consistent with the 857 GHz value.

gmw_00001035.name: GRAL113100-441959
gmw_00001035.overview: GRAL113100-441959 is a quadruply imaged lensed quasar [arXiv:1810.02624, arXiv:2012.10051] located at a redshift of 1.090 [arXiv:1810.02624]. Spectroscopic observations of the system show broad emission lines but reveal no evidence of the central lens galaxy [arXiv:1810.02624, arXiv:2012.10051].

sources.summary: The paper spectroscopically confirms GRAL113100-441959 as a quadruply imaged quasar at redshift 1.090 and models its lensing configuration, lens-galaxy position, and time delays.

quotes.quote: We report the spectroscopic confirmation and modeling of the quadruply imaged quasar , the first gravitational lens (GL) to be discovered mainly from astrometric considerations. Follow-up spectra obtained with Keck/LRIS reveal the lensing nature of this quadruply-imaged quasar with redshift = 1.090 0.002 , but show no evidence of the central lens galaxy. Using the image positions and G band flux ratios provided by Gaia Data Release 2 as constraints, we model the system with a singular power-law elliptical mass distribution (SPEMD) plus external shear, to different levels of complexity. We show that relaxing the isothermal constraint of the SPEMD is not statistically significant, and thus we simplify the SPEMD to a singular isothermal ellipsoid to estimate the Einstein radius of the main lens galaxy = 0. ^ 851 , the intensity and position angle of the external shear ( , _ ) = (0.044,11. ^ 5) , and we predict the lensing galaxy position to be ( , ) = (-0. ^ 424,-0. ^ 744) with respect to image A. We provide time delay predictions for pairs of images, assuming a plausible range of lens redshift values between 0.5 and 0.9 .

The source is clearly identified as a quasar at z_s = 1.090 based on strong detections of broad emission from C III ] 1909 , Mg II 2800 , H and H . In addition, narrow, forbidden transitions of oxygen and highly ionized neon are also evident. These features, seen in both components of the PA =60^ spectra, clearly confirm the lensing nature of .

sources.summary: The paper summarizes GraL J113100.0 442000 as a machine-learning-discovered quadruply imaged quasar at z = 1.090 whose new models agree with previous dedicated work.
