quotes.quote: GraL J113100.0 442000 --- This is the quadruply-imaged quasar presented as a candidate in KroneMartins:18 and Delchambre:19 , and then spectroscopically confirmed and modelled in Wertz:19 . The latter is a detailed paper dedicated to this single source. This was the first confirmed gravitational lens to be discovered from a machine learning technique that relied only on the relative positions and fluxes of the observed images, without consideration of color information, and thus earned the name ``Centaurus' Victory.''

Delchambre:19 reported an ERT probability of P_ ERT = 0.96 , and a maximum separation of 1 6. The source is at intermediate redshift, z = 1.090 , and the Keck spectroscopy shows no evidence of the central lens galaxy. Gemini IFU observations separated the two quasar images with the smallest angular separation, called A and B in Wertz:19 , as seen in Fig. 3.
GraL J113100.0 442000 --- This system was modeled in GraL IV Wertz:19 . The models presented here agree with the previous ones.

sources.summary: The paper presents GRAL113100-441959 as one of three new Gaia gravitationally lensed quasar candidates, with four lensed images, high ERT probability, and successful simple lens modeling.

quotes.quote: We discovered three new candidates, namely GRAL113100-441959, GRAL203802-400815, and GRAL122629-454209, each of them being composed of four lensed images. The first two candidates are characterized by an ERT probability higher than 0.95 .

As a first step in the validation process, the three new systems were modeled using the public code lensmodel v1.99, Keeton_computationalMethods_2001 . We adopted a simple SIE model plus external shear, using the lensed image positions and G-band flux ratios, except for GRAL122629-454209, as observational constraints. The image positions are extremely well-reproduced (to within 1 mas) whereas the flux ratios are in reasonable agreement with those measured.

gmw_00001036.name: GRAL203802-400815
gmw_00001036.overview: GRAL203802-400815 is a gravitationally lensed quasar candidate [arXiv:1804.11051]. The system is composed of four lensed images that have been successfully modeled using a simple singular isothermal ellipsoid model with external shear [arXiv:1804.11051].

sources.summary: The paper presents GRAL203802-400815 as one of three new Gaia gravitationally lensed quasar candidates, with four lensed images, high ERT probability, and successful simple lens modeling.

quotes.quote: We discovered three new candidates, namely GRAL113100-441959, GRAL203802-400815, and GRAL122629-454209, each of them being composed of four lensed images. The first two candidates are characterized by an ERT probability higher than 0.95 .

As a first step in the validation process, the three new systems were modeled using the public code lensmodel v1.99, Keeton_computationalMethods_2001 . We adopted a simple SIE model plus external shear, using the lensed image positions and G-band flux ratios, except for GRAL122629-454209, as observational constraints. The image positions are extremely well-reproduced (to within 1 mas) whereas the flux ratios are in reasonable agreement with those measured.

gmw_00001037.name: GRAL122629-454209
gmw_00001037.overview: GRAL122629-454209 is a lensed-quasar candidate composed of four lensed images [arXiv:1804.11051]. The individual sources that make up this system are faint and lie close to the Gaia detection limit [arXiv:1804.11051].

sources.summary: The paper presents GRAL122629-454209 as a fainter four-image Gaia lensed-quasar candidate with lower ERT probability, flux uncertainties, Gaia-limit photometric biases, and lens-model validation.

quotes.quote: We discovered three new candidates, namely GRAL113100-441959, GRAL203802-400815, and GRAL122629-454209, each of them being composed of four lensed images.

The lens candidate GRAL122629-454209 is characterized by a lower ERT probability, due to large flux uncertainties. This comes as no surprise since the sources that compose this system are close to the Gaia detection limit. At this regime the Gaia DR2 photometry might show significant biases.

As a first step in the validation process, the three new systems were modeled using the public code lensmodel v1.99, Keeton_computationalMethods_2001 . We adopted a simple SIE model plus external shear, using the lensed image positions and G-band flux ratios, except for GRAL122629-454209, as observational constraints. The image positions are extremely well-reproduced (to within 1 mas) whereas the flux ratios are in reasonable agreement with those measured.

gmw_00001038.name: PKS 0736+017
gmw_00001038.overview: PKS 0736+017 is a flat-spectrum radio quasar and blazar located at a redshift of 0.189 [arXiv:1911.04761, arXiv:2209.01649]. It is hosted by an elliptical galaxy that exhibits a disturbed morphology with nearby companion galaxies [arXiv:astro-ph/0401626, arXiv:0808.0918].

sources.summary: The paper reports H.E.S.S. detection of very-high-energy emission from the z=0.189 FSRQ PKS 0736+017 during a 2015 flare and constrains the gamma-ray emitting region.

quotes.quote: Following the detection of MeV-GeV \ flaring activity from the FSRQ \ (z=0.189) with , the H.E.S.S. array of Cherenkov telescopes triggered target-of-opportunity (ToO) observations on February 18, 2015, with the goal of studying the \ emission in the VHE band. \ ToO observations were carried out during the nights of February 18, 19, 21, and 24, 2015. Together with the multi-wavelength coverage of the flare includes \ observations in soft-X-rays and optical/UV, and optical monitoring (photometry and spectro-polarimetry) by the Steward Observatory, the ATOM, the KAIT and the ASAS-SN telescope. VHE emission from \ was detected with \ during the night of February 19, 2015, only. \ data indicate the presence of a \ flare, peaking at the time of the \ detection, with a flux doubling time-scale of around six hours.

The 4FGL associated source for PKS 0736+017, 4FGL J0739.2+0137, is again best described by a log-parabolic spectrum, with a=2.33 0.02 , curvature b=0.09 0.01 , reference energy E_0 = 503.3 MeV and E F_ (100 MeV 100 GeV ) =(6.12 0.14) 10^ 11 erg cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 . A comparison with a power-law spectrum yielded a log-likelihood ratio of 27.5 in favor of the log-parabola. Under this spectral hypothesis, \ is detected with a test statistic TS; 1996ApJ...461..396M of 2061, i.e.

sources.summary: The paper presents multi-year BVR photometry of PKS 0736+017, describing strong optical variability, a bright 2001 outburst, anomalous reddening with brightness, and its classification as a blazar/FSRQ in an elliptical host.

quotes.quote: We present BVR photometric observations of the blazar PKS 0736+017 . These observations were carried out with three telescopes in Mexico and two in Spain between December 1998 and April 2003. PKS 0736+017 shows remarkable variation at different timescales and amplitudes. Maximum brightness was detected on December 19, 2001 (B=14.90 0.01, V=14.34 0.01, and R=13.79 0.01). A peculiar tendency to redden with increased brightness was detected throughout our observations.

PKS 0736+017 was classified as a blazar by Angel Stockman ( ), and shows strong permitted and forbidden emission lines in the optical domain (Falomo, Scarpa Bersanelli ). Padovani Urry ( ) and Wall Peacock ( ) reported PKS 0736+017 as a Flat Spectrum Radio Quasar (FSRQ), while Brown et al. ( ) report it as an Optically Violently Variable quasar, located at the centre of an elliptical galaxy, possibly a cD galaxy (Wright et al. ; Wyckoff, Gehren Wehinger ; Smith et al. ; Hutchings ; Falomo Ulrich ; Hughes et al. ; Kotilainen, Falomo Scarpa ), which has two close resolved faint companions that are embedded in the nebulosity of the object (Dunlop et al. ; Huthchings, Johnson Pyke ; Bondi et al. ). The absolute magnitude of the host galaxy has been determined between M_R =-24.3 and M_R =-22.2 (Falomo Ulrich ; Hutchings ), and M_V =-21.7 (Smith et al. ).

sources.summary: The paper identifies PKS 0736+017 as a flat-spectrum radio quasar at z=0.189 and measures its BLR size and black hole mass with reverberation mapping.

quotes.quote: PKS 0736+017 (hereafter PKS0736) is a flat-spectrum radio quasar (FSRQ) located at the redshift of 0.189 1967ApJ...147..837L,1993MNRAS.263..999T . The SED of PKS0736 spans from the radio through the ray wavelengths, consisting of the typical double hump structure of Blazars where the low-energy hump is due to synchrotron emission and high energy one is due to the external Compton process 1988AJ.....95..307I, 1998MNRAS.299..433F, Clements2003, Abdalla2020 . The source shows a one-sided parsec-scale jet 1998AJ....115.1295K,article and a compact core Gower1984, 1984A&A...135..289R and is speculated to have been hosted by an elliptical galaxy 1998MNRAS.295..799W,1999MNRAS.308..377M,2000A&A...357...91F . The optical-UV spectrum is characterized by broad emission lines and a big blue bump that is associated with thermal emission from the accretion disk 1975ApJ...201...26B, 1986ApJ...300..216M .

Using different methods, e.g., CCF, JAVELIN , von-neumann, we estimated the size of the BLR, which is found to be 66.4 ^ +6.0 _ 4.2 light days in the rest frame. The BLR size combined with the line width of H provides a black hole mass of 7.32 ^ +0.89 _ 0.91 10^ 7 M_ . The source closely follows the BLR size-luminosity relation of AGN.

sources.summary: The paper reports H.E.S.S. VHE detection of PKS 0736+017, describing it as a nearby FSRQ/blazar with radio jet structure, broad lines, and an elliptical host.

quotes.quote: In this contribution, we present the discovery with the H.E.S.S. telescopes of VHE emission from PKS 0736+017, a quasar at z=0.189, which thus becomes the sixth member of the small family of VHE FSRQs. PKS 0736+017 is a very well studied quasar, first discovered as a powerful radio source with the Parkes telescopes Day66 . The source exhibits observational properties typical of blazars: its radio-morphology is characterized by a core and a single-sided jet; its optical/UV spectrum shows broad emission lines; its host galaxy is a giant elliptical.

Since the beginning of the Fermi-LAT mission, PKS 0736+017 remained quiet until November 2014, when it experienced a first bright flare . After that, the source remained active, and experienced a second bright flare in February 2015, which triggered the H.E.S.S. observations reported in this contribution, and that resulted in the discovery of VHE emission.

Data were analyzed using both the monoscopic and the stereoscopic configurations: a point-like source of VHE photons at the position of PKS 0736+017 is detected during the night of February 19, 2015, only.

sources.summary: The paper presents 0736+017 as an HPQ with rapid variability, complex wavelet structure, and multiple long-lasting timescales across 22, 37, and 90 GHz.

quotes.quote: 0736+017 : This HPQ source was not detected by EGRET but has been listed as a possible ray source to be detected with the new satellite missions AGILE and GLAST bach07 . In Mets ahovi it has been monitored for over 20 years at 22 and 37 GHz and for 11 years at 90 GHz. It exhibits rapid variability and in the optical domain it has been studied by several authors e.g. clements03, ramirez04 . The wavelet plot has a complex structure at all the frequency bands and we found multiple timescales for the source. The most significant and long-lasting ones are 2.7, 3.4 and 1.7 years at 22, 37 and 90 GHz, respectively.

sources.summary: The paper describes PKS 0736+017 as a radio-loud compact flat-spectrum quasar whose disturbed host has nearby companions, high velocity dispersion, high mass-to-light ratio, and an intermediate-age spectrum.

quotes.quote: PKS 0736+017 PKS 0736+017 (labeled 3 in our plots) is a radio-loud, compact, flat-spectrum quasar gower84,romney84 with M _ V = 23.2 and L _ 5 GHz =9.0x10 ^ 42 erg s ^ 1 . The host galaxy (M _ V = 22.7) shows a disturbed morphology with nebulosity extending to nearby companions dunlop93 . This host galaxy's velocity dispersion (342 km s ^ 1 ) is above average for the radio-loud hosts. The effective radius (10.4 kpc) and surface brightness (21.0 mag arcsec ^ 1 ) are slightly below average. It sits high on the Fundamental Plane for its size.

sources.summary: The paper groups 0736+017 among persistently variable millimetre/gamma-ray sources, but its own data show sporadic gamma-ray activity and only a 95 per cent correlation at 360 days.

quotes.quote: On the other hand, sources 0716+714, 0736+017, 0805 077, 1510 089, and 2230+114 show persistent variability both in rays and millimetre band even during the time span considered here. Visual inspection of these sources shows a marginal connection of the events at both these bands which is also visible in the DCF, although the peaks are not significant at 99 per cent level in many cases. None of these sources, except 0805 077 showed a significant correlation in Paper I.

0736+017 : The ray data in this source is characterized by sporadic low-amplitude variations while it is less variable in the millimetre band. Despite the coincidence of the high-state around 2014.5 in millimetre and rays, a correlation only at 95 per cent significance level with a time delay of 360 d was obtained.

sources.summary: The review reports PKS 0736+017 as a newly detected VHE blazar/FSRQ at redshift 0.189, observed by H.E.S.S. during a 2015 gamma-ray flare with night-to-night variability.

quotes.quote: During this ICRC, the detection of four more blazars was reported--OJ 287 and RGB J2056+496 by VERITAS, PKS 0736+017 by H.E.S.S., and OT 081 by MAGIC.

PKS 0736+017 is an FSRQ with a redshift of 0.189 detected during gamma-ray flaring in the MeV--GeV energy band in 2015. Night-to-night flux variation was reported as well as a study of the location of the gamma-ray emission region.

gmw_00001039.name: 3FHL J0015.7+5551
gmw_00001039.overview: 3FHL J0015.7+5551 is a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:2211.04689, arXiv:2006.12865] located at a redshift of approximately 0.217 [arXiv:2211.04689, arXiv:2006.12865]. Its spectrum exhibits host galaxy stellar absorption features superposed on a non-thermal power-law continuum [arXiv:2006.12865], and it has a faint companion star located 2.5 arcseconds away [arXiv:2006.12865].

sources.summary: The paper reports GB6 J0015+5551 as an absorbed BL Lac with an extended NIR counterpart; Lick/KAST spectra reveal host absorption features and a firm z=0.2176 redshift.

quotes.quote: GB6 J0015+5551 is a BL Lac with an extended near-infrared (NIR) counterpart present in the 2MASX catalogue Jar00 . A featureless low S/N spectrum was obtained at the Kitt Peak National Observatory using the R-C spectrograph Alv16 and confirmed its classification. It is located near the Galactic plane at longitude b 6.7^ and very absorbed with E(B-V) = 0.37 Sch11 .

In 2019 August, we observed it with Lick/KAST for 5400 s. The resulting spectrum is shown in Fig. fig_spec1 , top panel, on the left. At z 0.217, Ca ii HK and Mg _b are detected at slightly less than 3 while Na i D is detected at greater than 10 but is contaminated by water absorption. We note that the jet-to-galaxy flux ratio is quite low at 1.1 0.2, which causes the absorption lines to be quite intense, allowing their detection even with a low-to-medium S/N spectrum. The resulting redshift is z = 0.2176 0.0004, compatible with the one reported by Pai20 .

sources.summary: The paper identifies 3FHL J0015.7+5551 with optical, X-ray, and radio counterparts, measures host-galaxy absorption at z=0.2168, and notes a faint nearby stellar companion.

quotes.quote: 3FHL J0015.7+5551 : The observed optical counterpart (g = 18.7) of this gamma-ray source is at (RA,DEC)=(00:15:40, +55:51:45) and spatially coincident there is one X-ray source (J001540+555144), detected by Swift , and one radio source (NVSS J001540+555144). The de-reddened ( E(B-V) = 0.37) spectrum of the source is characterized by stellar absorption features of the host galaxy (Ca II 3934,3968, G-band 4305, Ca+Fe 5269) at z = 0.2168, superposed to a non thermal power law continuum (F _ ^ , = -1.2). From the spectral decomposition of the two components, we set a N/H = 1.5. This object was previously studied by alvarez2016a , who due to low S/N of the spectrum failed to detect the absorption features. At 2.5" (West on the right), there is a faint companion (g = 20.7) that was placed in the slit during our observation and exhibits a stellar spectrum.

gmw_00001040.name: 3FHL J0045.3+2127
gmw_00001040.overview: 3FHL J0045.3+2127 is a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:2312.14144, arXiv:2006.12865] located at a redshift of z = 0.4253 [arXiv:2006.12865]. It has a nearby companion galaxy at a similar redshift [arXiv:2006.12865] and exhibits optical emission that leads its gamma-ray emission by approximately 200 days [arXiv:1808.05625].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 1RXS J004519.6+212735 as a BL Lac object observed by TESS, finding a persistent but cautious 5.8-day QPO candidate.

quotes.quote: The upper right panel shows the light curve of 1RXS J004519.6+212735. This BL Lac object was observed beginning on 8 October 2019 for about 23.7 days in Sec. \ 17 of Cycle 2 observations.

The upper right plot of Fig. \ 3 shows the GLSP for Cycle 2 data of 1RXS J004519.6+212735. The peaks at the period of 5.8 days are marginally consistent with 3 significance. The color-color diagram of the WWZ analysis of this observation (upper right panel of Fig. \ 4) shows that the peak around 5.88 days is persistent throughout the observation and is the only peak having significance more than 3 in the time-averaged WWZ plot on the right-hand side of the panel. Although the peak at 5.9 days is nominally the most significant measurement from both methods, it corresponds to only 4.0 cycles during the length of the observations, so cannot be fully trusted.

sources.summary: The paper reports a power-law optical spectrum for 3FHL J0045.3+2127, identifies a Ca II absorption redshift of z=0.4253, and notes a close galaxy at similar redshift.

quotes.quote: 3FHL J0045.3+2127 : Our optical spectrum (S/N 90) is characterized by a power law emission ( = -1.3). We detect an faint absorption doublet at 5600 that we identify as Ca II 3934,3968 at z = 0.4253. It is worth to note that there is a galaxy close to our target at a projected distance of 10" (see Fig. fig:0045imaging ) with redshift z = 0.4265 provided by SDSS survey. A previous spectrum provided by SDSS survey failed to detect any feature, while shaw2013 provided a lower limit compatible with our measurement. figure 3

sources.summary: The paper identifies J0045+2127 as a BL Lac object whose optical emission leads gamma rays by about 200 days; its radio faintness prevents confirming the expected gamma-ray-to-radio lag.

quotes.quote: There is one BL Lac object (J0045+2127) with the optical leading the rays by roughly 200 days ( 2 ). Considering the typical radio lag noted above, we might speculate that for this source the rays are produced closer to the radio core. However, J0045+2127 is radio-faint and does not provide a significant ray--radio DCF peak (see Table tab:results ), preventing us from seeing the small lag that would be then expected.

gmw_00001041.name: 3FHL J0045.7+1217
gmw_00001041.overview: 3FHL J0045.7+1217 is an intermediate-synchrotron-peaked BL Lacertae object located at a redshift of approximately 0.254 [arXiv:2211.04689, arXiv:2006.12865]. Its spectrum reveals absorption features from the host galaxy's stellar population alongside faint oxygen emission [arXiv:2006.12865].

sources.summary: The paper describes GB6 J0045+1217 as a BL Lac/IBL whose new Lick/KAST spectrum detects host absorption features and yields a firm z=0.2544 redshift.

quotes.quote: GB6 J0045+1217 This BL Lac has a point-like optical counterpart in the SDSS database Bla17 . Inspection of its SED suggests that it is an IBL. Two featureless low signal-to-noise spectra have been reported Shaw13,Krog15 .

In 2019 November, GB6 J0045+1217 was observed with Lick/KAST, obtaining a medium S/N spectrum (Fig. fig_spec1 , top panel, on the right) in which we detected Ca ii HK, Ca i G and Mg _b with low-to-medium signal-to-noise. We measure a redshift value z = 0.2544 0.0005. This is consistent with the values reported by Pai20 and SDSSDR16 and is a firm result.

sources.summary: The paper measures host-galaxy absorption and faint [O II] emission for 3FHL J0045.7+1217, deriving z=0.2549 and decomposing host and nuclear continuum contributions.

quotes.quote: 3FHL J0045.7+1217 : In our spectrum we find absorption lines due to Ca II 3934,3968, G-band 4305, Mg I 5175 and Ca+Fe 5269 attributed to the stellar population of the host galaxy. In addition we also detect a faint (EW = 0.2 ) emission line due to [ O II ] 3727 (see Fig. fig:closeup ). The redshift is 0.2549. This is consistent with the lower limit proposed by shaw2013 . From the spectral decomposition, we set a N/H 4.5 and = -1.0 for the nuclear power law component.

gmw_00001042.name: 3FHL J0134.4+2638
gmw_00001042.overview: 3FHL J0134.4+2638 is a blazar classified as a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:2006.12865]. Its optical spectrum is featureless, and the lack of detectable host galaxy features establishes a redshift lower limit of z > 0.15 [arXiv:2006.12865].

sources.summary: The paper finds 3FHL J0134.4+2638 featureless in its optical spectrum, sets z>0.15 from absent host features, and questions a previously reported Mg II emission feature.

quotes.quote: 3FHL J0134.4+2638 : We obtain an optical spectrum that is featureless. No emission or absorption lines are detected out to a minimum EW of 0.35 - 0.50 . This is consistent with the featureless spectrum of the SDSS survey. From the lack of detection of host galaxy features, we can set a redshift lower limit z > 0.15. An optical spectrum obtained by marchesi2018 exhibits a prominent emission line at 4400 which is interpreted as Mg II 2800.

gmw_00001043.name: 3FHL J0137.9+5815
gmw_00001043.overview: 3FHL J0137.9+5815 is a blazar and BL Lacertae object located at a redshift of z = 0.2745 [arXiv:2006.12865]. Its spectrum is dominated by a non-thermal continuum with very weak absorption features originating from its host galaxy [arXiv:2006.12865].

sources.summary: The paper describes 3FHL J0137.9+5815 as having a non-thermal continuum with weak host-galaxy absorption, yielding z=0.2745 and a spectral decomposition estimate.

quotes.quote: 3FHL J0137.9+5815 : Our spectrum (S/N 145) is dominated by non-thermal continuum. We detect very weak absorption features from the host galaxy (Ca II 3934,3968, Mg I 5175 and Ca+Fe 5269), yielding a z = 0.2745, and from the spectral decomposition we obtain N/H 3. No previous spectrum is available in literature.

gmw_00001044.name: 3FGL J0141.4-0929
gmw_00001044.overview: 3FGL J0141.4-0929 is a BL Lac object [arXiv:1106.1587] and blazar [arXiv:1502.07121] with a redshift of at least 0.501 [arXiv:2006.12865]. In the radio band, it exhibits a core and a one-sided parsec-scale jet [arXiv:1106.1587].

sources.summary: The paper reports PKS 0138 097 as a BL Lac object with a variable Mg II BAL, high-state detections, low-state disappearances, and a core plus one-sided parsec-scale jet.

quotes.quote: We report the discovery of a Broad Absorption Line (BAL) of 10^ 4   km s^ 1 in width in the previously known BL Lac object PKS 0138 097, which we tentatively identified as a Mg II BAL. This is the first detection of a BAL , which is sometimes seen in powerful quasars with high accretion rates, in a BL Lac object . The BAL was clearly detected in its spectra of two epochs at a high luminosity state taken in the Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS), while it disappeared in three SDSS spectra taken at a low luminosity state. The BAL and its variability pattern was also found in its historical multi-epoch spectra in the literature, but has been overlooked previously . In its high resolution radio maps, PKS 0138 097 shows a core plus an one-sided parsec-scale jet.

sources.summary: The paper detects Mg II and oxygen lines in this blazar, derives z=0.736, finds no hydrogen lines because H falls amid sky contamination, and identifies an intervening absorber.

quotes.quote: BZBJ0141 0928 (PKS 0138 097) For this blazar we detected the Mg II doublet at 2800 and three oxygen lines, namely, [ O II ] and the [ O III ] doublet (see Fig. fig1 ). A redshift of z=0.736 0.001 is derived, in agreement with that reported by . Despite a very high S/N ratio, we can not detect Hydrogen emission lines, but the H feature falls in a region contaminated by several sky lines. An intervening system is detected at z=0.5005 0.0005 , featuring absorption by the Mg II 2796,2803, Fe II 2586,2600 doublets, and the Fe II 2382 line.

sources.summary: The paper detects an intervening Mg II absorption system in 3FGL J0141.4-0929, derives a redshift lower limit, and rejects previously proposed Mg II and [O II] emission.

quotes.quote: 3FGL J0141.4-0929 : The only feature in our spectrum is an absorption doublet at 4200 , which is due to intervening Mg II 2800, yielding a spectroscopic lower limit of the redshift of z 0.501. This feature is also present in the spectra reported in stickel1993 and stocke1997 . Note that we do not confirm the emission of Mg II 2800 and [ O II ] 3727 at z = 0.737 proposed by stocke1997 .

gmw_00001045.name: 3FHL J0148.2+5201
gmw_00001045.overview: 3FHL J0148.2+5201 is a blazar and BL Lacertae object located at a redshift of z = 0.437 [arXiv:2006.12865]. Its spectrum exhibits Ca II and G-band absorption lines alongside a nuclear power law component [arXiv:2006.12865].

sources.summary: The paper detects Ca II and G-band absorption in 3FHL J0148.2+5201, measures z=0.437, and contrasts the result with an earlier featureless low-S/N spectrum.

quotes.quote: 3FHL J0148.2+5201 : In our spectrum, the Ca II 3934,3968 doublet is apparent at 5670 and, together with the G-band 4305 absorption line at 6183 , yield z = 0.437. The decomposition of the spectrum provides a N/H 5, and =1.3 for the nuclear power law component. The spectrum published by alvarez2016a , with a poorer S/N, is featureless.

gmw_00001046.name: 3FHL J0241.3+6543
gmw_00001046.overview: 3FHL J0241.3+6543 is a BL Lacertae object and blazar [arXiv:2006.12865]. Spectroscopic analysis of its emission and absorption lines places the galaxy at a redshift of z = 0.1211 [arXiv:2006.12865].

sources.summary: The paper derives z=0.1211 for 3FHL J0241.3+6543 from emission and absorption lines and notes this conflicts with a previously suggested higher redshift limit.

quotes.quote: 3FHL J0241.3+6543 : We detect two emission lines at 7370 which can be interpreted as H _ 6563 and [ N II ] 6584 at z = 0.1211 (see the close-up in Fig. fig:closeup ). At the same redshift, there is a further emission doublet that we attribute to [ S II ] 6716,6731, and an absorption line due to Na I 5892. We note that marchesi2018 , on the basis of an absorption doublet at 4600 , identified as Mg II, suggest a redshift lower limit z 0.645. This conflicts with our redshift determination.

gmw_00001047.name: 3FHL J0250.5+1712
gmw_00001047.overview: 3FHL J0250.5+1712 is a BL Lacertae object and blazar located at a redshift of z = 0.2435 [arXiv:2006.12865]. Its optical spectrum exhibits clear absorption lines from its host galaxy, while its emission lines are constrained to an equivalent width upper limit of 0.45 Å [arXiv:2006.12865].

sources.summary: The paper reports clear host-galaxy absorption in 3FHL J0250.5+1712, measures z=0.2435, limits emission-line strength, and detects the host in spectral decomposition.

quotes.quote: 3FHL J0250.5+1712 : We obtain a spectrum with S/N 100 which exhibits clear absorption lines attributed to the host galaxy (Ca II 3934,3968, H _ 4861, G-band 4305, Mg I 5175, H _ 4861 and Na I 5892). This yields a redshift of z = 0.2435, confirming the proposal of archambault2016 . For the emission lines, the upper limit of EW is 0.45 . The present of the host galaxy is well detected in the decomposition reported in Fig. fig:decomposition , where a N/H = 2 is estimated.

gmw_00001048.name: 3FHL J0423.8+4149
gmw_00001048.overview: 3FHL J0423.8+4149 is a blazar and BL Lacertae object [arXiv:1807.04299, arXiv:2006.12865] with a tentative redshift of z = 0.3977 [arXiv:2006.12865]. It is classified as a low-synchrotron-peaked source [arXiv:1807.04299] and has been proposed as a neutrino source [arXiv:2006.12865].

sources.summary: The paper describes 4C+41.11 as a puzzling LSP source whose SED peaks in the IR, has a hard XRT/LAT component, and resembles AP Lib.

quotes.quote: The SED associated to 4C+41.11 is puzzling. The archival and the UVOT data locate the maximum of the synchrotron peak in the IR band. The hard XRT spectrum suggests that the X-ray continuum is associated to the second bump, likely peaking in the LAT energy band. The position of the synchrotron peak define 4C+41.11 as a LSP. However, the flat LAT spectrum (photon index 2 ) is quite atypical for this class (Ackermann et al. 2015).

sources.summary: The paper detects a single emission line in 3FHL J0423.8+4149, tentatively identifies it as [O III] at z=0.3977, and notes the target as a proposed neutrino source.

quotes.quote: 3FHL J0423.8+4149 : We detect a single emission line at 6997.6 of equivalent width EW = 1.9 . The most plausible identification of this line is [ O III ] 5007 that yields a tentative redshift of z = 0.3977. The other component of [ O III ] 4959 doublet is washed out by the telluric band at 6870 . This target was proposed as neutrino source by righi2019mwl and paiano2019ATel4c41 .

gmw_00001049.name: 3FHL J0433.6+2905
gmw_00001049.overview: 3FHL J0433.6+2905 is a blazar with a severely reddened spectrum [arXiv:2006.12865] and a tentative redshift of approximately 0.91 [arXiv:2006.12865]. The galaxy exhibits correlated multi-wavelength variability, with its optical emission leading both radio and gamma-ray emissions by 20 to 30 days [arXiv:1808.05625].

sources.summary: The paper describes 3FHL J0433.6+2905 as reddened, with a possible broad feature that could be Mg II, implying a tentative redshift of about 0.91.

quotes.quote: 3FHL J0433.6+2905 : The spectrum is severely reddened with E(B-V) = 0.66. There is a possible broad emission feature centered at 5340 in a region of the spectrum where the S/N 15. A similar feature maybe is present in the spectrum reported in shaw2013 , but not identified by the authors. Supposing that this feature is due Mg II 2800, the redshift would be 0.91.

sources.summary: The paper singles out J0433+2905 among sources with nearly simultaneous radio, optical, and gamma-ray correlations; unusually, its optical emission leads both radio and gamma rays by 20-30 days.

quotes.quote: However, there are three sources (J0433+2905, J1849+6705, and J2236+2828) in which all three wavelengths are correlated with lags <30 d. In fact, unusually, J0433+2905 shows the optical emission coming 20--30 d earlier than both radio and rays (the other two are more conventional). This suggests that the emission regions are located in close proximity.

gmw_00001050.name: 3FHL J0500.3+5238
gmw_00001050.overview: 3FHL J0500.3+5238 is a highly reddened blazar located at a redshift of z = 0.1229 [arXiv:2006.12865]. Its spectrum displays several stellar absorption lines alongside a narrow [N II] emission line associated with the host galaxy [arXiv:2006.12865].

sources.summary: The paper measures stellar absorption and narrow [N II] emission in 3FHL J0500.3+5238 at z=0.1229, with host and nuclear components separated by spectral decomposition.

quotes.quote: 3FHL J0500.3+5238 : The target is highly reddened with E(B-V)=0.75 . Several photospheric absorption lines are apparent (G-band 4305, H _ 4861, Mg I 5175, Na I 5892 and H _ 6564), yielding z = 0.1229. At the same redshift there is a narrow emission line attributed to [ N II ] 6584 with EW 1.3 . The spectral decomposition in the power law ( 1.0), due to the nucleus, and in the host galaxy template is reported in Fig. fig:decomposition and we find a N/H = 0.1.

gmw_00001051.name: 3FHL J0540.5+5823
gmw_00001051.overview: 3FHL J0540.5+5823 is a blazar classified as a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:2006.12865]. Its optical spectrum lacks convincing emission or absorption features, which establishes a lower redshift limit of z > 0.10 based on the absence of host galaxy absorption lines [arXiv:2006.12865].

sources.summary: The paper finds no convincing spectral features in 3FHL J0540.5+5823, sets z>0.10 from absent host absorption, and notes its IceCube hard gamma-ray source context.

quotes.quote: 3FHL J0540.5+5823 : There are no convincing emission/absorption features in our spectrum (S/N 60). A lower limit of z > 0.10 can be set from the lack of detected absorption lines of the host galaxy. This source is inserted in the padovani2016 list of hard gamma-ray sources found around the position of IceCube events.

gmw_00001052.name: 3FHL J0600.3+1245
gmw_00001052.overview: 3FHL J0600.3+1245 is a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:2211.04689] located at a redshift of approximately 0.083 [arXiv:2211.04689, arXiv:2006.12865]. Its optical spectrum reveals absorption features from the host galaxy's old stellar population [arXiv:2006.12865] alongside narrow H-alpha and [N II] emission lines [arXiv:2211.04689, arXiv:2006.12865].

sources.summary: The paper describes NVSS J060015+124344 as an absorbed BL Lac with an extended NIR counterpart; its spectrum shows host features and gives a precise z=0.0838 redshift.

quotes.quote: NVSS J060015+124344 NVSS J060015+124344 has an extended NIR counterpart in the 2MASX catalogue Jar00 . It is excessively absorbed with E(B-V) = 0.41, being near the Galactic plane at b 5 ^ . We observed it in 2019 November for 7200 s with Lick/KAST.

The spectrum in Fig. fig_spec2 , top panel, on the right, shows clearly the presence of host galaxy features and indicates that the source is a BL Lac. We detected Ca ii HK, Mg _b , Na i D in absorption and H and [ N ii ] b in emission, obtaining a precise redshift z = 0.0838 0.0003. This value agrees with that reported by Pai20 .

sources.summary: The paper associates 3FHL J0600.3+1245 with radio, X-ray, and optical counterparts, measures host absorption at z=0.0835, and detects narrow H and [N II] emission.

quotes.quote: 3FHL J0600.3+1245 : This gamma-ray object, associated to the radio source NVSS J060015+124344, is classified as BCU in the Fermi catalogs. It is spatially coincident with the X-ray source 1RXS J006014.8+124341 and with the optical counterpart at (RA,DEC) = (06:00:15, 12:43:43). Our optical spectrum exhibits several absorption lines (G-band 4305, H _ 4861, Mg I 5175, Na I 5892 and H _ 6563) attributed to the old stellar population of the host galaxy, that allow us to measure z = 0.0835. At the same redshift, we also detect, at 7133 , two narrow emission lines (EW = 1.2 and EW = 1.8 ) due to H _ 6563 and [ N II ] 6584 (see the close-up in Fig. fig:closeup ).

gmw_00001053.name: 3FHL J0601.0+3837
gmw_00001053.overview: 3FHL J0601.0+3837 is a faint, optically absorbed BL Lacertae object [arXiv:2401.07911, arXiv:2006.12865] that is classified as a blazar [arXiv:1404.5211, arXiv:1506.07006]. The galaxy has a tentative redshift of z = 0.662 [arXiv:2401.07911, arXiv:2006.12865] and is associated with a very luminous host galaxy [arXiv:2401.07911].

sources.summary: B2 0557+38 is a weak, absorbed BL Lac; Keck spectroscopy tentatively detects [O II] at z=0.6622 and implies a very luminous host galaxy.

quotes.quote: B2 0557+38 is a rather weak (i(PanStarrs)=18.4) absorbed ( E_ B-V = 0.484) source in the optical range.

We observed the source with Keck/ESI for 7200 s. In our Keck spectrum (Fig. fig_spec1 , bottom panel, on the left) the detection of Ca II HK absorption feature is uncertain, but we possibly detect [ OII ] at z = 0.6622. Given the low S/N of the feature (38), we consider this as a tentative detection. The redshift of B2 0557+38 remains tentative. The host galaxy is very luminous at M_ R = 24.3.

sources.summary: The paper presents WISEJ060102.86+383829.2 as a Fermi-associated blazar candidate with optical, X-ray, and radio counterparts, selected from low-frequency radio properties and confirmed by a featureless BZB spectrum.

quotes.quote: WISEJ060102.86+383829.2 The Fermi UGS 2FGLJ0600.9+3839 has been associated with the gamma-ray blazar candidate WISEJ060102.86+383829.2 by 2013arXiv1303.3585M . The correspondent optical counterpart in the USNO-B catalog, at 0.1'' , features magnitudes R1=19.11 mag, R2=19.84 mag and I2=18.48 mag. According to 2013ApJS..209....9P this source is positionally consistent ( 0.6'' ) with the X-ray Swift source SWXRTJ060102.8+383829 having a 0.3-10 keV unabsorbed flux 2.3 10 ^ 13 erg cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 .

It is also associated with the radio sources NVSSJ060102+383828 ( 0.5'' ) and WN0557.5+3838 ( 0.8'' ); in particular, based on the source low-frequency radio properties, massaro2013b classified this source as a ray blazar candidate. As shown in Figure fig:spectra1 the featureless spectrum of WISEJ050558.78+611335.9 confirms its BZB nature.

sources.summary: The paper identifies B20557+38 as the X-ray/radio counterpart of 1FHL J0601.0+3838, reporting strong radio fluxes, radio spectral index, blazar-like WISE colors, and BL Lac classification.

quotes.quote: The small XRT error circle of 1FHL J0601.0 + 3838 allows the identification of the X-ray source with the bright radio object B20557+38, which displays 20 and 92 cm flux densities of 704 and 1882 mJy, respectively. This object is reported in various radio archives and has a radio spectrum with index of 0.7 (see NASA/IPAC Extragalactic Database, NED). The source has WISE colours W2-W3=2.47 and W1-W2=0.97 , typical of gamma-ray emitting blazars. It was optically classified as a BL Lac by [ 11 ] .

sources.summary: The paper describes 3FHL J0601.0+3837 as faint and severely extincted, with a mostly featureless spectrum and a possible host Ca II doublet implying z=0.662.

quotes.quote: 3FHL J0601.0+3837 : The source was proposed as a BLL by paggi2014 who show a featureless spectrum of the X-ray counterpart. The source is faint (g = 20.5) and severely extincted ( E(B-V) = 0.46), and we obtained a spectrum with a S/N 40. This is basically featureless, apart for a possible doublet (at 6560 ) that, if attributed to Ca II doublet of the host galaxy, corresponds to a redshift z = 0.662.

gmw_00001054.name: 3FHL J0602.0+5316
gmw_00001054.overview: 3FHL J0602.0+5316 is a gamma-ray source associated with the bright radio galaxy GB6 J0601+5315 [arXiv:2006.12865]. Its optical spectrum reveals a stellar population at a redshift of z = 0.0522, which contains both an elliptical galaxy template and a non-thermal power-law component [arXiv:2006.12865].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 3FHL J0602.0+5316 with radio galaxy GB6J601+5315, measures host stellar absorption at z=0.0522, and decomposes elliptical-galaxy and power-law components.

quotes.quote: 3FHL J0602.0+5316 : The gamma-ray source is associated to the bright radio galaxy GB6J601+5315 and it is inside the error box of a neutrino event detected by IceCube padovani2016 . No previous optical spectrum is found in literature. Our spectrum of the source (g = 17.0) shows the absorption lines (Ca II, G-band, etc...) of the galaxy stellar population at z = 0.0522. The decomposition of the optical spectrum (see Fig. fig:decomposition ) in an elliptical template and power-law ( 1.0) emission indicates the presence of a non-thermal component contributing for 45 .

gmw_00001055.name: 3FHL J0612.8+4122
gmw_00001055.overview: 3FHL J0612.8+4122 is a blazar classified as a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:2006.12865]. Its spectrum is dominated by a power-law continuum without intrinsic emission or absorption lines, though it exhibits intervening Mg II and Fe II absorption systems at a redshift of 1.107 [arXiv:2006.12865].

sources.summary: The paper finds 3FHL J0612.8+4122 dominated by a power-law continuum without intrinsic lines, but detects intervening Mg II and Fe II absorption systems at z=1.107.

quotes.quote: 3FHL J0612.8+4122 : Our spectrum is dominated by a power law continuum without significant intrinsic emission/absorption lines. However, we clearly detect intervening absorption systems of Mg II 2800, Fe II 2382, and Fe II 2586,2600 at z = 1.107. The Mg II absorption line doublet is also apparent in shaw2013 .

gmw_00001056.name: 3FHL J0620.6+2645
gmw_00001056.overview: 3FHL J0620.6+2645 is a gamma-ray source classified as a blazar candidate of uncertain type [arXiv:2006.12865]. Its optical spectrum reveals numerous absorption lines from its host elliptical galaxy at a redshift of z = 0.1329 [arXiv:2006.12865].

sources.summary: The paper classifies 3FHL J0620.6+2645 as a Fermi BCU, reports many host-galaxy absorption lines at z=0.1329, and decomposes elliptical and power-law emission.

quotes.quote: 3FHL J0620.6+2645 : This gamma-ray source (g = 18.5) is classified as BCU in the Fermi catalogs. No previous optical spectrum is available. In our spectrum, many absorption lines from the host galaxy are clearly detected, yielding z = 0.1329. The decomposition of the spectrum, in a elliptical galaxy and a power law ( = -1.5) emission, allows us to set N/H 0.5.

gmw_00001057.name: 3FHL J0640.0-1254
gmw_00001057.overview: 3FHL J0640.0-1254 is a BL Lacertae object and blazar candidate located at a redshift of z = 0.1365 [arXiv:1506.07373, arXiv:2006.12865]. Its spectrum reveals host-galaxy absorption lines diluted by non-thermal nuclear emission, alongside a narrow [N II] emission line [arXiv:2006.12865].

sources.summary: The paper discusses 2MASX J06400717--1253150/PMN J0640--1253 as a possible off-ellipse counterpart with flat radio emission, blazar-strip WISE colors, bright X-rays, and BL Lac candidacy.

quotes.quote: The only X-ray detection lies around 11 arcmin away from the Fermi position (see Figure f3 ). This object is associated with 2MASX J06400717--1253150 and with the flat spectrum radio object PMN J0640--1253 ( 0.4 , Chhetri et al. 2013). This source is fairly bright in radio with a 20 cm flux density of 225 mJy and is detected by WISE with colours ( W2-W3=1.74 and W1-W2=0.457 ), which are once again fully compatible with the blazar strip. It is also quite bright in X-rays (see Table tab4 ), thus strengthening its association with 1FHL J0639.6--1244. It was proposed as a BL Lac candidate for TeV emission by Massaro et al. (2013b).

sources.summary: The paper measures host-galaxy absorption in 3FHL J0640.0-1254 at z=0.1365, decomposes host and nuclear emission, and also detects narrow [N II] emission.

quotes.quote: 3FHL J0640.0-1254 : No previous redshift is reported in literature. Our spectrum exhibits absorption lines (Ca II, G-band, Mg I, Na I) of the host galaxy diluted by the non thermal emission from the nucleus, allowing to measure z = 0.1365. The spectral decomposition in the elliptical galaxy and nucleus power law component provides an N/H 1 (see Fig. fig:decomposition ). In addition to the absorption feature, we also detect a narrow emission line at 7482 (EW = 0.6 ) corresponding to [ N II ] 6584. We note that the object visible at 6.5" from the target is a star and it is derived by the GTC spectrum obtained during the same observation of our target.

gmw_00001058.name: 3FHL J0702.6-1950
gmw_00001058.overview: 3FHL J0702.6-1950 is a blazar classified as a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:2006.12865]. Its optical spectrum is dominated by a featureless power-law continuum, which establishes a lower redshift limit of z > 0.10 due to the absence of host galaxy spectral features [arXiv:2006.12865].

sources.summary: The paper reports no earlier optical spectra for 3FHL J0702.6-1950, finds a featureless power-law continuum, and sets z>0.10 from absent host-galaxy features.

quotes.quote: 3FHL J0702.6-1950 : No optical spectra are found in literature. We obtain the spectrum orientating the slit to intersect the target (g = 19.1) and the close object at 5", which is a star (the classification is provided by GTC spectroscopy). The moderate S/N 50 spectrum of the ray target is dominated by a featureless continuum characterized by a typical power law shape with spectral index = -1.4. Based on the lack of spectral features from the host galaxy, we set a lower limit of z > 0.10.

gmw_00001059.name: 3FHL J0706.5+3744
gmw_00001059.overview: 3FHL J0706.5+3744 is a BL Lacertae object located at a redshift of z = 0.1042 [arXiv:2006.12865]. Its spectrum displays a faint Ca II absorption doublet, which is likely caused by the halo gas of a neighboring galaxy situated 12 arcseconds away [arXiv:2006.12865].

sources.summary: The paper detects a Ca II absorption doublet in 3FHL J0706.5+3744 at z=0.1042 and argues it may arise from halo gas of a nearby galaxy.

quotes.quote: 3FHL J0706.5+3744 : We detect a faint absorption doublet at 4350 (EW 0.6 ) that is attributed to Ca II 3934,3968 (see Fig. fig:closeup ) yielding the redshift z = 0.1042. If these lines were ascribed to the starlight of the host elliptical galaxy, we would expect to observe the trace of the host galaxy in the continuum, which is not present. Moreover these detected lines are narrower, compared to the typical Ca II line width from elliptical galaxies, rather indicating an interstellar absorption origin. Indeed at 12" from the target, there is another galaxy for which we obtained an optical spectrum: we found several emission lines due to H _ 4861, [ OIII ] 4959,5007 and H _ 6563 at z = 0.1042, the same value of the BLL. We suppose that the halo gas of this close galaxy (located at the projected distance 23 kpc at that redshift) can be responsible of the absorption doublet found in the spectrum of ray source.

gmw_00001060.name: 3FHL J0708.9+2240
gmw_00001060.overview: 3FHL J0708.9+2240 is a gamma-ray emitting blazar associated with the radio source GB6J0708+2241 [arXiv:2006.12865]. Its optical spectrum reveals absorption lines from an old stellar population in the host galaxy, placing the object at a redshift of z = 0.2966 [arXiv:2006.12865].

sources.summary: The paper associates 3FHL J0708.9+2240 with a radio source, detects old host stellar absorption at z=0.2966, and decomposes non-thermal and host components.

quotes.quote: 3FHL J0708.9+2240 : This object is a BCU gamma-ray emitter associated to the radio source GB6J0708+2241. The optical counterpart (g = 17.4) is at (RA,DEC)=(07:08:58.3, 22:41:36.0). massaro2015 report an optical spectrum without evident features. We detect absorption lines (Ca II, G-band, and Mg I) due to the old stellar population of the host galaxy, allowing us to locate the source at z = 0.2966. The spectral decomposition, reported in Fig. fig:decomposition ), indicates the presence of a no-thermal power law component ( =-1.15) and a N/H = 5.5.

gmw_00001061.name: 3FHL J0709.1-1525
gmw_00001061.overview: 3FHL J0709.1-1525 is a high-synchrotron-peaked blazar [arXiv:2104.13333] associated with the radio source PKS 0706-15 [arXiv:2006.12865]. Classified as a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:2104.13333], its optical spectrum resembles a reddened elliptical galaxy [arXiv:2006.12865] with a redshift of at least 0.142 [arXiv:2006.12865, arXiv:2104.13333].

sources.summary: The paper confirms 3FHL J0709.1-1525 with PKS 0706-15, finds an elliptical-galaxy-like reddened spectrum at z=0.1420, and detects [N II] emission.

quotes.quote: 3FHL J0709.1-1525 : In the Fermi catalog, this BCU ray source is associated to the radio source PKS 0706-15. From the analysis of Swift /XRT data, inside the Fermi error box we detect the X-ray source J070912-152703, spatially coincident with the radio counterpart and the optical counterpart (g = 18.9) at (RA,DEC)=(07:09:12.3,-15:27:00.0). This confirms the association with PKS 0706-15. No previous spectrum is reported in literature. Our spectrum, highly reddened ( E(B-V) = 0.55), exhibits the shape of an elliptical galaxy with evident absorption lines (Ca II, G-band, H _ , Ca+Fe, Na I, and H _ ) at redshift z = 0.1420.

sources.summary: The paper finds two absorption features in 3FHL J0709.1 1525, derives z>0.142, classifies it as a BL Lac, and notes its HSP blazar classification in 4LAC.

quotes.quote: The optical spectrum for this source exhibits two absorption features at 5551 (H ) and 6017 (Ca + Fe), respectively. This result gives us z> 0.142 for this source which we classify as a BL Lac. This result is in mild disagreement with the redshift obtained by of z=0.142 .
This source is an HSP blazar in 4LAC.

gmw_00001062.name: 3FHL J0723.0-0732
gmw_00001062.overview: 3FHL J0723.0-0732 is an extragalactic blazar located at a redshift of z = 0.3285 [arXiv:astro-ph/0401518, arXiv:2006.12865]. The source exhibits a relativistic, one-sided radio jet [arXiv:astro-ph/0401518], and its optical spectrum shows Ca II and G-band absorption features alongside a non-thermal power-law component [arXiv:2006.12865].

sources.summary: The paper treats 1RXS J072259.5 073131 as a likely extragalactic blazar, based on its one-sided radio jet, blazar-like jet bending, featureless continuum, and blue color.

quotes.quote: 1RXS J072259.5 073131 A relativistic one-sided radio jet (with >0.3 ) has been detected from this source at both arcsecond (PRM02) and sub-arcsecond scales (Rib o et al. ). The bending of the jet in the EVN maps was reminiscent of the ones seen in blazar sources. The featureless continuum typical of blazars that appears in the optical spectrum of Fig.  spec5 is also in agreement with this interpretation. 1RXS J072259.5 073131 is therefore a likely extragalactic source. The possiblity of a stellar reddened continuum does not seem likely as the object appears significantly blue.

sources.summary: The paper detects Ca II and G-band absorption in 3FHL J0723.0-0732, derives z=0.3285, and decomposes elliptical-template and non-thermal power-law components.

quotes.quote: 3FHL J0723.0-0732 : The blazar classification of this ray emitter is proposed by marti2004 who report a featureless optical spectrum. In our spectrum (S/N 170), we detect the absorption doublet attributed to Ca II 3934,3968 and a faint feature due to G-band. This yields a redshift of z = 0.3285. The decomposition of the spectrum (see Fig. fig:decomposition ) in an elliptical template and power-law emission indicates the presence of no-thermal component and allows us to set N/H 5.

gmw_00001063.name: 3FHL J0811.9+0237
gmw_00001063.overview: 3FHL J0811.9+0237 is a blazar at a redshift of z = 0.1726 [arXiv:2006.12865]. Its spectrum reveals absorption lines from an elliptical host galaxy, faint [N II] emission, and a non-thermal component that suggests it may belong to a small group of galaxies [arXiv:2006.12865].

sources.summary: The paper reports host elliptical absorption and faint [N II] emission for 3FHL J0811.9+0237 at z=0.1726 and discusses possible small-group membership.

quotes.quote: 3FHL J0811.9+0237 : There are not previous spectra available for this object. Our spectrum clearly exhibits absorption lines (Ca II, G-band, H _ , Mg I, Ca+Fe, and Na I ) due to the host elliptical galaxy at z = 0.1726. In addition a faint emission line attributed to [ N II ] 6584 is detected at the same redshift. The decomposition of the spectrum in an elliptical galaxy and power-law ( =-1) emission reveals the presence of no-thermal component and the N/H 0.75 (see Fig. fig:decomposition ). We note that 3FHL J0811.9+0237 could belong to a small group of galaxies.

gmw_00001064.name: 3FHL J0816.4-1311
gmw_00001064.overview: 3FHL J0816.4-1311 is a blazar classified as a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:2006.12865]. High-quality optical spectroscopy of the source reveals a featureless spectrum with no emission or absorption lines, establishing a redshift lower limit of z > 0.40 [arXiv:2006.12865].

sources.summary: The paper finds 3FHL J0816.4-1311 featureless despite high S/N, sets z>0.40 from absent host features, and compares earlier intervening Mg II detections.

quotes.quote: 3FHL J0816.4-1311 : Despite the high quality optical spectrum (S/N 300), no emission/absorption lines are found. On the basis of the minimum detectable EW ( 0.10 ) and the lack of feature detection from the host galaxy, we can set a redshift lower limit of z > 0.40. There are previous spectra reported in literature by shaw2013 , who show a featureless spectrum, and by pita2014 that found intervening absorption systems attributed to Mg II 2800 (at z = 0.1902, z = 0.2336, and z = 0.2882) in a spectral range not covered by our spectrum.

gmw_00001065.name: 3FHL J0905.5+1357
gmw_00001065.overview: 3FHL J0905.5+1357 is a BL Lacertae object located at a redshift of approximately 0.2239 [arXiv:2006.12865]. The galaxy exhibits a typical BL Lac continuum with a variable magnitude and a single detected [O III] emission line [arXiv:2006.12865].

sources.summary: The paper observes 3FHL J0905.5+1357 at two epochs, reports BL Lac-like continuum variability, and identifies a possible [O III] line giving z=0.2239.

quotes.quote: 3FHL J0905.5+1357 : We observed this source at two different epochs separated by an interval of about one year (see Table tab:sample ). It is noticeable that the source magnitude obtained from the acquisition images varied by one mag. In Fig. fig:spectra we report the average spectrum of the two epochs. The shape is the typical BLL continuum and the only intrinsic feature detected is an emission line (average EW 0.7 ) at 6133 (see the close-up around this line in Fig. fig:closeup ) which, if attributed to [ OIII ] 5007, yields a redshift of 0.2239. The line luminosity is 8 10 ^ 40 erg/s during the two states, while the continuous varies of a factor of 3.

gmw_00001066.name: 3FHL J0953.0-0840
gmw_00001066.overview: 3FHL J0953.0-0840 is a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:2006.12865] that exhibits a featureless optical spectrum [arXiv:2006.12865]. This spectrum displays a power-law shape typical of its class, establishing a redshift lower limit of z > 0.15 [arXiv:2006.12865].

sources.summary: The paper finds 3FHL J0953.0-0840 featureless in high-quality optical spectroscopy, describes a BL Lac-like power-law spectrum, and sets a redshift lower limit.

quotes.quote: 3FHL J0953.0-0840 : The only spectrum available in literature is provided by shaw2013 that is featureless. In our good optical spectrum (S/N 220), still no emission/absorption line is detected. The spectrum exhibits the power-law shape, typical for BLLs, with spectral index of = -1.5. By the minimum EW method, we can set the redshift lower limit z > 0.15.

gmw_00001067.name: 3FHL J1037.6+5711
gmw_00001067.overview: 3FHL J1037.6+5711 is a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:2211.04689, arXiv:2006.12865] that exhibits a typical power-law continuum [arXiv:2006.12865]. Multiple spectroscopic observations have shown featureless spectra [arXiv:2211.04689, arXiv:2006.12865], leaving the galaxy without a measured redshift but with an estimated lower limit of z > 0.25 [arXiv:2006.12865].

sources.summary: The paper finds that GB6 J1037+5711 remains without a measured redshift after several featureless spectra, including a new high-S/N Lick/KAST observation.

quotes.quote: GB6 J1037+5711 This BL Lac has been observed in spectroscopy several times. Moderate S/N spectra have been reported Shaw13,SDSSDR16 while a high S/N spectrum can be found in Pai20 . All these spectra are featureless and no redshift has been measured yet.
A 3400 s observation was performed with Lick/KAST resulting in a S/N = 100 spectrum. No features could be detected (Fig. fig_spec2 , bottom panel, on the left).

sources.summary: The paper confirms previous featureless spectra for 3FHL J1037.6+5711, finds a BL Lac-like power-law continuum, and derives a z>0.25 lower limit.

quotes.quote: 3FHL J1037.6+5711 : All previous spectra reported in literature and the SDSS survey lau1998, caccianiga2002, shaw2013 do not exhibit emission/absorption lines. Our high quality (S/N 300) spectrum shows the typical BLL power-law shape and it still is featureless. The estimated minimum EW is 0.15 and this yields a redshift lower limit z > 0.25.

gmw_00001068.name: 3FHL J1055.6-0125
gmw_00001068.overview: 3FHL J1055.6-0125 is a blazar classified as a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:2006.12865]. Its spectrum is characterized by a power-law continuum with no apparent emission or absorption features, establishing a redshift lower limit of z > 0.55 [arXiv:2006.12865].

sources.summary: The paper reports no literature redshift for 3FHL J1055.6-0125, finds a BL Lac-like power-law continuum, and sets z>0.55 from absent spectral features.

quotes.quote: 3FHL J1055.6-0125 : No redshift is reported in literature. The spectral shape of our spectrum is well described by a power law emission ( -1.5), typical of a BLL. No emission or absorption features are apparent at level of EW 0.40 and we can set a redshift lower limit of z > 0.55.

gmw_00001069.name: B2 0003+38A
gmw_00001069.overview: B2 0003+38A is a low-redshift flat-spectrum radio quasar hosted by a disk galaxy [arXiv:2106.03940]. The system features a rotating disk, an extended emission-line region stretching to a projected distance of 20 kiloparsecs, and an outflow [arXiv:2106.03940].

sources.summary: The paper analyzes B2 0003+38A as a low-redshift FSRQ hosted by a gas-rich, rotation-dominated disk galaxy with an extended emission-line region and outflow.

quotes.quote: We present a detailed analysis of the single-slit optical spectrum of the Flat-Spectrum Radio Quasar (FSRQ) B2 0003+38A, taken by the Echellette Spectrograph and Imager (ESI) on the Keck II telescope. This classical low-redshift FSRQ ( z=0.22911 , as measured from the stellar absorption lines) remains underexplored in its emission lines, though its broad-band continuum properties from radio to X-ray is well-studied. After removing the unresolved quasar nucleus and the starlight from the host galaxy, we obtain a spatially-resolved 2-D spectrum, which clearly shows three components, indicating a rotating disk, an extended emission line region (EELR) and an outflow. The bulk of the EELR, with a characteristic mass M_ EELR 10^ 7 M_ , and redshifted by v_ EELR 120 km s ^ 1 with respect to the quasar systemic velocity, shows a one-sided structure stretching to a projected distance of r_ EELR 20 kpc from the nucleus. The rotation curve of the rotating disk is well consistent with that of a typical galactic disk, suggesting that the FSRQ is hosted by a disk galaxy.

B2 0003+38A is classified as an FSRQ due to its broad emission lines in the optical spectrum and the flat radio spectrum with = -0.3 Stickel1994,Healey2007,Massaro2009 . Resolved by a VLBI mapping at 2.29 GHz Morabito1982 , it shows a prominent core that emits more than 95 per cent of the 2.3 GHz flux, along with a weak jet pointing to the sourth-east Fey2000 , as shown in Figure fig:fig0 . Kinematic analyses show quasi-stationary knots at the jet base and relativistic motions downstream Hervet2016 .

gmw_00001070.name: GN-z11
gmw_00001070.overview: GN-z11 is a highly luminous Lyman-break galaxy at a spectroscopically confirmed redshift of z = 10.603 [arXiv:2302.07256, arXiv:2306.02468]. It hosts an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2405.05772] and exhibits a compact, rotation-dominated disk structure [arXiv:2404.16963] with nitrogen-rich chemical enrichment [arXiv:2310.00671].

sources.summary: The paper models GN-z11 as a high-redshift starburst whose supernova-driven outflows can temporarily clear dust, explaining its low reddening, UV visibility, and later re-obscuration by returning dusty gas.

quotes.quote: Only one spectroscopically confirmed very high-redshift ( z 10 ) galaxy is known to date, dubbed GN-z11, at redshift z=11.09^ +0.08 _ 0.12 , when the age of the Universe was 400 Myr. GN-z11 has been identified as a bright ( M_ uv 22 mag), massive (stellar mass M_ 10^9 ) Lyman- break galaxy in observations with the Hubble Space Telscope /WFC3/IR and Spitzer /IRAC instrument Oesch2016 . Near-infrared observations of [ CIII ] 1907, CIII ] 1909 doublet and OIII ] 1666 lines with Keck I telescope allowed the measurement of the redshift more precisely as z=10.957_ 0.001 ^ +0.001 Jiang2020 .

Therefore, we will concentrate in this paper on GN-z11, treating it as a prototypical high- z starburst galaxy. The photometric spectral energy distribution (SED) of GN-z11 fits the standard templates of Bruzual2003 , corresponding to stellar mass M_ 10^9 with a half-light raidus R_h 0.6 0.3 kpc, the best fit age being 40 Myr. The stellar metallicity is Z=0.05 - 0.5Z_ Oesch2016 , along with little to no dust E(B-V) 0.01 0.01 Jiang2020 .

Even though the amount of dust that can be produced and ejected by SNe during the entire period of star formation in GN-z11 ( 40 Myr) is quite sufficient ( M_d 8 10^5 ) to enshroud the stellar light, the mechanical power from the same SNe is capable to blow the dust shell out and to break in it patchy transparent windows. The apparent `puzzling' lack of dust in the galaxy GN-z11 might reflect that detection of this galaxy was rather accidental, when its evolutionary phase passed through a period of ``fog clearing''.

sources.summary: The paper presents JWST/NIRSpec spectroscopy confirming GN-z11 at z=10.603, detecting Lyman-alpha emission, rich emission lines, high ionization, low oxygen abundance, and possible unusual nitrogen enrichment.

quotes.quote: We present JWST/NIRSpec spectroscopy of one of the most luminous galaxies at z>10 . GN-z11 is in the GOODS-North field and had previously been identified as a Lyman break galaxy candidate by Oesch2015 , with a tentative redshift of z=11.1 from a continuum break in slitless HST/WFC3 spectroscopy Oesch2016 . We see numerous emission lines and a strong Lyman- break in our NIRSpec spectroscopy, and we unambiguously measure the redshift to be z=10.603 . Our grating spectrum reveals Lyman- in emission, making it the first object at z>9 with confirmed Lyman- emission.

The rest-frame equivalent width is W_0=18 . The emission is offset 555 redward of the systemic redshift and spatially extended. These properties are consistent with models of Lyman- backscattering off the far side of galactic scale outflows. The NIRSpec spectrum of GN-z11 is remarkably rich with emission lines, enabling us to study the ISM properties at z>10 .

sources.summary: The paper uses JWST observations of GN-z11, a z=10.603 Lyman-break galaxy candidate with Ly emission, to constrain the neutral hydrogen fraction at z=10.6.

quotes.quote: Recent observations of GN-z11 with JWST have revealed a Ly emission line with an equivalent width of 18 2 . At z=10.6 , this galaxy is expected to lie in the heart of reionization. We use a series of inhomogeneous reionization simulations to derive the distribution of the \ EW after traveling through the neutral intergalactic medium with varying average neutral gas fraction, . We use these distribution to place an upper limit of \ < 0.88 at z=10.6 at 95 confidence level.

Recently, presented the spectroscopic confirmation of the Lyman-break galaxy candidate GN-z11. Using multiple rest-frame optical and UV emission lines, the redshift of GN-z11 was measured to be z=10.603 0.0013 , currently the highest known for a likely star-forming galaxy (see discussion in bunker2023 ). At redshift well above 10 , GN-z11 is expected to be embedded in a largely neutral Universe, as inferred from a number of available data at z 8 e.g., fan2006, mcgreer2011, mcgreer2015, ono2012, schroeder2013, choudhury2015, greig2016, mason2018, mason2019, greig2019, hoag2019, jung2020 . These observational studies suggest that the universe volume weighted neutral fraction ( ) is well above 90 at z 10 , although no observational constraints currently exist at such early epochs. The spectrum of GN-z11 offers the unique opportunity to understand the state of the diffuse hydrogen at z>10 .

sources.summary: GN-z11 is treated as a spectroscopically confirmed z=10.60 JWST galaxy, broadly consistent with extrapolated simulations but unusual, with AGN signatures that the simulations do not model.

quotes.quote: The JADES survey Bunker_2019 has provided measurements on five galaxies with spectroscopically confirmed redshifts at z>10 . Three of these galaxies are the most distant yet detected. , and have constrained physical properties of these five galaxies, finding that the galaxies lie at (mean) spectroscopic redshifts of z = 10.38 (GS-z10-0), z = 11.58 (GS-z11-0), z = 12.63 (GS-z12-0), z = 13.20 (GS-z13-0) and z = 10.60 (GN-z11).

Both GN-z11 and GS-z11-0 are unusual galaxies and detailed hydrodynamic simulations are clearly struggling to match their luminosities and stellar masses perfectly. It may be that these galaxies contain star formation processes not currently correctly modelled by our subgrid recipes - black by this we mean that these JWST galaxies may contain stars born from an extremely top heavy IMF including a population of extremely massive stars e.g. Charbonnel_2023, Nagele_2023 or unusually strong bursts of star formation e.g. Kobayashi_2023 . In addition to this, GN-z11 shows signatures of AGN activity maoilino-agn, GNz11-AGN-scholtz which is not currently modelled by for example black and the processes responsible for seeding this massive black hole may well be a factor in explaining its large luminosity .

We also address the fact that both the GN-z11 and GS-z11-0 galaxies are above the (extrapolated) mass of the most massive galaxy in the RP region (when a sSFR of 10^ 8 yr ^ 1 is used). GN-z11 was recently found to have an anomalously high abundance of N/O GN-z11-NO-abundance and likely hosts an active AGN Maiolino_2023, GNz11-AGN-scholtz , a characteristic that will be missed by Renaissance .

sources.summary: The paper compares APOGEE high-N/O stars to GN-z11, arguing its anomalous nitrogen abundance and stellar mass could be explained if much of its star formation occurred in bound compact clusters.

quotes.quote: One such example is GN-z11, identified previously with the HST and Spitzer Oesch2016 . The follow-up observations with NIRSpec reported measurements of oxygen, carbon, and neon lines, as well as unusually prominent levels of nitrogen emission, indicating high relative [ N/O ] abundance at moderately low oxygen abundance [ O/H ] Bunker2023 . Using these measurements Cameron2023 estimated the relative nitrogen abundance in GN-z11 to be ( N / O ) 0.25 , which is much higher than this ratio in the Sun, i.e. ( N / O )_ = -0.86 see Lodders2019 .

Conservative (fiducial) measurements reported by Cameron2023 for the GN-z11 galaxy are shown with grey (black) lines. The Figure shows a large number of APOGEE field giants with N/O and O/H abundance ratios similar to those reported for GN-z11. There is a total of 100 high- [ N/O ] low-metallicity ( Fe/H <-0.7 ) stars marked with larger filled circles. Of these 73 are classified unambiguously as in-situ and 27 as accreted. Given our conclusion that the majority of the in-situ high- [ N/O ] stars originated in massive bound clusters and that the clusters, in turn, constituted a significant fraction of star formation at early epochs, the implication of these results is that a significant fraction of star formation in GN-z11 also occurs in bound compact star clusters.

For a galaxy like GN-z11 with a stellar mass M_ 5 10^8 M_ at z 10.6 corresponding to the cosmic time of 430 Myr, f_ cl =0.5 implies that 100-200 bound clusters of mass 10^6-10^7 M_ form during 300-400 Myr. On Myr time scales the UV luminosity of the parent galaxy will thus spike shortly after cluster formation and will rapidly decrease before the next cluster forms.

sources.summary: The paper presents JWST observations of GN-z11 at z=10.603, identifying AGN indicators, an extended Ly halo, nearby emission-line candidates, and a candidate protocluster-core environment.

quotes.quote: Recent observations with the James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) have further refined the spectroscopic redshift of GN-z11, one of the most distant galaxies identified with the Hubble Space Telescope (HST) at z=10.603 . The presence of extremely dense gas ( >10^ 10 cm ^ 3 ), the detection of high-ionisation lines and of CII*1335 emission, as well as the presence of an ionisation cone, indicate that GN-z11 also hosts an Active Galactic Nucleus (AGN). Further photometric and spectroscopic follow-up demonstrates that it lies in a large-scale, overdense structure with possible signatures of Population III (PopIII) stars in its halo. Surprisingly, Ly has also been detected despite the expected largely neutral inter-galactic medium at such a redshift.

We exploit recent JWST/NIRSpec IFU observations to demonstrate that the Ly emission in GN-z11 is part of an extended halo with a minimum size of 0.8--3.2 kpc, depending on the definition used to derive the halo size. The surface brightness of the Ly halo around GN-z11 appears consistent with Ly halos observed around z 6 quasars. At the wavelength of Ly at z 10.6, we identify three other emission line candidates within the IFU Field-of-View with no UV rest-frame counterpart visible in deep images from the JWST/NIRCam. If confirmed, this could be the first evidence that the local region of GN-z11 represents a candidate protocluster core, forming just 400 Myr after the Big Bang.

sources.summary: GN-z11 is discussed as a compact z=10.60 galaxy with strong nitrogen emission, high N/O abundance, proto-GC-like conditions, and a possible intermediate-mass black hole.

quotes.quote: Long known as the most distant spectroscopically-confirmed galaxy Oesch2016 , GN-z11 has recently lead to new exciting and intriguing results, after the first spectra of this galaxy were obtained with the JWST. Indeed, the JWST/NIRSpec observations of allowed to confirm a very high redshift of this source ( z=10.60 ) and showed the presence of hydrogen, carbon, oxygen, magnesium, and neon emission lines in the rest-UV and rest-optical spectrum, often seen in star-forming galaxies at low-redshift and detected at z 4-8 in other JWST spectra see e.g., Schaerer2022_SMACS,Cameron2023_JADES, Nakajima2023JWST-Census-for, Tang2023JWST/NIRSpec-Sp . Most surprisingly, however, the spectrum of GN-z11 revealed the presence of strong \ and \ lines Bunker2023JADES-NIRSpec-S , which are very rarely detected in galaxies see e.g., Barchiesi2022The-ALPINE-ALMA .

Not only the abundance ratios observed in GN-z11 resemble those of GC stars. Its compactness and high ISM density also indicate conditions expected in young very massive clusters, which could be proto-GCs Senchyna2023GN-z11-in-conte,Charbonnel2023N-enhancement-i . GN-z11 might thus also be the first high-redshift object where the long sought-for peculiar abundance patterns characterizing GCs are observed e.g., and references therein 2017MNRAS.469L..63R,2019A&ARv..27....8G . These exciting and surprising findings obviously pose the question of the uniqueness of GN-z11, beg for more examples, and call for a better understanding of similar objects, if they exist.
Finally, we propose that some N-emitters enriched by SMS could also have formed intermediate mass black holes, and we suggest that this might be the case for GN-z11.

sources.summary: The paper describes GN-z11 as a luminous z=10.6 high-redshift/Lyman-break galaxy whose JADES imaging and spectroscopy reveal extreme early-universe properties and unusual nitrogen-rich emission.

quotes.quote: the JWST NIRCam 9-band near-infrared imaging of the luminous z=10.6 galaxy GN-z11 from the JWST Advanced Deep Extragalactic Survey (JADES) proved that the spectral energy distribution (SED) is entirely consistent with the expected form of the high-redshift galaxy. In a simultaneous work the spectroscopy of GN-z11, the most luminous candidate z>10 Lyman break galaxy is presented. The nitrogen lines are clearly observed. Quoting the authors: "The spectroscopy confirms that GN-z11 is a remarkable galaxy with extreme properties seen 430 Myr after the Big Bang."

Observations of GN-z11 with JWST/NIRSpec disclosed numerous oxygen, carbon, nitrogen, and helium emission lines at z=10.6 . The data prefers (N/O), greater than 4 times solar and the derived C/O 30 solar. Nitrogen enhancement in GN-z11 cannot be explained by enrichment from metal-free Population III stars. The suggested explanation is that yields from runaway stellar collisions in a dense stellar cluster or a tidal disruption event provide promising solutions to give rise to these unusual emission lines at z=10.6 , and explain the resemblance between GN-z11 and a nitrogen-loud quasar.

sources.summary: The paper identifies GN-z11 as a remarkably distant, bright galaxy whose grism spectrum shows flux along its trace, while IRAC photometry indicates a blue, low-dust, young stellar population.

quotes.quote: This shows clear flux exactly along the dispersion location of GN-z11. After rebinning to a spectral resolution of 93 \ (the native resolution element of the grism) this flux detection is 1-1.5 per resolution element longward of 1.47 m and consistent with zero flux shortward of that. The total spectral flux averaged over 1.47-1.65 m represents a clear 5.5 detection.

Despite being the most distant known galaxy, GN-z11 is relatively bright and reliably detected in both IRAC 3.6 and 4.5 m bands from the S-CANDELS survey Ashby15 . This provides a sampling of its rest-frame UV spectral energy distribution and even partially covers the rest-frame optical wavelengths in the IRAC 4.5 m band (see Figure fig:SEDfits ). The photometry of GN-z11 is consistent with a spectral energy distribution (SED) of M/M_ 9 using standard templates see appendix Bruzual03 . The UV continuum is relatively blue with a UV spectral slope =-2.5 0.2 as derived from a powerlaw fit to the H_ 160 , K , and [ 3.6 ] fluxes only, indicating very little dust extinction see also Wilkins15 .

sources.summary: The paper derives super-solar N/O and elevated C/O in GN-z11 at z=10.6, arguing ordinary enrichment is fine-tuned while stellar-collision or tidal-disruption scenarios may explain the lines.

quotes.quote: Recent observations of GN-z11 with JWST /NIRSpec revealed numerous oxygen, carbon, nitrogen, and helium emission lines at z=10.6 . Using the measured line fluxes, we derive abundance ratios of individual elements within the interstellar medium (ISM) of this super-luminous galaxy. Driven by the unusually-bright 1750 and 1486 emission lines (and by comparison faint 1660, 1666 lines), our fiducial model prefers ( N/O )>-0.25 , greater than four times solar and in stark contrast to lower-redshift star-forming galaxies.

The derived ( C/O )>-0.78 , ( 30 solar) is also elevated with respect to galaxies of similar metallicity ( 12+ ( O/H ) 7.82 ), although less at odds with lower-redshift measurements. Given the long timescale typically expected to enrich nitrogen with stellar winds, traditional scenarios require a very fine-tuned formation history to reproduce such an elevated N/O. We find no compelling evidence that nitrogen enhancement in GN-z11 can be explained by enrichment from metal-free Population III stars.

Interestingly, yields from runaway stellar collisions in a dense stellar cluster or a tidal disruption event provide promising solutions to give rise to these unusual emission lines at z=10.6 , and explain the resemblance between GN-z11 and a nitrogen-loud quasar.

sources.summary: The paper describes GN-z11 as a compact z=10.6 galaxy with exceptional nitrogen enrichment, arguing that supermassive-star pollution in proto-globular-cluster-like conditions can explain its abundance ratios.

quotes.quote: The detection of unusually bright NIII ] and NIV ] UV emission lines in the JWST/NIRSpec spectrum of GN-z11 2023arXiv230207256B revealed exceptional nitrogen enrichment in this high- z galaxy. The N/O abundance ratio in the interstellar medium (ISM) of GN-z11 derived by see also Senchyna2023 2023arXiv230210142C is more than four times solar (log(N/O) 0.25 ). The C/O ratio of GN-z11 is compatible with normal values but poorly constrained 2023arXiv230210142C . The peculiar abundance ratios inferred in GN-z11 cannot be explained by classical stellar yields and standard galactic chemical evolution.

In this Letter we present an empirical comparison of the C, N, and O abundance ratios in the compact z=10.6 galaxy GN-z11 derived recently from JWST observations with data in GC and field star observations, and a quantitative estimate of SMS nucleosynthesis that can form through runaway collisions in proto-GCs, as proposed in the scenario of . We show that the SMS polluter models can simultaneously explain the observations of GN-z11 and of the GCs, with similar dilution values between the H-processed material in the SMS and the original proto-GC gas. Finally, N-enriched and O-depleted gas in GN-z11 should also be enriched in Na and Al and in similar high- z objects, if the observations reflect the conditions during GC formation.

sources.summary: The paper describes GN-z11 as a luminous near-redshift-eleven galaxy with strong nitrogen lines and argues metal-enriched supermassive stars could explain its inferred nitrogen enrichment.

quotes.quote: GN-z11 is an unusually luminous high redshift galaxy which was recently observed to have strong nitrogen lines while at the same time lacking traditional signatures of AGN activity. These observations have been interpreted as a super-solar nitrogen abundance which is challenging to explain with standard stellar evolution and supernovae enrichment. We present simulations of four models of metal enriched supermassive stars after the zero age main sequence which produce super-solar nitrogen consistent with the observations of GN-z11. We then show that the most massive model ends its life in a violent explosion which results in even greater nitrogen pollution.

This brings us to GN-z11, a luminous galaxy slightly under redshift eleven with metallicity about ten percent the solar value Oesch2016ApJ...819..129O,Jiang2021NatAs...5..256J,Tacchella2023arXiv230207234T . GN-z11 was recently observed by JWST NIRSpec to have unusually bright nitrogen lines, while simultaneously lacking the N V (E > 77.5 eV) associated with an active galactic nucleus (AGN) Bunker2023arXiv230207256B . Cameron2023arXiv230210142C analyze the NIRSpec observations and infer a N/O ratio two to four times the solar value, though they also conclude that an AGN origin cannot be ruled out.

sources.summary: The paper presents JWST spectroscopy of GN-z11, arguing that high-ionization lines, dense BLR-like gas, CIV outflow, and virial estimates reveal an accreting black hole powering its luminosity.

quotes.quote: Here we present an extensive analysis of the JWST-NIRSpec spectrum (from the JADES survey) of GN-z11, an exceptionally luminous galaxy oesch_remarkably_2016 at z=10.6, revealing the detection of the high ionization [ NeIV ] 2423 transition and semi-forbidden nebular lines tracing gas densities higher than 10^ 10 cm^ 3 , typical of the Broad Line Region of Active Galactic Nuclei (AGN). These spectral features indicate that, in addition to star formation, GN-z11 also hosts an accreting black hole. We do not exclude a contribution from extreme stellar populations, however Wolf Rayet stars alone cannot account for many of the spectral properties.

The spectrum also reveals a deep and blueshifted CIV 1549 absorption trough, tracing an outflow with a velocity of 800-1000 , higher than typically observed in starburst galaxies, hence likely driven by the AGN.
