It is also characterised by a relatively low redshift (z = 0.218, Spinrad et al. 1985) and short lobes (compare Table 1). 3C123 also shows exceptionally high radio luminosity and non-typical radio structure (Riley Pooley 1978). Its hot spots along with the general spectral analysis were studied in detail by Looney Hardcastle (2000) with the highest resolution to date (0.6 ).

The authors concluded that 3C123 may have two pairs of (primary and secondary) hot spots, and there is no clear evidence for a continuing outflow between these hot spots, suggesting that the source may be undergoing recurrent activity.

The radio spectrum of this source is very broad thanks to its high radio brightness. Surprisingly, analysis shows that there is no major difference between the spectra resulting from the predicted flux densities for KDA EXT and KDA models (Fig. 4), though the KDA EXT fit is slightly more consistent with observations. There is also no statistical preference of the KDA EXT model, according to the obtained value for the adopted level of significance (Table 5).

sources.summary: The paper treats 3C 123 as a luminous, structurally peculiar FR II source, fitting its eastern hot spot with an SSC model and comparing its energetics with Cygnus A.

quotes.quote: 3C 123 ( z=0.2177 ) is famous for its high luminosity and peculiar radio structure. The lobes take the form of diffuse twisted plumes unlike those in any other well-known sources (e.g., Riley Pooley 1978; Hardcastle et al. 1997). We adopt the observational data including Chandra's one compiled by Hardcastle et al (2001).

The thick solid curve in Figure fig:3c123 shows the best fit spectrum for the eastern hot spot in 3C 123 obtained by the one-zone SSC model for _ min =1 and c_ 1 =1 . Although the radio spectrum is rather poorly fitted, this does not much affect our conclusions as discussed later in this subsection. As is for Cygnus A, by comparing Table tablehotspots_3c123 with Table tablehotspots_ana , we find that analytic estimation in the previous subsection is a good approximation. The important result is that U_ 3,acc /U_ B =18 for 3C 123.

sources.summary: The paper treats 3C 123 as a strongly reddened FR II radio galaxy at redshift 0.22, with MERLIN/VLA-resolved hotspots, a weak unresolved core, and recovered 1.4 GHz flux.

quotes.quote: 3C 123 The continuum image of 3C 123 ( l = 170 58, b = --11 66) is shown in Fig. 3c123cont . Previous high-resolution VLA images of this FR II radio galaxy have been presented by cgs91 (images made by R. Laing at 1.7, 4.9 and 14.9 GHz) and by hapr97 . 3C 123 has been identified with a 19 mag galaxy with strong Galactic reddening at a redshift of 0.22.

hapr97 show a 5 GHz MERLIN image (beam 0.05 arcsec); both components shown in Fig. 3c123cont (the E and W hotspots) have been imaged by these authors. The weak core ( 50 mJy) is also detected in the current image at right ascension 04^ h 37^ m 04 38 , declination 29 40 13 8 (J2000). The MERLIN continuum image of 3C123 contains all the flux density that was detected by the VLA at 1.4 GHz with 1.2 arcsec resolution (R. Laing in Atlas of DRAGNS http://www.jb.man.ac.uk/atlas/).

sources.summary: The paper presents 3C123 as a large radio galaxy and low-frequency calibrator, finding it mostly non-variable but noting a small unexplained low-frequency flux-density drop after 2008.

quotes.quote: 3C123 is a radio galaxy at redshift z=0.218. With an angular size of 44 arcseconds, it is the largest of the objects in this study, and is of limited use for the calibration of high-resolution interferometers. We included it in this program primarily because it is a well-known calibrator source at low frequencies.

The structure of the source, with 3 arcseconds resolution at 22.5 GHz, utilizing the data taken in this program, is shown in Fig. fig:3C123Maps . 3C123 is one of the non-variable objects that we have identified. The right panel of Fig. fig:3C123Maps shows the source's secular flux density evolution, based on our polynomial expression for 3C286.

sources.summary: The paper reports Chandra detection of 3C 123 hotspot X-rays, interpreting them as synchrotron self-Compton emission near equipartition and noting its peculiar high-luminosity radio structure.

quotes.quote: Chandra X-ray Observatory observations of the powerful, peculiar radio galaxy 3C 123 have resulted in an X-ray detection of the bright eastern hotspot, with a 1-keV flux density of 5 nJy. The X-ray flux and spectrum of the hotspot are consistent with the X-rays being inverse-Compton scattering of radio synchrotron photons by the population of electrons responsible for the radio emission (`synchrotron self-Compton emission') if the magnetic fields in the hotspot are close to their equipartition values. 3C 123 is thus the third radio galaxy to show X-ray emission from a hotspot which is consistent with being in equipartition.

Here we report a third detection, of the E hotspot of the radio galaxy 3C 123, based on our Chandra AO1 guest observer (GO) observations. 3C 123 is a z = 0.2177 radio galaxy, notable for its peculiar radio structure. Like normal classical double sources it has twin hot spots on either side of the active nucleus, but the lobes take the form of diffuse twisted plumes unlike those in any other well-studied object (e.g. Riley Pooley 1978; Hardcastle \ 1997, hereafter H97).

sources.summary: The peculiar FRII galaxy 3C 123 is dominated by complex extended X-ray emission, with only an upper limit derived for its core contribution.

quotes.quote: 3C 123 This peculiar FRII radio galaxy is dominated by an X-ray extended component. Radial profiling does not require a core component, but the X-ray distribution is complex. Since radial profiling may not be appropriate, we derive an upper limit on the core contribution from a 15-arcsec source circle around the brightest X-ray peak. The counts we tabulate are corrected to our standard 1-arcmin source circle and 1--2-arcmin background region. We will discuss this object in more detail elsewhere (Hardcastle , in preparation).

sources.summary: The paper uses 90 GHz BIMA results for 3C 123, noting lobe emission and a northern-lobe spectrum inconsistent with standard spectral aging.

quotes.quote: More recently, we used BIMA to observe the radio galaxies 3C 123 and 3C 20 (Looney Hardcastle 2000, Hardcastle Looney 2001) at similar frequencies. We noted that the integrated spectrum of a part of the northern lobe of 3C 123 was inconsistent with an ageing model, leading us to suggest that particle acceleration might be ongoing in that region. An upper limit on the flux density of the southern lobe was consistent with ageing models, however.

As discussed in Section intro , we showed in the case of the peculiar object 3C 123 that the spectrum of the detected northern lobe region is inconsistent with a Jaffe Perola (1973; hereafter J-P) spectral ageing model, perhaps a result of particle reacceleration in the lobe.

sources.summary: The paper uses earlier Chandra Galactic-column estimates for 3C 123, fits a dominant absorbed power law, and notes that reddening makes its excitation classification uncertain.

quotes.quote: 3C 123 The Galactic column density used for this source is taken from the earlier analysis of the Chandra data by Hardcastle \ (2001) and the dominant (absorbed) power law model has similar parameters to the one fitted there. The large amount of Galactic reddening (the source is known to lie behind a molecular cloud) makes its classification as a low-excitation object uncertain. Chiaberge \ (2002) show that its position on their diagnostic plane (equivalent width of [ OIII ] vs. optical to radio core flux ratio) is more consistent with a classification as a NLRG, and this would be consistent with the observed luminous, heavily absorbed nuclear X-ray component.

gmw_00001080.name: NGC 7418
gmw_00001080.overview: NGC 7418 is a massive, late-type spiral galaxy featuring a weak optical bar and an asymmetric stellar disk [arXiv:1912.07452]. It is associated with a kinematically distinct H I cloud [arXiv:1506.04399] and a 500-kpc H I tail likely stripped via tidal interactions [arXiv:1803.08263, arXiv:1912.07452].

sources.summary: The paper reports a kinematically distinct H I cloud at NGC 7418, with no obvious stellar counterpart, perhaps embedded in a broader low-column-density gas distribution.

quotes.quote: The other galaxy where we detect an \ cloud is NGC 7418. The \ disc of this object has similar size as the stellar disc, and we detect the cloud at the disc's north-west edge (Fig. fig:ic5270 ). The cloud is clearly a separate gas system as it is kinematically distinct from the disc rotation. It hosts 6 10^8 \ of , about 13 percent of the total, and its velocity is close to systemic. As in the case of IC 5270, we find no obvious stellar counterpart to the \ cloud in available GALEX, DSS and WISE images.

This suggests that the cloud is not a dwarf companion of NGC 7418. Similar to the case of IC 5270, the Parkes spectrum of NGC 7418 shows some excess emission relative to the ASKAP spectrum (see velocities between 1400 and 1500 \ in Fig. fig:ic5270 ), and the HIPASS detection is resolved by the 15.5 arcmin PSF. Therefore, it is possible that the \ cloud of NGC 7418, too, is embedded in an underlying, low-column-density gas distribution.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 7418 as the likely source of a 500-kpc H I tail, arguing that its morphology and kinematics favor tidal stripping over ram pressure.

quotes.quote: The morphology of the tail, and the fact that it appears to connect to NGC 7418, suggests that it consists of \ stripped from this galaxy. However, the tail extends beyond NGC 7814 which makes it unlikely the tail is due to ram-pressure stripping. Figure fig:tails shows a position-velocity diagram of the large \ tail taken along the curved path shown in Fig. \ fig:KAT7mom0 . This figure shows that also the kinematics of the tail suggest a link with NGC 7418.

In the VLA data, we only detect that part of the \ tail which is west of NGC 7418 and the morphology clearly shows that it consists of \ coming from NGC 7418. The larger, eastern part of the tail is not (or barely) detected in the VLA observations. This is consistent with the KAT-7 data, which show that towards NGC 7418, the eastern tail gets fainter and that it may consists of more diffuse .

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 7418 as a massive late-type spiral with asymmetric disk, weak bar, blue outer arms, and connection to complex HI tidal features.

quotes.quote: NGC 7418 It is the second brightest ( M_g=-21.98 mag), massive ( 10.87 10^ 10 M _ ), and biggest ( R_ e ,g =21.72 kpc), galaxy of the group and it is at a projected distance of 34 8 from IC 1459 in the South region of the group. This late-type spiral has a very asymmetric stellar disk in the North-West direction. It has a weak optical bar, seen in the surface brightness profile rather than in the optical colour composite image (Figure fig:ngc7418sp ). It seems to be stronger in H band imaging Eskridge2002 . The spiral arms are well defined in the inner regions, becoming bluer and smoothed in the outer parts.

As explored by Serra2015 and Oosterloo2018 , the complex HI tidal features elongated from the North region of the group to NGC 7418 seems to be the result of the first tidal galaxy-galaxy and galaxy-environment interactions having occurred in the BGG neighbourhood.

sources.summary: The paper reports Balmer emission, fitting degeneracy, and an unusually large nuclear-cluster mass for NGC 7418, making it dominate recent-star-formation averages.

quotes.quote: Three of the spectra (NGC 1042, NGC 7418, NGC 7424) have prominent Balmer emission lines, which are less broad than the absorption lines.

NGC 7418 has two minima that are almost equally good fits (a consequence of the double valued H _A ). The other minimum is at log( ) = 6.86 _ 0.06 ^ +0.05 and Z =0.008. The values we quote are marginally preferred because the minimum is somewhat broader and the age agrees with the one that would be obtained if one would marginalize over all metallicities.

NGC 7418, which has by far the largest mass of all NCs and also a sizable percentage of young stars, would contribute the bulk of the recent star formation to a simple average although it may not be typical for NCs as a class.

gmw_00001081.name: IC 5270
gmw_00001081.overview: IC 5270 is a galaxy characterized by a highly asymmetric neutral hydrogen distribution [arXiv:1506.04399, arXiv:1803.08263]. Its gas disk is notably extended to the northeast [arXiv:1803.08263], and two massive neutral hydrogen clouds have been detected in its immediate vicinity [arXiv:1506.04399].

sources.summary: ASKAP detects two massive H I clouds near IC 5270; lacking obvious stellar counterparts, they are interpreted through higher-resolution data as shaping the asymmetric HIPASS spectrum.

quotes.quote: The most interesting result of these observations is the detection of a few \ clouds in the proximity of IC 5270 and NGC 7418 (Figs. fig:mosaic and fig:ic5270 ). In the former we detect two clouds. The brightest of the two is 4 arcmin north of IC 5270 and contains 1.6 10^9 \ of . The second cloud is 7 arcmin north-east of IC 5270 and its \ mass is 1.0 10^9 .

Therefore, while we find no obvious stellar counterpart, deeper images may be necessary to definitively establish that the \ clouds are not gas-rich dwarf neighbours of IC 5270. Another possibility is that the clouds are truly ``dark'', and are the densest clumps of a larger, low-column-density \ structure -- possibly made of \ stripped from IC 5270.

The obvious conclusion is that the \ clouds are responsible for the strong peak above 1900 , which gives the spectrum its unusually asymmetric appearance. While the Parkes data alone were not sufficient to unambiguously resolve the clouds from \ in IC 5270 on the sky or in velocity, the new ASKAP data allow us to interpret unambiguously the shape of the HIPASS spectrum.

sources.summary: The paper links IC 5270 to the northern intragroup H I structure, emphasizing its similar velocity to the tail and its very asymmetric H I distribution.

quotes.quote: However, Fig. fig:tails also shows that the northern part of the tail, the cloud N of the tail, and IC 5270 lie at similar velocities, perhaps indicating that IC 5270 also may have a connection to the intragroup \ observed. The very asymmetric \ distribution of IC 5270 reinforces this see also ser15 .

Starting from the north, the \ disk of IC 5270 is quite extended to the NE (as was also observed by ser15 ), IC 5269B to the SE, IC 5264 to the E, NGC 7418A (the only galaxy showing optical distortions) to the NE, NGC 7418 to the SW, ESO 406-83 to the S, NGC 7421 to the SE and IC 5274 to the SE. In fact, none of the gas-rich members have a regular well centred \ distribution.

gmw_00001082.name: IC 5264
gmw_00001082.overview: IC 5264 is a small, edge-on early-type spiral galaxy [arXiv:1912.07452] that features a warped outer stellar envelope and a dust lane [arXiv:1912.07452]. It exhibits a faint, broad H I velocity profile [arXiv:1803.08263, arXiv:1912.07452] and a lower-velocity gas cloud that may be an edge-on tail [arXiv:1803.08263].

sources.summary: The paper describes IC 5264 as having an unusually broad H I velocity profile plus a lower-velocity cloud, possibly an edge-on tail from the galaxy.

quotes.quote: Starting from the north, the \ disk of IC 5270 is quite extended to the NE (as was also observed by ser15 ), IC 5269B to the SE, IC 5264 to the E, NGC 7418A (the only galaxy showing optical distortions) to the NE, NGC 7418 to the SW, ESO 406-83 to the S, NGC 7421 to the SE and IC 5274 to the SE. In fact, none of the gas-rich members have a regular well centred \ distribution.

Another feature, not evident from the integrated \ distribution, is revealed in Fig. \ fig:tails , when looking at the spectrum taken at a position near IC 5264. \ is detected over a very large velocity range of 800 \ and the profile seems to consist of the two-horned profile from IC 5264 with in addition an \ cloud at lower velocities. No optical counterpart is present to this cloud and one possibility is that IC 5264 is moving mostly along the line of sight and that the \ cloud is a tail originating from IC 5264 and which is seen edge on.

sources.summary: The paper describes IC 5264 as a small edge-on early-type spiral projected in IC 1459 halo, with warped stellar structure and faint extended HI.

quotes.quote: IC 5264 This is a peculiar, very small ( R_ e ,g =4.05 kpc), edge-on, early-type spiral. The average colour ( g-=0.79 0.06 mag), is too red for a LTG, but this galaxy is seen in projection in the IC 1459 stellar halo. Hence this could explain that they have a similar g- average colour. From the VST images (Figure fig:ic5264sp ), we were able to detect the warped structure of the outer stellar envelope and a dust lane in the southeaster side of the stellar disk in agreement with the HI off-centre distribution, which seems to be tangent to the outer envelope of IC 1459 in the eastern direction. Its HI component is faint and it is spread in a wide velocity range Serra2015 . It has been suggested that IC 5264 is the principal donor of HI gas around IC 1459 due to a tidal interaction Saponara2018 .

gmw_00001083.name: NGC 7418A
gmw_00001083.overview: NGC 7418A is a low-mass, HI-rich spiral galaxy with a clumpy, extended star-forming disk [arXiv:1912.07452, arXiv:1506.04399]. It exhibits optical distortions and asymmetric HI morphology, suggesting possible tidal interactions with the neighboring galaxies IC 1459 and NGC 7418 [arXiv:1912.07452, arXiv:1803.08263].

sources.summary: The paper identifies ESO 406-27 as the bluest bright group galaxy, an HI-rich low-mass system possibly interacting with IC 1459 and NGC 7418.

quotes.quote: ESO 406-27 This is the bluest ( g- = 0.34 0.20 mag) bright galaxy of the group, and has a total stellar mass ( 3.2 10^ 9 M _ ) one order of magnitude smaller than the other galaxies except for IC 5269B. It is located on the South-West side of IC 1459, at a project distance of 19.5 . It is one of the HI brightest sources with a HI mass of ( 4.6 0.9) 10^9 M _ Serra2015 . From Figure fig:eso40627sp , ESO 406-27 might be interacting both with IC 1459 and NGC 7418. It has a two spiral-arms pattern clearly in the direction of these galaxies, which corresponds to the HI asymmetry.

However we have not enough information yet for making an hypothesis regarding the evolution/interaction scenario between IC 1459, ESO 406-27, and NGC 7418. GALEX data confirms that there are two spiral, UV-bright arms extending from northeast to southwest Thilker2007 .

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 7418A as having disturbed, asymmetric H I following an extended clumpy star-forming disk, possibly shaped by interaction with IC 1459 or accretion.

quotes.quote: Our data show hints of a disturbed \ morphology in NGC 7418A, whose asymmetric \ disc follows the shape of the low surface brightness, extended, clumpy optical star-forming disc with prominent spiral arms. classify it is as ``mixed-type extended UV disc'', i.e., an object exhibiting ``a large, blue low-surface-brightness zone and structured UV-bright complexes at extreme galactocentric distances beyond the traditional star-formation threshold''. The proximity of NGC 7418A to the (also disturbed) early-type central galaxy IC 1459 suggests that the two may have interacted recently. The intra-group \ detected by and north of NGC 7418A may have been removed from this galaxy during such interaction. Alternatively, NGC 7418A may have recently accreted gas from this diffuse structure, and the faint, clumpy star formation in its outer disc may have been triggered by such accretion.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 7418A as a possible interaction partner of NGC 7418, while noting KAT-7 rules out a direct velocity connection to the long H I tail.

quotes.quote: The ATCA data suggested a possible link with NGC 7418A, but the KAT-7 image shows that this is not the case.

Starting from the north, the \ disk of IC 5270 is quite extended to the NE (as was also observed by ser15 ), IC 5269B to the SE, IC 5264 to the E, NGC 7418A (the only galaxy showing optical distortions) to the NE, NGC 7418 to the SW, ESO 406-83 to the S, NGC 7421 to the SE and IC 5274 to the SE.
The most likely candidate that NGC 7418 is interacting with is NGC 7418A, a galaxy which also shows strong signs of tidal interactions, even on optical images.

gmw_00001084.name: KUG 1141
gmw_00001084.overview: KUG 1141 is a Seyfert 1 galaxy [arXiv:2304.10125, arXiv:2011.14254] that hosts an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2304.10125]. Over the past decade, the galaxy has exhibited a simultaneous flux increase across the optical, ultraviolet, and X-ray bands [arXiv:2304.10125, arXiv:2011.14254].

sources.summary: The paper treats KUG 1141 + 371 as a Seyfert 1 galaxy whose rising X-ray emission resembles accretion-state changes, and whose compact VLBI radio emission and brightness temperature indicate an AGN origin.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: right ascension =11^ h 44^ m 29.87078^ s and declination =36^ 53' 8.616711''

quotes.quote: Jiang et al. described the multi-wavelength variability observed in the Seyfert 1 galaxy, KUG 1141 + 371 ( z=0.038 ). Notably, its hard X-ray flux has increased by over a magnitude since 2009, while its soft X-ray flux has also shown a steady increase since 2007. They used the optical and UV data to describe the thermal emission of the accretion disk, and calculated the X-ray-to-UV ratio to use it analogously to the X-ray hardness of stellar-mass black hole systems. From the modeling of the spectral energy distribution (SED), they argue that the variability behaviour of KUG 1141 + 371 is similar to that of the state transitions seen in X-ray binaries and can be related to changes in the accretion state.

At both frequencies, KUG 1141 + 371 appeared as a single compact component (Fig. fig:EVNmaps ). The coordinates of the brightest pixel in the 4.9 GHz image are right ascension =11^ h 44^ m 29.87078^ s and declination =36^ 53' 8.616711'' with an accuracy of 1 mas .

Therefore, the brightness temperature can only be calculated at 1.7 GHz, T_ B,1.7 =3.3 10^7 K, while only a lower limit can be given at 4.9 GHz, T_ B, 4.9 1.1 10^ 8 K. These high values indicate that the radio emission in KUG 1141 + 371 originates from an AGN .

sources.summary: The paper studies KUG 1141 as a Seyfert 1 AGN with decade-scale optical, UV, and soft X-ray brightening, while decomposing UVOT images into AGN and host-galaxy light.

quotes.quote: \ is a Seyfert 1 galaxy that shows a simultaneous flux increase in the optical and UV bands in the past decade. For instance, the latest \ observation in 2019 shows that the UVW2 flux of the AGN in \ has increased by over one order of magnitude since 2009. Meanwhile, the soft X-ray flux of \ also shows a steady increase by one order of magnitude since 2007.

For each of the image, a point spread function (PSF) is constructed from nearby stars in the field of view with count rates comparable to that of KUG 1141 (within 0.1 - 0.3 dex). The background is calculated from a source-free region near the source independently and fixed in the fitting. The AGN component is modelled with the point-like source, i.e. a PSF function, with an initial magnitude from the the results of UVOTSOURCE . The host galaxy component is modelled with an exponential-disc function or a more general S ersic function, whichever shows a better fit.

gmw_00001085.name: IC 2497
gmw_00001085.overview: IC 2497 is a nearby spiral [arXiv:1601.07550] and postmerger galaxy [arXiv:1711.06270] hosting a Compton-thick active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1711.06270] that has recently faded from a luminous quasar state [arXiv:1104.2521, arXiv:1110.6921]. Past interactions triggered nuclear starburst activity [arXiv:1006.4096] and created a massive surrounding reservoir of neutral hydrogen gas [arXiv:1006.4096, arXiv:1601.07550].

sources.summary: The paper presents EVN and MERLIN radio observations of IC 2497, arguing that it hosts an obscured AGN, a nuclear starburst, jet-related radio emission, and interaction-fueled gas.

quotes.quote: We present high- and intermediate resolution radio observations of the central region in the spiral galaxy IC 2497, performed using the European VLBI Network (EVN) at 18 cm, and the Multi-Element Radio Linked Interferometer Network (MERLIN) at 18 cm and 6 cm. We detect two compact radio sources, with brightness temperatures above 10 ^5 K, suggesting that they are related to AGN activity. We show that the total 18 cm radio emission from the galaxy is dominated neither by these compact sources nor large-scale emission, but extended emission confined within a sub-kpc central region.

In conclusion, our results suggest that IC 2497 is an "active" galaxy in the broadest possible sense - it exhibits evidence of both a highly obscured AGN and nuclear star-forming activity in its central regions. There is also evidence (e.g. HI absorption, see Joshetal09 ) that our line-of-sight towards the nuclear regions of IC 2497 is obscured. Past interactions between IC 2497 and neighbouring galaxies have presumably triggered and fuelled both the AGN and enhanced star-forming activity observed, and are likely to be responsible for the creation of a huge reservoir of HI gas around the galaxy. We believe that Hanny's Voorwerp corresponds to that part of the debris trail that is illuminated by the AGN ionisation cone (and indeed the nuclear starburst). Many AGN with weak radio sources exist but the presence of a large surrounding gas reservoir is most likely only present in interacting systems (such as LIRGs, which IC 2497 seems to be a prototype of) .

sources.summary: The paper argues IC 2497 no longer hosts the luminous quasar required to ionize Hanny's Voorwerp, finding only low-luminosity AGN evidence and highlighting the host galaxy.

quotes.quote: Archival Infrared Data We have obtained multiple, independent observations to assess the current nuclear luminosity of IC 2497. Light from an obscured L_ bol 10^ 46 \ quasar in IC 2497 should be re-emitted at mid- and far-infrared wavelengths. Archival IRAS observations show that the infrared temperature of IC 2497 of f_ 25 m /f_ 60 m = 0.1 is cold compared to that of nearby, luminous highly-obscured AGN 1990IRASF.C......0M, 1999ApJ...522..157R and IC 2497 also adheres to the radio-far infrared correlation 2010A&A...517L...8R . Thus, IC 2497 does not exhibit any evidence for reprocessed infrared emission from an active nucleus.

We then obtained a second observation of IC 2497 with the \ X-ray space observatory using the EPIC-pn, MOS-1 and MOS-2 detectors on 2010-04-19 with a total useful observing time of 11 kiloseconds and sensitivity between 0.1 and 7 keV. With , we detect a source at 0.1-5 keV, which can be fit with a combination of two spectral components: a collisionally ionized plasma ( T=0.78^ +0.18 _ 0.14 keV), consistent with thermal emission from a warm interstellar medium in IC 2497, and an unabsorbed power law ( = 2.5 0.7 ) from a very low luminosity AGN with L_ 2-10 keV = 4.2 10^ 40 .

Since the quasar in IC 2497 shut down less than 70,000 years ago, it offers an unobstructed view of the host galaxy of a quasar. The close distance of IC 2497 furthermore means that we can view this quasar host galaxy in greater detail than any other system.

sources.summary: The paper describes IC 2497 as hosting an obscured AGN, neutral hydrogen reservoir, compact central radio components, and extended emission suggestive of a jet toward Hanny's Voorwerp.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: R.A. = 09h41m04.094s and Dec. = +34d43m58.03s (J2000 coordinates extracted from VLA FIRST Survey )

quotes.quote: Recent radio observations using the Westerbork Synthesis Telescope (WSRT) , has shown the existence of a radio continuum source at the central position of IC 2497, with an extension in the direction of Hanny's Voorwerp. In addition, neutral hydrogen is detected around the galaxy and the Voorwerp is probably part of this large gas reservoir. HI is also detected in absorption towards the central radio core. Clearly, obscuring material in the direction of the core of IC 2497 is present, while the extended continuum points towards the presence of a radio jet.

Our observations further support the hypothesis that IC 2497 contains an obscured AGN at its core . In this scenario the Voorwerp is illuminated by the AGN after a jet clears a path from the AGN towards the Voorwerp. From our e-VLBI and MERLIN observations we detect 2 compact radio sources (C1 and C2), at the center of IC 2497.

sources.summary: IC 2497 hosts Hanny's Voorwerp and a fading AGN; the paper links the energy mismatch to past quasar-level luminosity, radio jets, a circumnuclear outflow, hot gas, and possible kinetic-mode switching.

quotes.quote: A very luminous and extensive EELR was found in the course of the Galaxy Zoo project cjl2008 near the spiral galaxy IC 2497. Known after its discoverer as Hanny's Voorwerp, this object shows high-ionization emission lines with ratios essentially identical to the narrow-line region of an AGN, spanning a projected range from 15-35 kpc from the galaxy nucleus, which fails by at least 2 orders of magnitude in bolometric output to match the ionization requirements of the cloud ( cjl2009 , HSTHV ). The cloud's electron temperature, narrow line widths, and quiescent velocity field indicate photoionization rather than shocks as the energy source. H I mapping by shows this to be the ionized part of a 300-kpc trail of otherwise neutral gas, suggesting a strong galaxy interaction many Gyr ago. The energy mismatch between the nucleus of IC 2497 and the ionization requirements in Hanny's Voorwerp indicates that the nucleus faded from a luminosity associated with QSOs to a modest Seyfert or LINER level within 10^5 years.

The identification of radio jets and a circumnuclear outflow in IC 2497 ( Jozsa , Rampadarath , HSTHV ) led us to speculate that some of the extreme variability needed to explain the ionization of Hanny's Voorwerp might come not solely from a drop in the accretion-driven luminosity of the AGN in IC 2497, but from some of the ``missing" luminosity switching to a kinetic mode. This idea is supported by a ``bubble" of hot gas around the fading AGN of IC 2497 seen with Chandra Sartori .

sources.summary: The paper describes IC 2497 as a spiral, type-2 Seyfert/QSO host whose faded AGN ionized Hanny's Voorwerp and whose nucleus has dust, weak X-rays, and an ionized gas ring.

quotes.quote: We present Hubble Space Telescope imaging and spectroscopy, along with supporting GALEX and ground-based data, for the extended high-ionization cloud known as Hanny's Voorwerp, near the spiral galaxy IC 2497. WFC3 images show complex dust absorption near the nucleus of IC 2497. The galaxy core in these data is, within the errors, coincident with the VLBI core component marking the active nucleus.

STIS optical spectra show the AGN to be a type 2 Seyfert of rather low luminosity. The derived ionization parameter log U = -3.5 is in accord with the weak X-ray emission from the AGN. We find no high-ionization gas near the nucleus, adding to the evidence that the AGN is currently at a low radiative output (perhaps with the central black hole having switched to a mode dominated by kinetic energy).

sources.summary: The paper presents IC 2497 as a nearby spiral hosting a fading AGN, extended hot X-ray gas, tidal H I structure, and possible AGN-inflated bubbles or shocks.

quotes.quote: We present deep X-ray observations of the core of IC 2497, the galaxy associated with Hanny's Voorwerp and hosting a fading AGN. We find extended soft X-ray emission from hot gas around the low intrinsic luminosity (unobscured) AGN ( L_ bol 10^ 42 10^ 44 erg s ^ 1 ). The temperature structure in the hot gas suggests the presence of a bubble or cavity around the fading AGN ( E _ bub 10^ 54 10^ 55 erg).

IC 2497 is a massive ( M 10^ 10 M_ ), nearby ( z = 0.05 ) spiral galaxy associated with Hanny's Voorwerp (HV hereafter), an extended emission line region (11 x 16 kpc in projected extent) located at 20 kpc in projection from the core of the galaxy and closely matching its redshift. Large scale radio observations of the system revealed an extended HI structure ( M = 8.5 2.1 10^ 10 M_ ) with irregular kinematics around the southern part of IC 2497, which suggests a tidal origin ( Jozsa2009 ). These observations also show a kpc-scale structure which may be a jet coming from the AGN.

sources.summary: The paper links IC2497's active nucleus and radio jet to Hanny's Voorwerp, finding extended steep-spectrum emission from the nucleus that likely punctured the neutral gas halo and faded long ago.

quotes.quote: HV is a highly-ionised region in the environs of the galaxy IC2497, first discovered by the Galaxy Zoo project. The new 150 MHz observations are considered in the context of existing multi-frequency radio data and archival narrow-band imaging from the Hubble Space Telescope , centred on the [ Oiii ] emission line. The combined sensitivity and spatial resolution of the LoTSS data -- which far exceed what was previously available at radio frequencies -- reveal clear evidence for large-scale extended emission emanating from the nucleus of IC2497. The radio jet appears to have punched a hole in the neutral gas halo, in a region co-located with HV. The new 150 MHz data, alongside newly-processed archival 1.64 GHz eVLA data, reveal that the extended emission has a steep spectrum, implying an age >10^8 yr.

The jet supplying the extended 150 MHz structure must have ``turned off'' long before the change in X-ray luminosity reported in recent works. In this picture, a combination of jet activity and the influence of the radiatively efficient active galactic nucleus are responsible for the unusual appearance of HV.

sources.summary: The paper presents IC 2497 as a recently active galaxy whose quasar faded rapidly, leaving Hanny's Voorwerp as a light echo and a comparison point for post-active radio relic systems.

quotes.quote: The above findings are in line with those reported recently for IC 2497, a galaxy that 70,000 years ago or less hosted a quasar but now appears as a normal one. This scenario stems from the presence of Hanny's Voorwerp, a nebulous object in its vicinity excited by that QSO in the epoch when IC 2497 was active.

To date, perhaps the most spectacular evidence of a past activity in a galaxy that currently appears as normal was brought by Schaw2010 for IC 2497 . No more than 70,000 years ago, it hosted a luminous quasar, the light echo of which is now observable in the form of a nebulous object known as Hanny's Voorwerp, located 15-25 kpc (in projection) from IC 2497. The authors point out that the ``death'' of the QSO hosted by IC 2497 must have been sudden so that, statistically, objects similar to Hanny's Voorwerp should be very rare because of their ephemeral nature.

sources.summary: The paper presents X-ray, mid-IR, and optical evidence that IC 2497 is a postmerger galaxy hosting a Compton-thick AGN whose luminosity recently declined sharply.

quotes.quote: firstpage - lastpage We present new Nuclear Spectroscopic Telescope Array ( NuSTAR ) observations of the core of IC 2497, the galaxy associated with Hanny's Voorwerp . The combined fits of the (0.5-8 keV) and (3-24 keV) X-ray spectra, together with mid-IR photometry, optical longslit spectroscopy and optical narrow-band imaging, suggest that the galaxy hosts a Compton-thick AGN ( N_ H 2 10^ 24 cm ^ 2 , current intrinsic luminosity L_ bol 2-5 10^ 44 erg s ^ 1 ) whose luminosity dropped by a factor of black 50 within the last 100 kyr.

HV is an extended emission line region ( 11 16 kpc in projected extent) located at 20 kpc in projection from the core of IC 2497, a massive ( M_* 10^ 11 M_ ), nearby ( z = 0.0502 ) postmerger galaxy. The optical spectrum and the Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS) g band luminosity of HV are dominated by [ O iii ] 5007 emission. The presence of emission lines of high-ionization species such as [ Ne v ] 3346, 3426 and [ ] 4616 , together with the relatively quiet kinematics (line widths <100 km s ^ 1 ) and the low electron temperature ( T_ e 13,500 K) suggests that the gas is photoionized by the hard continuum of an AGN in IC 2497 rather than ionization from star formation or shocks Lintott2009 . In order to produce sufficient ionizing photons to power the observed emission, the AGN output should be at least L_ ion 10^ 45 erg s ^ 1 between 1 and 4 Ryd (13.6-54.4 eV), corresponding to a bolometric luminosity L_ bol 10^ 46 erg s ^ 1 . Although the photoionization state of HV requires the presence of a strong AGN, the optical spectrum of the core of IC 2497 only shows weak optical emission lines ( Lintott2009 ; Keel2012b ) and a weak radio source ( 10^ 38 erg s ^ 1 ; Jozsa2009 ).

sources.summary: IC 2497 is described as a LINER galaxy whose Hanny Voorwerp cloud requires a formerly QSO-level nucleus that faded dramatically within roughly 100,000 years.

quotes.quote: Using extended emission-line clouds as probes of AGN history came of age with the discovery of Hanny's Voorwerp, a high-ionization region extending 45 kpc in projection from the low-ionization nuclear emission-line region (LINER) galaxy IC 2497 at z=0.05 Lintott2009 . Linewidths and electron temperature indicate that the gas is photoionized rather than shock-excited, while a combination of ionization-parameter and recombination arguments bound the required nuclear ionizing luminosity to be 1-4 10^ 45 erg s ^ 1 . However, X-ray spectroscopy shows the nucleus of IC 2497 to be only modestly absorbed, with ionizing luminosity only 10^ 42 erg s ^ 1 Schawinski2010a . It is difficult to avoid the conclusion that the nucleus of IC 2497 was in fact a QSO (the nearest known luminous QSO) until roughly 10^5 years before our current view, and has faded dramatically in the interim; radio and HST observations offer hints that some of its energy output may have switched to kinetic forms over this timespan ( Josza2009 , Rampadarath , Schawinski2010a , Keel et al. in preparation).

sources.summary: The paper links IC 2497 to Hanny's Voorwerp, describing a nearby faint spiral whose past encounter drew tidal debris and triggered a transient quasar episode.

quotes.quote: This was the case with `Hanny's Voorwerp (Object)', a colourful cloud of ionised material and some active star formation located near a faint spiral galaxy, IC 2497. Figure zoo shows an HST image from Keel et al. (2012). Follow-up studies suggest that the Voorwerp is tidal debris from a past encounter between IC 2497 and another galaxy that drew out a tidal tail and triggered infall into a supermassive black hole, producing a transient quasar episode. The Voorwerp's light is an echo of the quasar phase acting on tidal debris (Lintott et al.

sources.summary: IC 2497 is discussed as the galaxy hosting Hanny's Voorwerp, where AGN fading or obscuration can explain the absent hard X-ray nucleus and extended ionized emission.

quotes.quote: Besides that, such hypotheses were proposed to explain, on a larger scale, the observed emission from the irregular gas cloud SDSS J094103.80+344334.2, known as ``Hanny's Voorwerp'', which is located 25 kpc south-east from the galaxy IC 2497 joz09,lin09 . The absence of a hard X-ray source at the nucleus of IC 2497, together with the Seyfert-like emission from Hanny's Voorwerp, led to the scenario involving a reduction of the luminosity of the AGN at the nucleus of IC 2497 during the last 10^5 yr. In this case, Hanny's Voorwerp is still being illuminated by the ionizing radiation emitted by the AGN in the past lin09,kee12 . The alternative scenario, involving no reduction of the AGN luminosity but instead a high obscuration towards the AGN in IC 2497, was also discussed joz09,lin09,ram10 .

gmw_00001086.name: HD1
gmw_00001086.overview: HD1 is a Lyman-break galaxy candidate at a photometric redshift of z > 12 [arXiv:2112.09141, arXiv:2201.00823]. It is characterized by a lack of detected dust continuum [arXiv:2211.03896] and may be powered either by extreme star formation [arXiv:2201.00823] or a supermassive black hole [arXiv:2201.00823].

sources.summary: The paper identifies HD1 as a z about 13 galaxy candidate, supports z greater than 12 photometrically, and reports a tentative ALMA line consistent with [O III] at z=13.27.

quotes.quote: After these careful screening processes, we finally identify two z 13 galaxy candidates, HD1 and HD2, in the COSMOS and SXDS fields, respectively. Figure fig_SED shows images and SEDs of HD1 and HD2, and Table tab_photo summarizes their measured fluxes. HD1 and HD2 are spatially isolated from other nearby sources, ensuring the robustness of the photometry.

We additionally test with deeper SMUVS images 2018ApJS..237...39A . We carefully measure the magnitudes in the SMUVS images and still find that the best photometric redshift for HD1 is z>12 . Magnitudes in the other bands including the K_ s band in the COSMOS2020 catalog are consistent with our measurements, although their flux errors are much smaller than ours.
If this feature is the Oiii 88 m emission line, the redshift of HD1 is z=13.27 , in good agreement with the photometric redshift estimate.

sources.summary: The paper investigates HD1 as a z~13 LBG candidate: its UV luminosity implies SFR 78.9 solar masses per year if star-forming, or a 1.2e8 solar-mass black hole if quasar-powered.

quotes.quote: Recently, presented the detection of two H-band drop-out Lyman-Break Galaxy (LBG) candidates at z 13 (HD1 and HD2). Although also explicable at z 4 , the z 13 solutions to photometric fitting offer significantly lower ^2 statistics. While not confirmed by spectroscopy, one source is backed by a 4 signal of the [ OIII ] 88 m line at z = 13.27 with the Atacama Large Millimeter Array (ALMA), further supporting the z 13 estimate.

This results in SFR _ UV =78.9 yr^ 1 and SFR _ UV =125.2 yr^ 1 for HD1 and HD2, respectively, as also noted in Table table1 . On the other hand, our theoretical scalings produce SFR _ UV values of about 5.3 (16.5) yr^ 1 for case 1 (2) for the observed number density. This implies that these galaxies are extreme star formers , whose UV SFR (and hence star formation efficiencies) are a factor 15-24 (4.8-7.6) higher than expected in case 1 (2) for a given halo mass.

For the two sources, the inferred black hole masses are = 1.2 10^ 8 and = 1.7 10^ 8 , respectively, and their accretion rates are M _ BH = 2.7 yr^ 1 and M _ BH = 3.7 yr^ 1 (assuming a standard matter-to-energy conversion efficiency = 0.1 ).

sources.summary: The paper includes HD1 in joint ALMA-SED analysis, describing it as a remarkably bright z 13 candidate with no ALMA dust continuum and the most luminous z>11 object in the comparison.

quotes.quote: The non-detection of the dust continuum from \ is reminiscent of recent ALMA results in other three UV-bright galaxy candidates at z 11 -13: GHZ1/GLz11 , GHZ2/GLz13, and HD1 harikane2022b, bakx2022, popping2022, yoon2022, kaasinen2022, yoon2022 . GHZ1/GLz11 and GHZ2/GLz13 were also identified in the early \ data from the GLASS field treu2022 from different teams e.g., castellano2022, naidu2022, donnan2022, harikane2023 . No robust dust continuum is detected in follow-up deep 1-mm observations with a total of >10 hrs observing time for both candidates bakx2022, popping2022, yoon2022 , while a tentative ( 2.6 ) detection is reported in GHZ1/GLz11 yoon2022 . HD1 was found as a remarkably bright ( M_ UV 24 ) galaxy candidate at z 13 in a systematic search over a 2.3 deg ^ 2 area in ground-based telescopes and Spitzer data harikane2022b .

Similarly deep ALMA 1-mm and 2-mm band observations have been carried out, showing no dust continuum detection in both ALMA observations harikane2022b, kaasinen2022 . These results may imply a low possibility of contamination from lower- z dusty star-forming galaxies with strong emission lines among the high- z candidates at z 11--17 recently identified and observed with ALMA.
Owing to our and recent deep ALMA observations, the high- z solutions at z 11 -17 are all favored in the UV bright high- z candidates of S5-z17-1, GHZ1/GLz11, GHZ2/GLz13, and HD1 (Section sec:sed ).

sources.summary: The paper tests HD1 as a proposed z=13.27 galaxy, finding ALMA features consistent with noise and concluding JWST spectroscopy is needed.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: The observations were centered on RA 10 ^h 01 ^m 51.31 ^s , Dec +02 ^d 32 ^m 50.0 ^s

quotes.quote: The galaxy ``HD1'' was recently proposed to be a z 13.27 galaxy based on its potential Lyman break and tentative \ detection with ALMA. We hereby aim to test this scenario with new ALMA Band 4, DDT observations of what would be the \ emission, if HD1 is at z 13.27 . We carefully analyse the new ALMA Band 4 observations as well as re-analysing the existing ALMA Band 6 data on the source to determine the proposed redshift.

We conclude that we are more likely to be recovering noise features than both \ and \ emission from a source at z 13.27 . Although we find no evidence of a z 13.27 galaxy, we cannot entirely rule out this scenario. Determining where and exactly what type of galaxy HD1 is, will now likely require JWST/NIRSpec spectroscopy.

sources.summary: The paper treats HD1 as a tentative z=13.27 candidate targeted by ALMA, where neither OIII nor CII was clearly detected and only upper limits were obtained.

quotes.quote: For a third candidate (HD1) a tentative z=13.27 was reported Harikane22a . To confirm this detection, ALMA Band 6 (Band 4) observations were designed to target 88 m ( 158 m) emission Harikane22a, Kaasinen22 . No clear detection was found in either of the two BANDS. The 4 upper limit for the 88 m and 158 m emission lines are 5.88 10^8 and 0.7 10^8 L_ , respectively Kaasinen22 .

gmw_00001087.name: HD2
gmw_00001087.overview: HD2 is a Lyman-break galaxy candidate located in the SXDS field [arXiv:2112.09141, arXiv:2201.00823]. Photometric fitting suggests a high-redshift solution at z ~ 13 [arXiv:2112.09141, arXiv:2201.00823], which may represent either an extreme star-forming galaxy or a system hosting a supermassive black hole [arXiv:2201.00823].

sources.summary: The paper identifies HD2 as a z about 13 galaxy candidate in SXDS, reports isolated photometry, and finds high-redshift SED solutions with best-fit redshift z=12.3.

quotes.quote: After these careful screening processes, we finally identify two z 13 galaxy candidates, HD1 and HD2, in the COSMOS and SXDS fields, respectively. Figure fig_SED shows images and SEDs of HD1 and HD2, and Table tab_photo summarizes their measured fluxes. HD1 and HD2 are spatially isolated from other nearby sources, ensuring the robustness of the photometry.

For HD2, the best-fit redshift is z=12.3 ( ^2=4.2 ) and the 1 range ( ^2<1 ) is 12.0<z<12.7 . We find two types of the high redshift solutions. One is a very young starburst: an age less than 10 Myr, M_* 7 10^9 M_ , SFR 10^ 3-4 M_ yr ^ 1 , and A_ V 0.8 . _ SFH and metallicity are not constrained. The other case is a massive and relatively mature galaxy: an age greater than 100 Myr, M_* 1 10^ 11 M_ , SFR<10^ 2 M_ yr ^ 1 , A_ V <0.5 , and _ SFH <60 Myr.

gmw_00001088.name: F568-V1
gmw_00001088.overview: F568-V1 is a low-surface-brightness disk galaxy [arXiv:2201.02109]. Its baryonic mass is dominated by an extended gas disk rather than its smaller stellar disk [arXiv:2201.02109].

sources.summary: The paper models F568-V1 as a low-surface-brightness disk galaxy dominated by an extended gas disk and predicts how its MOND rotation curve responds to an external field.

quotes.quote: F568-V1 is an LSB galaxy whose baryonic mass is dominated by an extended gas disk rather than the smaller stellar disk. We assume a total baryonic mass of M_ bar =6.4 10^ 9 M _ with the corresponding MOND radius of r_ M =2.8 kpc. The gas disk is assumed to occupy 52 of the baryonic mass.

For the external field we take e_ N =4.2 10^ 3 (i.e. \ e=0.067 ) from . As expected from Figure efe_paral , the predicted inner RC for F568-V1, which has a larger R_ d /r_ M ratio than NGC 5055, deviates more clearly from the algebraic MOND curve. The maximum deviation in the inner part amounts to 5.9 of the maximum circular speed while it is 3.8 in NGC 5055. As in NGC 5055, the outer part is adequately described by the AQUAL fitting function and shows a difference from the QUMOND fitting functions.

gmw_00001089.name: WPVS 48
gmw_00001089.overview: WPVS 48 is a nearby Seyfert 1 galaxy located at a redshift of approximately 0.037 [arXiv:1401.2834, arXiv:1810.01242]. The galaxy exhibits strong optical and near-infrared variability, with a sharp dust echo that suggests a face-on torus geometry [arXiv:1401.2834, arXiv:1810.01242].

sources.summary: The paper monitors WPVS48 in optical and near-infrared bands, measures hot-dust reverberation lags, and argues that its sharp dust echo favors a face-on or foreshortened torus geometry.

quotes.quote: Using robotic telescopes of the Universit atssternwarte Bochum near Cerro Armazones in Chile, we monitored the z=0.0377 Seyfert-1 galaxy WPVS48 (2MASX J09594263-3112581) in the optical ( B and R ) and near-infrared (NIR, J and K_ s ) with a cadence of two days. The light curves show unprecedented variability details. The NIR variation features of WPVS48 are consistent with the corresponding optical variations, but the features appear sharper in the NIR than in the optical, suggesting that the optical photons undergo multiple scatterings. The J and K_ s emission, tracing the hot ( 1600 K) dust echo, lags the B and R variations by on average = 64 4 days and 71 5 days, respectively (restframe).
Therefore the sharp dust echo of WPVS48 argues in favor of a face-on torus geometry.

sources.summary: The paper explores the central geometry of the nearby Seyfert 1 galaxy WPVS 48, noting its optical and near-infrared variability, redshift, H-line width, and multi-band monitoring for reverberation mapping.

quotes.quote: Here, we explore the central geometry of the nearby Seyfert 1 galaxy WPVS 48 and discuss a bowl-shaped BLR/dust torus model configuration. Previous data has already been analysed in , where WPVS 48 was shown to exhibit strong optical and near-infrared variations. It is located at redshift z = 0.037 and the FWHM of H is 937 km/s. Since the central region of AGN cannot be spatially resolved on images, the reverberation mapping (RM) technique is applied. WPVS 48 has been monitored for 6 months using the VYSOS-6, BEST-II, BMT and IRIS telescopes located at the Bochum observatory near Cerro Armazones, the future location of the ESO Extreme Large Telescope (ELT) in Chile http://www.astro.ruhr-uni-bochum.de/astro/oca/ .

gmw_00001090.name: 4C 02.27
gmw_00001090.overview: 4C 02.27 is a double-double radio quasar located at a redshift of 0.649 [arXiv:1012.2992] that exhibits episodic radio activity [arXiv:1104.1086]. It features a compact core with a one-sided approaching jet [arXiv:1012.2992] and a hot spot in its western lobe [arXiv:1104.1086].

sources.summary: The paper describes 4C 02.27 as a double-double radio quasar with redshift 0.649, compact VLBI core emission, a one-sided approaching jet, and no detected innermost double structure.

quotes.quote: Recently identified 4C 02.27 (J0935+0204) as the first possible example of a double-double radio quasar (DDRQ). Its redshift is z =0.649 as measured in the Sloan Digital Sky Survey . The outer pair of lobes can easily be identified in the 1.4-GHz radio image (Fig. FIRST-image ) taken in the US National Radio Astronomy Observatory (NRAO) Very Large Array (VLA) Faint Images of the Radio Sky at Twenty-centimeters (FIRST) survey .

According to our e-EVN image, the core of 4C 02.27 is compact, slightly extended to NE. Its brightness distribution is well described by a central circular Gaussian component (60.8 mJy flux density, 1.6 mas diameter, full width at half maximum) and a weak extension (1.6 mJy, 20 mas) at the position angle of 47^ o , separated by 32 mas from the central one. The apparently one-sided jet-like structure indicates that the NE side is the approaching one, just as deduced from the appearance of the inner lobes .

With VLBI we observe the base of the approaching relativistic jet in 4C 02.27, a currently active radio quasar. At the resolution provided by the e-EVN at 1.6 GHz, we found no sign of an innermost double structure (unlike e.g. , in the case of the radio galaxy B0818+214) which would have indicated a third and most recent cycle of activity in this unique radio-loud quasar.

sources.summary: The paper uses J0935+0204 as the comparison double-double radio quasar, describing its normal blue quasar spectrum, stronger broad-line velocities, black hole estimate basis, and recurrent jet activity.

quotes.quote: Optical data The optical analysis is based on archival spectra obtained from the SDSS DR9 catalog for both the DDRQs J0746+4526 and J0935+0204. The Galactic reddening E(B-V) , adopted from the NASA Extragalactic Database (NED), towards J0746+4526 and J0935+0204 are respectively 0.056 and 0.042 mag. The Galactic extinction and redshift corrected optical spectrum of J0746+4526 is presented in Figure spec_com , along with the spectrum of J0935+0204. The spectrum of J0935+0204 is similar to the typical spectra of normal quasars, while the spectrum of J0746+4526 is typical of red quasars. For both quasars, the \ lines at 4960 \ and 5008 \ associated with the narrow-line-region (NLR) clouds, are quite prominent, while there is a striking difference in the strength of the hydrogen Balmer lines which are weaker in J0746+4526.

For J0746+4526, FWHM of 2800 \ is 35.22 , whereas for J0935+0204 it is about 61.33 \ 2012MNRAS.422.1546K . These demonstrate that the velocity dispersions of several broad-line-region (BLR) clouds are much higher for J0935 + 0204 in comparison to that for J0746+4526 and further indicate that the former galaxy hosts a higher mass central black hole than the latter one.

We note that the \ and \ emission features are prominent in the spectrum of J0935+0204, whereas for J0746+4526 the \ line is clearly seen (although weaker) and the \ emission line is barely seen (see Figure spec_com ). Therefore, to determine the black hole mass of J0746+4526, we have used the \ emission line, whereas for J0935+0204, 2012MNRAS.426..851K used both the \ and \ emission lines.

sources.summary: The paper presents 4C02.27 as a promising quasar example of episodic radio activity, with steep diffuse relic emission, a western hot spot, and a 470 kpc projected size.

quotes.quote: From a systematic comparison of FIRST and NVSS images of a large number of radio selected quasars, and imaging with the GMRT, one of the most promising examples of episodic activity in a quasar is 4C02.27 (Figure 2). Diffuse radio emission, possibly from an earlier cycle, has been imaged with the GMRT, and shown to have a steep spectral index of 1.2 between 610 and 1400 MHz, while that of the western lobe is 1.0 ,. There is a prominent hot-spot in the western lobe, constraining the time scale of episodic activity to be 1.8 3.9 Myr, depending on the orientation of the source . Considering the relatively small number of giant radio quasars , evidence of episodic activity in 4C02.27 with an overall projected linear size of 470 kpc is consistent with the unified scheme for radio galaxies and quasars .

gmw_00001091.name: WNB1734+6407
gmw_00001091.overview: WNB1734+6407 is a fossil radio galaxy located at the center of the Abell 2276 cluster [arXiv:1210.6864]. It features a slightly extended core coinciding with the central cD galaxy, alongside two relaxed radio lobes that lack jets and hot-spots [arXiv:1210.6864].

sources.summary: The paper describes WNB1734+6407 as the fossil radio galaxy in Abell 2276, with relaxed lobes, a possible X-ray depression, and AGN power sufficient to reheat the cluster core.

quotes.quote: Abell 2276 hosts the dying source WNB1734+6407 (Murgia et al. 2011). Characterized by two relaxed lobes lacking jets and hot-spots, the radio morphology of WNB1734+6407 can be considered the prototype of fossil radio galaxies. The VLA images also reveal a slightly extended core component in coincidence with the cD galaxy at the peak of the X-ray emission.

For this cluster there is a hint of an X-ray depression associated to the south lobe of WNB 1734+6407. We compared these numbers with the observed counts over the region of the south lobe of WNB 1734+6407. Over the region of the south lobe, we measured a fractional depression in the X-ray counts of 19 13 , which is compatible with the predicted value for the source aligned to the plane of the sky.

For WNB 1734+6407, the dying source at the center of A2276, the radiative age obtained from the analysis of the radio spectrum matches the buoyancy age estimated from the X-ray analysis if the source is in the minimum energy condition.

gmw_00001092.name: WNB 1829+6911
gmw_00001092.overview: WNB 1829+6911 is a restarting radio galaxy located within the cluster ZwCl 1829.3+6912 [arXiv:1210.6864]. It features fossil radio lobes from past nuclear activity alongside newly restarting jets and an active core [arXiv:astro-ph/0405091, arXiv:1210.6864].

sources.summary: The paper describes WNB1829+6911 as a dying radio galaxy with fossil lobes and restarting jets, resembling 3C338, with steep extended emission and a flatter bright core.

quotes.quote: Fanti parma@ira.cnr.it, deruiter@ira.cnr.it, mack@ira.cnr.it, rfanti@ira.cnr.it We report the recent discovery of three `dying' radio galaxies belonging to the WENSS minisurvey sample: WNB1734+6407, WNB1829+6911 and WNB1851+5707. These sources have been selected on the basis of their extremely steep broad-band radio spectra, which is a strong indication that these objects either belong to the rare class of dying radio galaxies or that we are observing `fossil' radio plasma remaining from a previous nuclear activity. Deep spectral index images obtained with the Very Large Array confirmed that in these sources the central engine has ceased to be active for a significant fraction of their lifetime although their extended lobes have not yet completely faded away. In one case, WNB1829+6911, fossil radio lobes are seen in conjunction with newly restarting jets.

The radio appearance of WNB1829+6911 is virtually identical to that of 3C338, a nearby radio source associated with the central dominant galaxy in the cooling flow cluster Abell 2199. In both sources we observe the presence of fossil plasma remaining from a previous activity in conjunction with a restarting core. The extended emission of WNB1829+6911 has a very steep spectrum, > 2.2 . The source exhibits a bright core with a much flatter spectrum, = 0.6 .

sources.summary: The paper presents WNB1829+6911 as a restarting radio galaxy whose fossil lobes have steep spectra while a flatter-spectrum core dominates the high-frequency emission.

quotes.quote: The radio appearance of WNB1829+6911 is virtually identical to that of 3C 338, a nearby radio source associated with the central dominant galaxy in the cooling flow cluster Abell 2199 (Jones Preston 2001). In both sources we observe the presence of fossil plasma remaining from a previous activity in conjunction with a restarting core. The extended emission of WNB1829+6911 has a very steep spectrum, > 1.6 and > 1.9 respectively for the W and E-lobe.

The source exhibits a bright core with a much flatter spectrum, = 0.6 . Most probably this region of the radio source is powered by a new couple of restarting jets. The core emission is responsible for the high-frequency flattening seen in the source integrated spectrum (Fig. 2 bottom panel). An arc-like feature with a very steep spectrum ( > 2.5 ) can be seen on the SW side of the core (Fig. 2 top-left panel).

sources.summary: The paper describes WNB 1829+6911 as a restarting radio source in ZwCl 1829.3+6912, with an active core, fossil lobes, and marginal X-ray cavity evidence.

quotes.quote: The cluster hosts the restarting radio source WNB 1829+6911 (Murgia et al. 2011). The radio source is characterized by an active core, associated to the cD galaxy, and a fossil structure extended about 100 kpc in the E-W direction, following the elongation of the surrounding large-scale X-ray emission.

There is marginal evidence for a cavity in correspondence to the west lobe of WNB 1829+6911. The depression seems roughly to follow the morphology of the radio lobe. However, for the west lobe of the dying source WNB 1829+6911 we measured a depression in the X-ray counts of 13 7 . The X-ray cavity appears to follow the shape of the radio lobe.
In ZwCl 1829.3+691 a cavity is detected in the west lobe of the dying source WNB 1829+6911 at a distance of about 40 kpc from the core.

gmw_00001093.name: Segue 1
gmw_00001093.overview: Segue 1 is an ultra-faint dwarf spheroidal galaxy [arXiv:1212.1013, arXiv:1607.06390] and a tightly bound satellite of the Milky Way [arXiv:1711.09097]. It is highly dominated by dark matter [arXiv:1212.1013, arXiv:1704.01668] and is characterized by an old stellar population [arXiv:1704.01668, arXiv:2203.05750] with a very short star-formation history [arXiv:1509.00856, arXiv:1901.05465].

sources.summary: The paper treats Segue 1 as a disputed dwarf-galaxy candidate, but argues its velocity dispersion may be inflated by Sagittarius contamination and favors a dissolving star-cluster origin.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: It is located at equatorial coordinates _ 2000 152^ , _ 2000 16^ and has a heliocentric distance of 23 2 kpc.

quotes.quote: Using Keck/DEIMOS spectroscopy, they measured the radial velocities of 24 stars in Segue 1 with a mean heliocentric velocity of 206 kms ^ 1 and a velocity dispersion of 4.2 1.2 kms ^ 1 , leading to claims that Segue 1 is a dwarf galaxy rather than a globular cluster. Assuming these stars are gravitationally bound to Segue 1 and are in dynamical equilibrium, then the implied mass-to-light ratio is 1200   Ge09 , which would make Segue 1 the most dark matter dominated galaxy detected to date. However, both assumptions are questionable. If Segue 1 is a globular cluster that is undergoing tidal disruption, then extra-tidal stars may not be so easy to distinguish from gravitationally bound members and the hypothesis of dynamical equilibrium may be a poor one. More seriously, if Segue 1 is immersed in the Sagittarius stream, then contamination of any sample of Segue 1 stars by stream stars may be hard to avoid.

Accordingly, we favour the scenario in which Segue 1 is a star cluster stripped early on from the Sagittarius galaxy. This provides a natural explanation for the fact that all four of the phase space coordinates that are measured coincide with those of the Sagittarius stream. What is needed to make the argument complete is a demonstration that the metallicity of Segue 1 is compatible with an origin in the Sagittarius galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper treats Segue 1 as a dark-matter-dominated dwarf spheroidal target, re-estimating VERITAS annihilation limits with a full likelihood approach and forecasting MAGIC and CTA sensitivity to spectral-line signals from it.

quotes.quote: One of the most recent results from indirect DM searches with IACTs comes from the VERITAS Collaboration http://veritas.sao.arizona.edu , from observations of the dwarf spheroidal galaxy Segue 1 . Although this source is considered to be one of the most DM dominated objects known so far , nearly 50 hours of data showed no significant gamma-ray excess. Consequently, upper limits to the v were derived, for the full energy range and relaying on the conventional likelihood analysis. We have computed how these results could be further strengthened if the full likelihood was used instead.

We require a 5 detection in 50 hours of observations of Segue 1, with Einasto DM profile ( J = 1.7 10^ 19 GeV ^2 cm ^ 5 ). For the size of the background region we take = 12. Results are shown on figure Fig9 . MAGIC and CTA exhibit greatest sensitivity for m_ around 200 and 500 GeV, respectively, with CTA being a factor between 5 and 10 better than MAGIC.

sources.summary: The paper describes Segue 1 as an ultra-faint, extremely dark-matter-dominated dwarf spheroidal used in MAGIC, VERITAS, and IceCube searches for annihilation signals and cross-section limits.

quotes.quote: Segue 1 is a ultra-faint object discovered in 2007 by the SDSS and located 23 kpc from the Sun. It is the most dark-matter dominated object known so far, with M/L 1320-3400 M_ /L_ . Segue 1 has been observed with the MAGIC \ telescope for 29.4 hours Aleksic 331 . No significant gamma-ray signal was found above the background. The data have been used to probe dark-matter models that try to explain the PAMELA data, see magic-dm .

Segue 1 has also been observed by VERITAS for almost 48 hours Vivier 919 . For the W^+ W^- annihilation channel, a 95 .l. \ upper limit on the velocity-weighted annihilation cross section v has been obtained at the level of 8 10^ 24 cm ^3 s ^ 1 around 1 TeV. 13 dwarf spheroidal galaxies (also among them Segue 1) have been observed by IceCube-59 to search for dark matter L unemann 1024 .

sources.summary: The paper assesses Segue 1 as a possible Orphan stream parent, noting matching distance, velocity, velocity dispersion, and metallicity overlap, while abundance differences leave the association doubtful.

quotes.quote: Segue 1, an ultrafaint dwarf galaxy simon;et-al_2011,norris;et-al_2010 , resides at a similar distance ( 23 kpc) along the great circle path of the Orphan stream. Segue 1 also shares velocities that are consistent with the Orphan stream at its nearest point. Moreover, both Segue 1 and the Orphan stream exhibit low velocity dispersions: on the order of 4 km s ^ 1 .

Segue 1 hosts an extremely wide range in stellar metallicities: the mean is found to be [ Fe/H ] = -2.5 and the spread extends from [ Fe/H ] = 3.4 to 1.6 dex simon;et-al_2011 . There is significant overlap between the metallicity distributions reported for Segue 1 and the Orphan stream. The high-resolution spectra in this study confirm the wide metallicity range of Orphan stream members found by casey;et-al_2013a , and independently reported by sesar;et-al_2013 ( [ Fe/H ] = -1.5 to 2.7 ). Thus, in addition to on-sky position, distances, velocity, and velocity dispersion, Segue 1 and the Orphan stream share a wide and consistent range in metallicities. In contrast to these observables, vargas;et-al_2013 and Frebel et al. (submitted) find Segue 1 members to be extremely enhanced ( [ /Fe ] > 0.4 ), unlike what we observe in disrupted Orphan stream members.

sources.summary: The paper uses 158 hours of MAGIC observations of Segue 1, combined with LAT analysis, to constrain dark-matter annihilation and show how J-factor uncertainty affects gamma-ray limits.

quotes.quote: For this work we use MAGIC data corresponding to 158 hours of observations of the satellite galaxy Segue 1 , the deepest observations of any dSph by a Cherenkov telescope to date. Older MAGIC single-telescope DM searches (Draco , 7.8 hours; Willman 1 , 15.5 hours; and Segue 1 , 29.4 hours) yield a comparatively poor sensitivity and are therefore not included in this work. The data were taken between 2011 and 2013, with observations before, during, and after a major hardware upgrade .

The figure shows 2 ( ) vs. \ for =1 TeV DM particles annihilating into pairs in Segue 1. MAGIC and LAT individual curves are shown, as well as their combination. The effect of the statistical uncertainty on the J-factor is also illustrated by showing curves where J is treated as either a nuisance or fixed parameter. In each case, the 95 confidence level upper limits are obtained from the crossing point between the corresponding curve and the 2 ( ) = 2.71 line. In the case of treating J as a nuisance parameter, MAGIC and LAT individual limits are =1.2 10^ 24 \ and 6.8 10^ 25 , respectively, whereas the combined analysis yields =4.6 10^ 25 .

sources.summary: The MAGIC review presents Segue 1 as an ultra-faint dwarf-spheroidal target with debated J-factor estimates, later receiving 160 hours and yielding strong dark-matter limits.

quotes.quote: Two ultra-faint targets were observed: Willman 1 and Segue 1. The latter is an ultra-faint DSG, largely debated in the literature. It currently comprises about 70 member stars. The result of the J-factor estimation based on Jeans analysis goes from ranking Segue 1 as the best candidate to an unreliable candidate . MAGIC is recently computing the J-factors using the public CLUMPY code and the conclusions is that Segue 1 is still one of the candidate with the largest J-factor.

MAGIC devoted 160 h to the best DSG candidate (at that time): Segue 1. The Segue 1 stereo paper provided the absolute strongest upper limits from DSG for DM particles above few hundreds GeV (at lower DM masses, the results from Fermi-LAT are the most constraining). These MAGIC results made into the PDG's Review of Particle Physics .

sources.summary: The paper derives J-factor constraints for Segue I, notes its large uncertainty despite a high average factor, and describes background behavior affecting its dark-matter limits.

quotes.quote: We solve the above equations to obtain factors for eight classical Milky Way dSphs and Segue I by fitting their l.o.s. velocity dispersion, , tracer surface density, (R) , and VSPs as in . For the ultra-faint dSph Segue I, we use the data described in , with membership probability 0.9 or 0.95, as in . For the fits, we use both the FreeForm mass model described in , and the coreNFWtides model described in , with priors as in .

Finally, in Fig. fig:J-factors_segue we show our results for the factor determination for Segue I. Although the average factor determination is higher than the classical dSphs considered here, its uncertainty is one order of magnitude larger. We can thus expect that its constraining power will not be the best, once profiling over its uncertainties. Note, however, that the systematic uncertainties due to the choice of membership probability cut (right panel in Fig. fig:J-factors_segue ) or of the choice of mass model ( coreNFWtides vs. FreeForm ; grey vs. orange histograms in the left panel of Fig. fig:J-factors_segue ) are below one sigma. This leaves significant room for improvement in reducing statistical uncertainties through appropriate observational campaigns targeting ultra-faint galaxies like Segue I.

sources.summary: The paper treats Segue 1 as a dark-matter-dominated dwarf spheroidal target for MAGIC gamma-ray searches, using its distance, J factor, and observational campaign in the branon analysis.

quotes.quote: The ultra-faint dSph Segue 1, of absolute magnitude M_ V = -1.5_ 0.8 ^ +0.6 , was discovered in the Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS) imaging data in 2006 2007ApJ...654..897B and is with an estimated 3400 M_ /L_ mass-to-light ratio one of the most DM-dominated object known so far 2011ApJ...733...46S . Besides, Segue 1 is positioned in the Northern Hemisphere and outside of the Galactic plane ( RA = 10.12 h , DEC = 16.08^ ) only 23 2 kpc away from us, which leads to an excellent target for indirect DM searches in the VHE gamma-ray bands with the MAGIC telescopes 2011JCAP...06..035A,2012PhRvD..85f2001A,2014JCAP...02..008A,2016JCAP...02..039M . The MAGIC telescopes consist of a system of two 17 m diameter telescopes operating in stereoscopic mode at the Roque de los Muchachos Observatory ( 28.8^ N, 17.9^ W; 2200 m a.s.l.) on the Canary Island of La Palma, Spain.

In our analysis, we use the stereoscopic observation of the dSph galaxy Segue 1 with MAGIC The observation performed by the MAGIC-I telescope in single telescope mode 2011JCAP...06..035A are not included in our analysis. , which were already described and analyzed in 2014JCAP...02..008A,2016JCAP...02..039M . This observational campaign was carried out between 2011 and 2013 and is with 157.9 h the deepest observation of any dSph by a Cherenkov telescope to date. Likelihood analysis The data reduction of the Segue 1 observation have been kindly provided by the MAGIC Collaboration.

In our analysis, we are using the J factor and its statistical uncertainty for Segue 1 from 2015ApJ...801...74G , where the DM density distribution is modeled assuming a Navarro-Frenk-White (NFW) DM density profile 1996ApJ...462..563N . Thus, we consider the value of log _ 10 J GeV^ 2 cm^ 5 = 19.02_ 0.35 ^ +0.32 integrated up to the angular distance = 0.125^ of the Segue 1 DM halo according to the signal region.

sources.summary: Segue 1 is a nearby compact dwarf with high dark-matter content; the paper compares spectroscopic members and finds a transition boundary with only a few bright outer candidates.

quotes.quote: Segue 1 At a heliocentric distance of 23 kpc ( belokurov2007_cats ), Seg1 is one of the nearest dwarf galaxies. Its size is relatively compact ( r_ h = 3.95 = 0.07 ^ , or 30 pc; munoz2018 ) and similar in luminosity to other MW UFDs ( M_ V 1.5 mag; L_ V 340 L _ ; martin2008 ). In the discovery paper of belokurov2007_cats , this system was proposed as a globular cluster containing very few stars. However, spectroscopic follow-up conducted by geha2009 and martinez2011 suggested that Seg1's internal kinematics are consistent with a dwarf galaxy. The former determined that Seg1 has an exceptionally large dark matter content (M/L 1340 2440 M _ /L _ ) while the latter claimed this system may be one of the highest DM density systems in the Local Group.

Thus far, the most radially distant spectroscopic member of this system is 5.25 r_ h . For comparison, our most distant member (with no present spectroscopic follow-up) is located at 6.8 r_ h . We determine the radius of the transition boundary is r_ trans, ell = 0.11 ^ ( 1.57 r_ h ) and r_ trans, circ = 0.14 ^ ( 2 r_ h ) in the elliptical and Circular cases, respectively.

sources.summary: The paper presents Segue 1 as a promising dwarf-galaxy target for Fermi, estimating its distance, half-light radius, velocity dispersion, line-of-sight integral, and detectable gamma-ray signal.

quotes.quote: In Section sec:Fermipredictions , we discuss the prospects for the Fermi gamma-ray space telescope to detect a dark-matter-induced gamma-ray signal from the dwarf galaxy Segue 1. Among the known dwarf galaxies, Segue 1 could potentially provide one of the largest signals from dark matter annihilations .

Here, we will present the detection prospects for one recently discovered particularly promising dwarf galaxy, Segue 1 . This satellite is at a distance of 23 kpc from the Sun at a galactic latitude of 50.4^ and has an observed half-light radius of 30 pc . It has a measured 1-dimensional stellar velocity dispersion of 4.3 km/s . Segue 1 has only 24 stars with measured line-of-sight velocities , so that its L value will be less constrained than any of the previous four objects we have considered.
An exciting prospect is that Fermi could detect a gamma-ray signal from Segue 1 if dark matter annihilations are responsible for the PAMELA signal and/or the ATIC/PPB-BETS signal.

sources.summary: Segue 1 is emphasized as a dark-matter-rich dwarf; IceCube projections could outperform gamma-ray constraints for some annihilation channels, and ACT observations are expected.

quotes.quote: Dwarfs are largely devoid of astrophysical activity, and have very large mass to light ratios, Segue 1 being a particularly extreme example . The systems therefore lack astrophysical sources which could potentially mimic a signal of dark matter annihilation.

Finally, we examine the closest of the three dwarfs we have considered, Segue 1. In Fig. fig:segue , we present IceCube's projected sensitivity to dark matter annihilations in Segue 1, along with the current upper limits on the annihilation cross section from FGST measurements . For annihilation to muons (taus), IceCube may eventually set stronger exclusion limits for m_X > 450 (750) GeV. If there is a 50 branching fraction to neutrinos, IceCube will do better for all WIMP masses shown. As Segue 1 is newly discovered, we expect that ACTs will observe this galaxy in the near future.

sources.summary: The paper discusses Segue 1 as an ultra-faint satellite dwarf with measured member-star iron and carbon abundances, low enrichment, extended members, and internal metallicity dispersion.

quotes.quote: The `ultra-faint' satellite dwarf galaxies are clearly very interesting for study, with intriguing results even from very small samples. Our results for radial-velocity members of Bo o tes I (discovered by Belokurov et al. 2006; luminosity L _V 3 10^4 L _ , distance 60  kpc; Martin, de Jong Rix 2008) and of Segue 1 (discovered by Belokurov et al. 2007; L _V 335 L _ , distance 25  kpc; Martin et al. 2008) are shown in Fig. 2 (see also Norris et al. 2008; Norris et al. 2010b). Here I show derived iron and carbon abundances, based on intermediate-resolution spectra (from the 2-degree field of view, multi-object, fibre-fed spectrograph AAOmega on the Anglo-Australian Telescope) covering the blue Ca II K-line (from which the iron abundance is derived) and the CH G-band (synthesis of which is the basis for the carbon abundance). Thus the iron abundances are on the same scale as many of the surveys of metal-poor field halo stars (see Beers Christlieb 2005).

The vertical arrows along the x axis mark the half-light radii of these two systems (`S' for Segue 1 and `B' for Boo I). The wide field-of-view of AAOmega allows efficient mapping of these very sparse, nearby systems (and is critical given the apparent contamination in these lines-of-sight by stars from the Sagittarius dSph; see Niederste-Ostholt et al. 2009). Three points are immediately obvious: the mean level of enrichment is low, with a fraction of `extremely metal-poor' stars (those with [ Fe/H ] < -3.0  dex; see Table 1 of Beers Christlieb 2005); there are members well beyond the nominal half-light radius; and there is a large internal metallicity dispersion in each system, suggestive of self-enrichment.

sources.summary: The paper treats Segue 1 as a faint system whose nature may be dwarf galaxy or star cluster, measuring candidate-member carbon and iron spreads including an extremely metal-poor carbon-rich member.

quotes.quote: CHEMICAL ENRICHMENT IN THE FAINTEST GALAXIES: THE CARBON AND IRON ABUNDANCE SPREADS IN THE BO O TES I DWARF SPHEROIDAL GALAXY AND THE SEGUE 1 SYSTEM JOHN E.

We present an AAOmega spectroscopic study of red giant stars in , which is an dwarf galaxy, and , suggested to be either an extremely low-luminosity dwarf galaxy or a star cluster. Our focus is quantifying the mean abundance and abundance dispersion in iron and carbon, and searching for distant radial-velocity members, in these systems. The primary conclusion of our investigation is that the spread of carbon abundance in both and is large.

For , four of our 16 velocity members have [ C/H ] -3.1, while two have [ C/H ] -2.3, suggesting a range of [ C/H ] 0.8. For there exists a range [ C/H ] 1.0, including our discovery of a star with [ Fe/H ] = --3.5 and [ C/Fe ] = +2.3, which is a radial velocity member at a distance of 4 half-light radii from the system center. The accompanying ranges in iron abundance are [ Fe/H ] 1.6 for both and .
